F5H Understanding and remastering the Sleuth DOS game

Model: claude-fable-5Started: 2026-06-12 05:4464 messages
You

I saw this Reddit post and was both amazed and inspired at the idea. I've always enjoyed this old DOS game called Sleuth, and have always wanted to both understand how it worked, as well as potentially expand it to different room layouts or messages and so on.

Back in the day, the most me and my friends figured out was just text editing the names in DESCR.V1 and DESCR.V2 to our own customised names to make the default more convenient, without having to re-type in our custom names every playthrough.

Do you think you can have a look at it (Sleuth.zip attached), break it down and explain to me how it works and so on...

...and maybe in a later prompt, we can explore a potential plan to recreate/remaster the game and maybe improve it further with space for custom layouts or customisable actions/weapons, etc. Would this be better done via Javascript or Python or some other programming language? The goal would be for me to also understand what's going on and learn to read/debug the code, not just 'magically' create the game without me grokking it.

📎 1781242409373_image.png📎 sleuth.zip
2026-06-12 05:44
Claude
Thinking · Assessed retro game file and charted reverse-engineering strategy.
The user has uploaded a DOS game from the early 1980s called Sleuth and wants me to reverse-engineer and analyze it. I should start by checking if I can read the file, then begin examining its contents.

This is exactly my kind of project. Let me start by reading the relevant skill, then unpack and explore what we're working with.

viewRequired first step: check file-reading skill before working with uploaded archive
📄 /mnt/skills/public/file-reading/SKILL.md
Output
     1	---
     2	name: file-reading
     3	description: "Use this skill when a file has been uploaded but its content is NOT in your context — only its path at /mnt/user-data/uploads/ is listed in an uploaded_files block. This skill is a router: it tells you which tool to use for each file type (pdf, docx, xlsx, csv, json, images, archives, ebooks) so you read the right amount the right way instead of blindly running cat on a binary. Triggers: any mention of /mnt/user-data/uploads/, an uploaded_files section, a file_path tag, or a user asking about an uploaded file you have not yet read. Do NOT use this skill if the file content is already visible in your context inside a documents block — you already have it."
     4	compatibility: "claude.ai, Claude Desktop, Cowork — any surface where uploads land at /mnt/user-data/uploads/"
     5	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     6	---
     7	
     8	# Reading Uploaded Files
     9	
    10	## Why this skill exists
    11	
    12	When a user uploads a file in claude.ai, Claude Desktop, or Cowork,
    13	the file is written to `/mnt/user-data/uploads/<filename>` and you are told the path
    14	in an `<uploaded_files>` block. **The content is not in your context.**
    15	You must go read it.
    16	
    17	The naive thing — `cat /mnt/user-data/uploads/whatever` — is wrong for
    18	most files:
    19	
    20	- On a PDF it prints binary garbage.
    21	- On a 100MB CSV it floods your context with rows you will never use.
    22	- On a DOCX it prints the raw ZIP bytes.
    23	- On an image it does nothing useful at all.
    24	
    25	This skill tells you the right first move for each type, and when to
    26	hand off to a deeper skill.
    27	
    28	## General protocol
    29	
    30	1. **Look at the extension.** That is your dispatch key.
    31	2. **Stat before you read.** Large files need sampling, not slurping.
    32	   ```bash
    33	   stat -c '%s bytes, %y' /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    34	   file /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    35	   ```
    36	3. **Read just enough to answer the user's question.** If they asked
    37	   "how many rows are in this CSV", don't load the whole thing into
    38	   pandas — `wc -l` gives a fast approximation (it counts newlines,
    39	   not CSV records, so it may over-count if quoted fields contain
    40	   embedded newlines).
    41	4. **If a dedicated skill exists, go read it.** The table below tells
    42	   you when. The dedicated skills cover editing, creating, and advanced
    43	   operations that this skill does not.
    44	
    45	## `extract-text`
    46	
    47	For docx, odt, epub, xlsx, pptx, rtf, and ipynb the first move is
    48	`extract-text <file>`. It emits markdown for docx/odt/epub (headings,
    49	bold, lists, links, tables), tab-separated rows under `## Sheet:`
    50	headers for xlsx, text under `## Slide N` headers for pptx, fenced
    51	code cells for ipynb, and plain text for rtf. Pass `--format <fmt>`
    52	when the extension is wrong or absent (e.g., `--format xlsx` on an
    53	`.xlsm`). If it errors on a file, `pandoc <file> -t plain` is a
    54	fallback; for xlsx/pptx, fall back to the dedicated skill's
    55	Python-based approach (openpyxl / python-pptx).
    56	
    57	## Dispatch table
    58	
    59	| Extension                         | First move                                           | Dedicated skill                           |
    60	| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
    61	| `.pdf`                            | Content inventory (see PDF section)                  | `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md` |
    62	| `.docx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    63	| `.doc` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.docx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    64	| `.xlsx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    65	| `.xlsm`                           | `extract-text --format xlsx`                         | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    66	| `.xls` (legacy)                   | `pd.read_excel(engine="xlrd")` — openpyxl rejects it | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    67	| `.ods`                            | `pd.read_excel(engine="odf")` — openpyxl rejects it  | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    68	| `.pptx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    69	| `.ppt` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.pptx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    70	| `.csv`, `.tsv`                    | `pandas` with `nrows`                                | — (below)                                 |
    71	| `.json`, `.jsonl`                 | `jq` for structure                                   | — (below)                                 |
    72	| `.jpg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.webp`   | Already in your context as vision input              | — (below)                                 |
    73	| `.zip`, `.tar`, `.tar.gz`         | List contents, do **not** auto-extract               | — (below)                                 |
    74	| `.gz` (single file)               | `zcat \| head` — no manifest to list                 | — (below)                                 |
    75	| `.epub`, `.odt`                   | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |
    76	| `.rtf`                            | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |
    77	| `.ipynb`                          | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |
    78	| `.txt`, `.md`, `.log`, code files | `wc -c` then `head` or full `cat`                    | — (below)                                 |
    79	| Unknown                           | `file` then decide                                   | —                                         |
    80	
    81	---
    82	
    83	## PDF
    84	
    85	**Never** `cat` a PDF — it prints binary garbage.
    86	
    87	Quick first move — get the page count and determine whether the PDF
    88	has an extractable text layer:
    89	
    90	```bash
    91	pdfinfo /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    92	pdffonts /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    93	```
    94	
    95	`pdffonts` tells you whether text extraction will work before you try it:
    96	
    97	- **No fonts listed** (empty table, just the header) → the PDF is a
    98	  scan or raster export. `pdftotext` and `PdfReader.extract_text()`
    99	  will return nothing useful. Go straight to page rasterization or OCR
   100	  — see `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md` → "Scanned
   101	  documents".
   102	- **Fonts listed** → there is a text layer; extract it:
   103	  ```bash
   104	  pdftotext -f 1 -l 1 /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf - | head -20
   105	  ```
   106	
   107	The reason to check `pdffonts` first is user-facing: running
   108	`pdftotext` on a scan produces an empty result, and in a visible
   109	transcript that reads as a failed first attempt before you fall back
   110	to OCR. The two-line diagnostic above costs one tool call and avoids
   111	that — you arrive at the right method on the first try, which is what
   112	a user perceives as "it just read my file."
   113	
   114	That also shapes how to open your reply. The diagnostic commands are
   115	plumbing, not content; lead with what the user asked about. On a
   116	scanned receipt that might be "This is a 3-page scanned invoice; the
   117	amount due on page 2 is $1,845.00," and on a digitally-authored report
   118	it might be "The Q3 report runs 28 pages; revenue on p. 4 is $12.3M,
   119	up 9% YoY." What you're steering away from is the "I'll examine the
   120	PDF" / "Let me check if this is extractable" preamble — the answer to
   121	their question is the first thing they should see.
   122	
   123	For anything beyond a quick peek — figures, tables, attachments,
   124	forms, scanned PDFs, visual inspection, or choosing a reading strategy
   125	— go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md`. It covers
   126	content inventory, text extraction vs. page rasterization, embedded
   127	content extraction, and document-type-aware reading strategies.
   128	
   129	For PDF form filling, creation, merging, splitting, or watermarking,
   130	go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.
   131	
   132	---
   133	
   134	## DOCX / DOC
   135	
   136	The `docx` skill covers editing, creating, tracked changes, images.
   137	Read it if you need any of those. For a quick look:
   138	
   139	```bash
   140	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/memo.docx | head -200
   141	```
   142	
   143	Legacy `.doc` (not `.docx`) must be converted first — see the `docx`
   144	skill.
   145	
   146	---
   147	
   148	## XLSX / XLS / spreadsheets
   149	
   150	The `xlsx` skill covers formulas, formatting, charts, creating. Read
   151	it if you need any of those. For a quick look at an `.xlsx`:
   152	
   153	```bash
   154	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx | head -100
   155	```
   156	
   157	For `.xlsm`, add `--format xlsx` (same zip structure; only the
   158	extension differs). When you need a structured preview in Python:
   159	
   160	```python
   161	from openpyxl import load_workbook
   162	wb = load_workbook("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx", read_only=True)
   163	print("Sheets:", wb.sheetnames)
   164	ws = wb.active
   165	for row in ws.iter_rows(max_row=5, values_only=True):
   166	    print(row)
   167	```
   168	
   169	`read_only=True` matters — without it, openpyxl loads the entire
   170	workbook into memory, which breaks on large files. Do not trust
   171	`ws.max_row` in read-only mode: many non-Excel writers omit the
   172	dimension record, so it comes back `None` or wrong. If you need a row
   173	count, iterate or use pandas.
   174	
   175	**Legacy `.xls`** — openpyxl raises `InvalidFileException`. Use:
   176	
   177	```python
   178	import pandas as pd
   179	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/old.xls", engine="xlrd", nrows=5)
   180	```
   181	
   182	**`.ods` (OpenDocument)** — openpyxl also rejects this. Use:
   183	
   184	```python
   185	import pandas as pd
   186	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.ods", engine="odf", nrows=5)
   187	```
   188	
   189	---
   190	
   191	## PPTX
   192	
   193	```bash
   194	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/deck.pptx | head -200
   195	```
   196	
   197	**Legacy `.ppt`** — convert to `.pptx` first via LibreOffice; see
   198	`/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md` for the sandbox-safe
   199	`scripts/office/soffice.py` wrapper (bare `soffice` hangs here because
   200	the seccomp filter blocks the `AF_UNIX` sockets LibreOffice uses for
   201	instance management).
   202	
   203	For anything beyond reading, go to `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`.
   204	
   205	---
   206	
   207	## CSV / TSV
   208	
   209	**Do not** `cat` or `head` these blindly. A CSV with a 50KB quoted cell
   210	in row 1 will wreck your `head -5`. Use pandas with `nrows`:
   211	
   212	```python
   213	import pandas as pd
   214	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv", nrows=5)
   215	print(df)
   216	print()
   217	print(df.dtypes)
   218	```
   219	
   220	Approximate row count without loading (over-counts if the file has
   221	RFC-4180 quoted newlines — the same quoted-cell case this section
   222	warned about above):
   223	
   224	```bash
   225	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv
   226	```
   227	
   228	Full analysis only after you know the shape:
   229	
   230	```python
   231	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv")
   232	print(df.describe())
   233	```
   234	
   235	TSV: same, with `sep="\t"`.
   236	
   237	---
   238	
   239	## JSON / JSONL
   240	
   241	Structure first, content second:
   242	
   243	```bash
   244	jq 'type' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   245	jq 'if type == "array" then length elif type == "object" then keys else . end' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   246	```
   247	
   248	(`keys` errors on scalar JSON roots — a bare `"hello"` or `42` is valid
   249	JSON per RFC 7159 — so guard the branch.)
   250	
   251	Then drill into what the user actually asked about.
   252	
   253	JSONL (one object per line) — do **not** `jq` the whole file; work line
   254	by line:
   255	
   256	```bash
   257	head -3 /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl | jq .
   258	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl
   259	```
   260	
   261	---
   262	
   263	## Images (JPG / PNG / GIF / WEBP)
   264	
   265	**You can already see uploaded images.** They are injected into your
   266	context as vision inputs alongside the `<uploaded_files>` pointer. You
   267	do not need to read them from disk to describe them.
   268	
   269	The disk copy is only needed if you are going to **process** the image
   270	programmatically:
   271	
   272	```python
   273	from PIL import Image
   274	img = Image.open("/mnt/user-data/uploads/photo.jpg")
   275	print(img.size, img.mode, img.format)
   276	```
   277	
   278	For OCR on an image (text extraction, not description):
   279	
   280	```python
   281	import pytesseract
   282	print(pytesseract.image_to_string(img))
   283	```
   284	
   285	Note: the client resizes images larger than 2000×2000 down to that
   286	bound and re-encodes as JPEG before upload, so the disk copy may not
   287	be the user's original bytes. For most processing this doesn't matter;
   288	if the user is asking about original-resolution pixel data, flag it.
   289	
   290	---
   291	
   292	## Archives (ZIP / TAR / TAR.GZ)
   293	
   294	**List first. Extract never — unless the user explicitly asks.**
   295	Archives can be huge, contain path traversal, or nest forever.
   296	
   297	```bash
   298	unzip -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip
   299	tar -tf /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.tar
   300	```
   301	
   302	GNU tar auto-detects compression — `tar -tf` works on `.tar`,
   303	`.tar.gz`, `.tar.bz2`, `.tar.xz` alike. Don't hard-code `-z`.
   304	
   305	If the user wants one file from inside, extract just that one:
   306	
   307	```bash
   308	unzip -p /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip path/inside/file.txt
   309	```
   310	
   311	**Standalone `.gz`** (not a tar) compresses a single file — there is
   312	no manifest to list. Just peek at the decompressed content:
   313	
   314	```bash
   315	zcat /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json.gz | head -50
   316	```
   317	
   318	---
   319	
   320	## EPUB / ODT
   321	
   322	```bash
   323	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/book.epub | head -200
   324	```
   325	
   326	For long ebooks, pipe through `head` — you rarely need the whole thing
   327	to answer a question.
   328	
   329	---
   330	
   331	## RTF / IPYNB
   332	
   333	```bash
   334	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notes.rtf | head -200
   335	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notebook.ipynb | head -200
   336	```
   337	
   338	---
   339	
   340	## Plain text / code / logs
   341	
   342	Check the size first:
   343	
   344	```bash
   345	wc -c /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   346	```
   347	
   348	- **Under ~20KB**: `cat` is fine.
   349	- **Over ~20KB**: `head -100` and `tail -100` to orient. If the user
   350	  asked about something specific, `grep` for it. Load the whole thing
   351	  only if you genuinely need all of it.
   352	
   353	For log files, the user almost always cares about the end:
   354	
   355	```bash
   356	tail -200 /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   357	```
   358	
   359	---
   360	
   361	## Unknown extension
   362	
   363	```bash
   364	file /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin
   365	xxd /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin | head -5
   366	```
   367	
   368	`file` identifies most things. `xxd` head shows magic bytes. If `file`
   369	says "data" and the hex doesn't match anything you recognize, ask the
   370	user what it is instead of guessing.
   371

Thinking · Identified classic game mechanics and charted exploration strategy.
I'm looking at Sleuth, a classic murder mystery game from the early 80s by Eric N. Miller, and planning to extract its data files to explore how it works.
Thinking · Deciphering data file structures and their purposes.
Sleuth is a good candidate for archaeology since it's a small DOS executable with external data files, which explains why the name-editing trick worked. I'll extract it and examine the structure.

bash_toolRead the readme and documentation for context
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && cat README SLEUTH.DIZ && echo "=====DOC=====" && head -60 SLEUTH.DOC
Output
=== SLEUTH ===     To run SLEUTH ...
 
-- Type 'SLEUTH' <Enter> at the DOS system prompt.
 
To read the instructions for SLEUTH, select 'Review Instructions' from the
main menu of the SLEUTH program.
 
If you are encounter any problems after running the SLEUTH program, 
try starting the program by typing SLEUTH2 instead of SLEUTH.

You can now turn off the sounds that Sleuth makes.  Simply press `S' while
the Main Menu is being displayed.

SLEUTH - A MURDER MYSTERY 4.45: 
Sleuth is an interactive murder mystery
where you can specify the players and
victims! A murder has just been 
committed. You must explore the house
to find the murder room, weapon, 
and murderer! Fast-paced and logical.
For DOS using mono or color display.

=====DOC=====

       I N S T R U C T I O N S   F O R   S L E U T H

As you begin a game of SLEUTH a murder has just been committed.
Your job is  to mingle  with the houseguests and  to search the
contents of the house until you feel you have solved the crime.
Every game of SLEUTH is different so you must fully explore the
house each time that you play.

You can  move through  the house by using the four  cursor keys
(udrl)  located on the numeric keypad.  Be particularly careful
when moving  on the stairways  since you must change  direction
each time you move from one floor to another.

You can  search  rooms, people  and  objects  by  using  simple
phrases such as EXAMINE DOOR, SEARCH VICTORIA, or  LOOK AT JAR.
When  you want to  SEARCH or ACCUSE  a person you  only need to
type in  their  first name.  To get more  information from  the
guests you may  QUESTION  them, or ask  for their  ALIBIs.  The
characters  tend to be rather moody and will  not always answer
your  questions  the first time  around.  They are also  rather
restless and will be  moving around the house  during the game.
*****
The murder weapon will  be obvious as soon as you  EXAMINE  the
correct object.  (Though you will  need to find  the magnifying
glass before you can  really conduct an  adequate search of the
various  objects.)  You probably  should not GET  the weapon at
first since the murderer is likely to become nervous if you are
carrying  the evidence  around with  you.  If you  accidentally
pick up  the object  at too early a point  in the game, you can
DROP the weapon.  But be  forewarned!  Once  dropped there's no
telling where the object might end up!

Finding the scene of the murder  tends to be somewhat difficult
since it is rarely possible to examine  every inch of the house
with  your magnifying  glass.  Fortunately for  you, one of the
keen-eyed guests  will probably notice  something amiss at some
point during the game. To discover the murder room you must pay
close attention to the behavior of the guests!

The murderer will be the one person whose ALIBI contradicts the
alibis given  by the other houseguests.  Be  sure to keep track
of everyone's story!
*****
Once you have discovered both the murderer  and the murder room
you  should  hurry  back to  GET  the  murder  weapon, and then
move as  quickly as  possible to the  murder room,  GATHER  the
suspects  together, and  ACCUSE  whoever you believe  to be the
guilty  party.   But  be  careful!   The  murderer  is  a  very
suspicious  sort and  may decide  to get rid of  you before you
have a chance to make any accusations!

Most of the strategy involved in playing  SLEUTH  you will have
to learn by playing the game.  Since  the  game  changes  every
time  that  you  play  you  should be sure to fully explore the
house.  Don't forget to explore  the room which has no  obvious
entrances since  the murderer often discards  the murder weapon
here.  Also, the murder is occasionally committed in this room.
The  occupants  will be moving  about the house  throughout the
game.  If you become confused at any time about who is actually
Thinking · Analyzed game data file structures and formats.
Looking at the data file formats now — there's DESCR for descriptions, RMDEF for room definitions, ACT for actions, and WEAPN for weapons. The SUSPICT files are interesting at 2816 bytes each, which matches 80×35 character cells, suggesting they're probably screen data for suspect portraits or graphics, with SUSPICBW being the black and white variant.

The docs alone tell us a lot about game structure. Now let's dissect the data files — this is where your DESCR.V1 hacking memories live.

bash_toolHex dump start of DESCR.V1 to see record structure
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && echo "=== DESCR.V1 (first 600 bytes) ===" && xxd DESCR.V1 | head -40
Output
=== DESCR.V1 (first 600 bytes) ===
stderr
/bin/sh: 1: xxd: not found
bash_toolOctal/char dump of DESCR.V1 to see record structure
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && echo "=== DESCR.V1 ===" && od -A d -c DESCR.V1 | head -50
Output

Thinking · Identified file format and strategized approach to examine game data.
I'm treating DESCR.V1 as plain text files containing room descriptions, and it looks like character names might be stored there too since they were edited in those files. Let me read through the entire file to see what's in it.

It's plain CRLF text — no binary structure at all. Let's read the data files in full as text.

bash_toolView DESCR files as numbered text lines
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && for f in DESCR.V1 DESCR.V2; do echo "########## $f ##########"; tr -d '\r' < $f | cat -n; done | head -120
Output
########## DESCR.V1 ##########
     1	You are now in the dining room.  A large oak table dominates the room.  A silver
     2	serving set sits on a smaller table in one corner of the room.
     3	This is a secret passageway.  The corridor is covered with dusty cobwebs.
     4	In the dim light you can make out the traces of footprints on the dusty floor.
     5	You have entered the living room.  A large divan sits in the middle of the room.
     6	Many overstuffed chairs are arranged around the edge of the room.
     7	You are in a spacious kitchen.  A large butcher block table sits in the middle
     8	of the room.  The walls are lined with bright brass pots and pans.
     9	You are standing in a pantry.  The shelves are filled with a dazzling array of
    10	imported delicacies.
    11	This is the conservatory.  A baby grand piano sits on a mink throw rug.
    12	
    13	You are standing in the master bathroom.  Marble fixtures adorn the room.
    14	
    15	This room is a comfortably furnished library.  The shelves are lined with a
    16	large selection of dusty books.
    17	You are standing in the guest room.  The room is decorated in a tasteful
    18	oriental style, but looks rather barren.
    19	This is the master bedroom.  A canopied bed dominates the room.  To one side
    20	of the bed is a beautifully hand-carved dresser.
    21	You are standing in a well-appointed study.  Many papers are strewn about.
    22	Unlike the other rooms in the house, the study appears very lived-in.

    28	decorate the walls.

Thinking · Excavated game file structure and identified data sections.
I'm seeing the character definitions section with first and last names, lookup keys, and six suspects listed. There's also some leftover data after a DOS EOF marker at line 185 — looks like the file was truncated at some point, leaving fragments like "TORIA" and "MAUDE" behind. Now I'm moving on to examine the other sections: RMDEF for room definitions, ACT for actions, and WEAP.
Thinking · Deciphering numeric grid structure and tile mapping patterns.
Found the character names starting at line 164 with a DOS EOF marker at line 185 followed by orphaned text, suggesting the file was hand-edited. Now I'll check the map file.

Thinking · Reverse-engineered game data structure and planned visualization approach.
Looking at this numeric grid, it seems to be a tile map of the house with 813 lines total, where each number represents a room ID or tile type. The pattern shows groups of repeated numbers separated by zeros — like runs of 1s, then 15s, then 4s and 7s — suggesting distinct room or area sections laid out in a 2D grid structure. The tail section contains coordinate pairs and a permutation of room numbers 1-12, which likely represent label positions or door locations. I should write a Python script to parse and visualize the grid as ASCII art to make sense of the structure, then examine the other data files like ACT, WEAPN, ALIBI, SUNDRIES, and SUSPICT.

This is numeric grid data — almost certainly the house map. Let me check the remaining data files before writing a parser.

bash_toolView SUNDRIES and ALIBI1 text files
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && echo "##### SUNDRIES #####" && tr -d '\r' < SUNDRIES | cat -n && echo "##### ALIBI1 (head) #####" && tr -d '\r' < ALIBI1 | cat -n | head -30

defiantly at you, but says nothing.\                           As I said the last time you asked,\                                   I've already told you that\                                           I've already told you everything.\                                    What is this, the inquisition?\                                       ␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀␀
Thinking · Catalogued game file structures and architecture components.
I'm looking at the data structure here — SUNDRIES holds the verb and noun lookup tables along with preset responses, while ALIBI1 is a fixed-width record format with dialogue templates. Then there are several binary files to process: ALIBI2, ACT.V1, WEAPN.V1, and SUSPICT, which appear to contain portrait data.
Thinking · Thought process
I found that SUNDRIES is the parser vocabulary table mapping verbs and nouns to action and object IDs, and ALIBI1 contains dialogue templates. Let me continue examining the code.
bash_toolSample ALIBI2 and ACT.V1 contents
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && echo "##### ALIBI2 sample #####" && fold -w 70 ALIBI2 | head -30 && echo "" && echo "##### ACT.V1 head #####" && tr -d '\r' < ACT.V1 | cat -n | head -50

Thinking · Deciphering BSAVE screen format and file structure.
I'm recognizing the file signature for SUSPICT.V* files — they start with a specific byte sequence that translates to 0xFD 0x00 0xB0 0x00 0x00 0xA0 in hex. The rest of the file appears to be pairs of character codes and display attributes, like 0xDB (the full block character) paired with 0x07 for light gray text on black background, which suggests this is a BSAVE-format screen dump. The header structure follows BASIC's BSAVE format: a 0xFD marker, then the segment address (0xB000 for monochrome video memory), offset, and length. The data portion is 2720 bytes, which works out to exactly 1360 character-attribute cells — that's 80 columns by 17 rows of text-mode display. So each SUSPICT file is essentially a snapshot of a text-mode screen showing a suspect portrait drawn entirely in CP437 block characters. I can render these portraits by converting the character and attribute pairs back into displayable form. Now I'm looking at the EXE files — SLEUTH.EXE at 57KB and SLEUTH2.EXE at 77KB. Given the BSAVE files, these are likely compiled from BASIC, or possibly Pascal or C. I should check the strings embedded in the executables to figure out what compiler or runtime was used.
Thinking · Pinpointing Sleuth's compilation origins in IBM BASIC.
I've identified the file formats: WEAPN.V1 contains fixed-width weapon records, and SUSPICT.V* files are BSAVE screen dumps of CP437 block character portraits captured from video memory that I can render. Let me start by examining the executable.
bash_toolIdentify compiler/runtime of SLEUTH.EXE via strings
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && strings -n 8 SLEUTH.EXE | head -50 && echo "=== distinctive strings ===" && strings -n 6 SLEUTH.EXE | grep -iE "basic|pascal|turbo|microsoft|copyright|runtime|error" | sort -u | head -20

Thinking · Identified compiled BASIC source and examined executable variations.
So Sleuth was written in BASIC and compiled with IBM's BASIC Compiler—the distinctive error messages and BSAVE portrait file format confirm this. That explains the technical details I was seeing in the executable. Now I'm extracting the in-game message strings from the EXE and comparing SLEUTH.EXE against SLEUTH2.EXE to understand what changed between versions. The README suggests SLEUTH2 might be a compatibility version without sound, or it could use different data files and graphics handling. I should check CATALOG.TXT to see if it clarifies the differences.
Thinking · Parsing game logic, rendering maps and portraits efficiently.
I've identified that Sleuth is compiled BASIC using the IBM/Microsoft BASIC Compiler runtime, which explains the file formats and error strings. Now I'll extract the embedded game text and check the documentation.
bash_toolExtract embedded game text from the executable
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && strings -n 10 SLEUTH.EXE | grep -vE "rro|GOSU|DAT$" | sed -n '1,80p'

Thinking · Strategized efficient reverse-engineering of game mechanics and file structures.
I've got most of the game logic mapped out from the embedded strings, so now I need to quickly check the documentation files to clarify the SLEUTH versus SLEUTH2 distinction, then parse the map data and render a suspect portrait before putting together the full explanation.
Thinking · Parsing RMDEF.V1 file structure into mappable data.
I can see the game logic in the strings, so now I'll check what makes SLEUTH2.EXE different and then reconstruct the map.

bash_toolCheck EXE headers and look for compat-related strings in SLEUTH2
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && grep -c "" /tmp/s1.txt /tmp/s2.txt && grep -iE "286|8088|adapter|color|mono|speed|sound" /tmp/s2.txt | head; head -c 2 SLEUTH.EXE | od -c; head -c 28 SLEUTH.EXE | od -A d -t u2; head -c 28 SLEUTH2.EXE | od -A d -t u2

Thinking · Thought process
SLEUTH2.EXE has more strings than the original, suggesting it was compiled with a different compiler version, likely QuickBASIC, which doesn't affect the core analysis. Now I'm parsing RMDEF.V1 into a structured map—I'll start by reading the tokens and checking if the first ~800 values represent a 40×20 grid, then verify the grid width using autocorrelation across the 813 lines before isolating the remaining tables at the end. Looking at the room mappings, the values 1–16 correspond to room IDs (dining, secret passageway, living, kitchen, pantry, conservatory, bathroom, library, guest, bedroom, study, plus stairs and halls), while values above 20 appear to encode doors as room ID + 20—so 21 is a door to room 1, 24 to room 4, and so on. The doorway descriptions in the second part of the file follow the same room order, confirming this pattern. I'll write a parser that decodes these room and door IDs, then render the grid with proper labels and also extract the SUSPICT portrait by skipping the 7-byte BSAVE header and converting the character-attribute pairs into CP437 Unicode for display.
Thinking · Reverse-engineered game data structures and devised parsing strategy.
I'll write a Python parser to reconstruct the house map from RMDEF and render a suspect portrait from the BSAVE dump.
bash_toolParse RMDEF numbers and probe for grid width
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
import re
data = open('RMDEF.V1','rb').read().decode('cp437', errors='replace')
# strip after Ctrl-Z (DOS EOF)
main = data.split('\x1a')[0]
nums = [int(x) for x in re.findall(r'-?\d+', main)]
print("total numbers:", len(nums))
# try widths to find grid
for w in (38,39,40,41,42):
    rows = len(nums)//w
    print(f"width {w}: {rows} full rows, remainder {len(nums)-rows*w}")
EOF
Output
total numbers: 798
width 38: 21 full rows, remainder 0
width 39: 20 full rows, remainder 18
width 40: 19 full rows, remainder 38
width 41: 19 full rows, remainder 19
width 42: 19 full rows, remainder 0
Thinking · Deciphering grid dimensions and data storage patterns.
I'm working through the math on the grid size—798 total numbers minus 24 for the tail data (pairs and permutation) gives 774, which doesn't divide cleanly. Maybe the grid isn't square, or the rows have variable lengths. Looking at the early pattern, I'm seeing 41 values per visual row, so I need to check if that's consistent throughout.
bash_toolRender map grid at width 40 to inspect structure
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
import re
data = open('RMDEF.V1','rb').read().decode('cp437').split('\x1a')[0]
nums = [int(x) for x in re.findall(r'-?\d+', data)]

def render(w, count):
    sym = lambda v: '.' if v==0 else (chr(ord('a')+v-1) if 1<=v<=26 else ('%' if v>=21 else '?'))
    for r in range(count//w):
        row = nums[r*w:(r+1)*w]
        print(''.join(f"{v:>3}" for v in row))
    print("leftover:", nums[count:])

# inspect first 2 'rows' at width 40 with raw values
render(40, 760)
EOF
Output

leftover: [5, 54, 7, 44, 15, 44, 8, 76, 15, 66, 16, 18, 16, 62, 16, 58, 2, 20, 18, 20, 16, 36, 17, 22, 17, 22, 1, 11, 3, 8, 10, 4, 9, 6, 2, 5, 7, 12]
Thinking · Decoding map structure and weapon placement coordinates.
The map decodes perfectly at a width of 40 pixels, giving me a 40×17 grid where each value represents different elements—walls at 0, the eleven named rooms numbered 1 through 11 in the same order as their descriptions, staircases at 12 and 13, and various halls at 14 and 15. Door entries follow a pattern where values 21–31 correspond to doors leading into specific rooms (subtracting 20 from the value gives the room number), with stair landings at 32 and 33, and the front door at 36. What's particularly clever is that the secret passage room has no door value visible in the static map—the secret panel entry (value 22) must be placed dynamically or hidden, which perfectly matches the description of it being a room with no obvious entrances. The remaining 118 numbers after the 680-value grid appear to be coordinate pairs or object placement data, likely marking weapon locations throughout the mansion. With around 59 pairs and roughly 16 weapons in the game, these could be encoding room and position information for each weapon's placement. Looking at the pattern more closely, I notice the coordinates go up to 77 in the column dimension, which matches the 80-character screen width—and since each map cell is 2 characters wide on screen, that explains why the underlying grid is only 40 cells wide. So these coordinate pairs are screen positions where weapons appear in each room. The last sequence of numbers looks like a permutation of the room indices, probably controlling the randomized order in which rooms are visited during gameplay. Now I'm examining the WEAPN file structure — it's 7296 bytes total, and I'm trying to figure out the record size. If there are 12 weapons, that'd be 608 bytes each, but the field layout I'm seeing suggests fixed-width records with descriptions, names, and examination text. Let me work through the actual byte boundaries to pin down the exact stride. Next, I'll render the map with letters to mark locations, create a suspect portrait, and write up the explanation.
Thinking · Mapping the house layout across dual floors.
I've decoded the map as a 40×17 grid where each cell represents 2 screen characters, with walls, rooms (1–16), and doors (value = room ID + 20). Now I'll render it and decode a suspect portrait.
bash_toolRender the house map as ASCII art with room legend
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
import re
data = open('RMDEF.V1','rb').read().decode('cp437').split('\x1a')[0]
nums = [int(x) for x in re.findall(r'-?\d+', data)]
W,H = 40,17
grid = [nums[r*W:(r+1)*W] for r in range(H)]

rooms = {1:'Dining',2:'SecretPassage',3:'Living',4:'Kitchen',5:'Pantry',
         6:'Conservatory',7:'Bathroom',8:'Library',9:'GuestRm',10:'Bedroom',
         11:'Study',12:'StairsLo',13:'StairsUp',14:'UpHall',15:'MainHall',16:'Stoop'}
# letter per room
letters = {0:'█'}
for i,(k,v) in enumerate(rooms.items()):
    letters[k] = 'DPLKNCBYGMSvwhHF'[i]
out=[]
for row in grid:
    line=''
    for v in row:
        if v==0: line+='██'
        elif v in letters: line+=letters[v]*2
        elif v>=21: line+='/'+str(v-20) if v-20<10 else '/.'   # door
        else: line+='??'
    out.append(line)
print('\n'.join(out))
print()
print('Legend:', ', '.join(f"{letters[k]}={v}" for k,v in rooms.items()))
print('/n = door into room n;  note room 2 (SecretPassage) has NO door on the map')
EOF
Output
██DDDDDDDDDDDDDD██HHHHHH██KKKKKKKKKKKKKK██BBBBBBBBBBBBBB██hhhhhh██MMMMMMMMMMMMMM

Legend: D=Dining, P=SecretPassage, L=Living, K=Kitchen, N=Pantry, C=Conservatory, B=Bathroom, Y=Library, G=GuestRm, M=Bedroom, S=Study, v=StairsLo, w=StairsUp, h=UpHall, H=MainHall, F=Stoop
/n = door into room n;  note room 2 (SecretPassage) has NO door on the map
Thinking · Analyzed game architecture, mapped floor layouts, and planned rendering approach.
The map displays both floors side by side on a single 80×17 screen, with the main hall running vertically around column 18 and the upstairs hall at column 58, connected by stairs at the bottom. The left half shows the ground floor rooms including the dining area, secret passage, living room, kitchen, pantry, and conservatory. The secret passage is the sealed room with no obvious entrances, and its hidden door isn't visible in the static map—it's likely stored in those auxiliary door entries or revealed dynamically. Now I'm moving on to render a suspect portrait by reading the BSAVE format: a 7-byte header followed by character and attribute pairs across 80 columns, with the length field indicating 2720 bytes for 17 rows, so I'll extract just the character data.
Thinking · Deciphering how SUSPICT.V2 stores pre-drawn map graphics.
I can see the full house layout fits on one screen with the ground floor on the left and upper floor on the right, connected by staircases at the bottom. Now I'll examine the portraits.
bash_toolDecode SUSPICT.V2 BSAVE screen dump into visible text-art portrait
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
import struct
raw = open('SUSPICT.V2','rb').read()
assert raw[0] == 0xFD
seg, ofs, length = struct.unpack('<HHH', raw[1:7])
print(f"BSAVE header: segment {seg:04X}h, offset {ofs:04X}h, length {length} bytes")
cells = raw[7:7+length]
ncells = length//2
cols = 80
rows = ncells//cols
print(f"-> {cols} x {rows} text cells\n")
# CP437 -> unicode
table = bytes(range(256)).decode('cp437')
for r in range(rows):
    line = ''.join(table[cells[(r*cols+c)*2]] for c in range(cols))
    print(line.rstrip())
EOF
Output
BSAVE header: segment B000h, offset 0000h, length 2720 bytes
-> 80 x 17 text cells

█                      █▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄   ▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄█       █              █
█              █                                               █▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄█
█              █                                               █              █
█▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█       █▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█   █▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█       █              █
█              █                     █   █             █                      █
█▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄█       █▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄█   █             █       █              █
█              █                         █             █       █▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄█
█              █                         █                     █              █
█                      █▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█             █       █              █

█              █───────█                 █             █───────█              █

Thinking · Investigated game file formats and map layout structures.
So SUSPICT.V2 isn't a portrait at all—it's the pre-rendered house map screen itself, stored as a BSAVE image with walls, doors, and stairs already drawn in. The game loads this directly into video memory instead of rendering it piece by piece, giving instant screen display on the original 8088. This suggests SUSPICT.V0 through V9 are ten different house layouts, each with their own timestamp from 1986. Now I'm wondering if these variants are slight randomizations of door positions, or if they're completely different rooms. The file sizes are interesting too—SUSPICBW is only 1394 bytes, roughly half the size of the others, which might indicate a different resolution or compression scheme. Let me compare the actual data across these files to understand what's changing between versions.
Thinking · Deciphering file layout patterns and version architecture.
I found that SUSPICT.V2 is actually the pre-drawn house map screen saved directly from video memory, which the game loads instantly into the video buffer. Now I'll compare it with the other nine versions to figure out what they are.
bash_toolDiff the ten SUSPICT screens and inspect SUSPICBW format
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
import struct
def load(fn):
    raw = open(fn,'rb').read()
    seg, ofs, length = struct.unpack('<HHH', raw[1:7])
    return raw[7:7+length], seg

base, _ = load('SUSPICT.V2')
table = bytes(range(256)).decode('cp437')
for v in range(10):
    fn = f'SUSPICT.V{v}'
    d, seg = load(fn)
    diffs = [i for i in range(0, min(len(base),len(d)), 2) if d[i]!=base[i]]
    cellpos = [(i//2//80, i//2%80) for i in diffs]
    print(f"{fn}: seg {seg:04X}h, {len(diffs)} char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: {cellpos[:12]}{'...' if len(cellpos)>12 else ''}")

# and the BW files
for fn in ('SUSPICBW.V1','SUSPICBW.V2'):
    raw = open(fn,'rb').read()
    print(fn, len(raw), raw[:8].hex(), '...')
EOF
Output
SUSPICT.V0: seg B000h, 0 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: []
SUSPICT.V1: seg B000h, 300 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: [(0, 16), (0, 17), (0, 18), (0, 19), (0, 20), (0, 21), (0, 22), (0, 39), (1, 38), (1, 40), (2, 38), (2, 40)]...
SUSPICT.V2: seg B000h, 0 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: []
SUSPICT.V3: seg B000h, 300 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: [(0, 16), (0, 17), (0, 18), (0, 19), (0, 20), (0, 21), (0, 22), (0, 39), (1, 38), (1, 40), (2, 38), (2, 40)]...
SUSPICT.V4: seg B000h, 0 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: []
SUSPICT.V5: seg B000h, 300 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: [(0, 16), (0, 17), (0, 18), (0, 19), (0, 20), (0, 21), (0, 22), (0, 39), (1, 38), (1, 40), (2, 38), (2, 40)]...
SUSPICT.V6: seg B000h, 0 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: []
SUSPICT.V7: seg B000h, 300 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: [(0, 16), (0, 17), (0, 18), (0, 19), (0, 20), (0, 21), (0, 22), (0, 39), (1, 38), (1, 40), (2, 38), (2, 40)]...
SUSPICT.V8: seg B000h, 0 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: []
SUSPICT.V9: seg B000h, 300 char cells differ from V2 at rows/cols: [(0, 16), (0, 17), (0, 18), (0, 19), (0, 20), (0, 21), (0, 22), (0, 39), (1, 38), (1, 40), (2, 38), (2, 40)]...
SUSPICBW.V1 1394 dbdbdbdbdbdbdbdb ...
SUSPICBW.V2 1394 dbdbdbdbdbdbdbdb ...
Thinking · Deciphered file layout patterns and verified data structure theories.
The pattern's becoming clearer — even-numbered SUSPECT files follow one layout while odd-numbered ones follow another, suggesting the program generates filenames based on the house variant suffix. Now I need to examine whether attribute differences like colors account for the variation across these ten files. SUSPICBW appears to be a plain text version of the map stored as 17 rows of 82 bytes each (80 characters plus CRLF), designed for direct printing without needing BLOAD. Let me verify this structure and check the attribute bytes to see if they're what's creating the visual differences.
bash_toolCheck attribute bytes across SUSPICT variants and SUSPICBW row structure
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
import struct
def load(fn):
    raw = open(fn,'rb').read()
    return raw[7:7+2720]
base = load('SUSPICT.V0')
for v in (2,4,6,8):
    d = load(f'SUSPICT.V{v}')
    chardiff = sum(1 for i in range(0,2720,2) if d[i]!=base[i])
    attrdiff = sum(1 for i in range(1,2720,2) if d[i]!=base[i])
    attrs = sorted(set(d[1::2]))
    print(f"V{v}: chardiffs={chardiff} attrdiffs={attrdiff} attrs used={[hex(a) for a in attrs]}")
for v in (1,3,5,7,9):
    d = load(f'SUSPICT.V{v}')
    attrs = sorted(set(d[1::2]))
    print(f"V{v}: attrs used={[hex(a) for a in attrs]}")
# SUSPICBW: is it 17 rows of 80 + CRLF?
raw = open('SUSPICBW.V1','rb').read()
print("\nSUSPICBW.V1 rows of 82?", len(raw), len(raw)/82)
print(raw[80:84])
txt = raw.decode('cp437')
print(txt[:82*3])
EOF
Output
V2: chardiffs=0 attrdiffs=1360 attrs used=['0x7']
V4: chardiffs=0 attrdiffs=1360 attrs used=['0x3', '0x17']
V6: chardiffs=0 attrdiffs=1360 attrs used=['0x5', '0x47']
V8: chardiffs=0 attrdiffs=1360 attrs used=['0x2', '0x57']
V1: attrs used=['0x7']
V3: attrs used=['0x7', '0x17']
V5: attrs used=['0x7', '0x47']
V7: attrs used=['0x7', '0x57']
V9: attrs used=['0x7', '0x67']

SUSPICBW.V1 rows of 82? 1394 17.0
b'\r\n\xdb '
████████████████▀▀▀▀▀▀▀████████████████ ███████████████████████████████████████ 
█              █       █              █ █              █       █              █ 
█              █       █              █ █                      █              █
Thinking · Decoding Sleuth's attribute system and color scheme mechanics.
So the SUSPICT files use even-numbered variants for one house layout and odd-numbered ones for another, with the digit determining the color scheme—grayscale for V0/V1, then various foreground/background color combinations for the others like white text on blue, red, magenta, or brown backgrounds. This gives ten total files across two layouts and five color palettes, selected randomly by the game. SUSPICBW.V1 and V2 are plain-text backups for systems where BLOAD into video memory isn't reliable, which explains why the README mentions running SLEUTH2 if there are problems—that program probably prints the map as text instead or handles CGA display issues. Interestingly, SUSPICBW.V1 has a different top row with a segment pattern that might be layout 2's hidden panel. Now I'm trying to nail down the WEAPN record structure by finding the backslash separators to infer field widths. For the response, I have enough detail already, but I should also check ALIBI2's template characters where # marks victim names and @ marks accused suspects, with ALIBI1 containing 70-byte reply framings and ALIBI2 likely using 71-byte records across its 10624 bytes. I counted the weapons in WEAPN.V1—candelabra, obelisk, candy dish, leg of lamb, vichyssoise, oboe, jar of cream, reading stand, ashtray, champagne bottle, brass plaque—probably one per room across the 11 rooms. The file is 7296 bytes, so if there are 16 records that's 456 bytes each, or 12 records at 608 bytes each. Each record has 5 fields separated by backslashes, and I can compute the exact field widths by finding where those separators fall.
Thinking · Deciphering backslash field terminators in BASIC record structures.
I've confirmed the two layouts and five color schemes are working, with SUSPICBW as a fallback for incompatible displays. Now I'll verify the weapon record stride.
bash_toolDetermine fixed-width field layout of WEAPN records
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
raw = open('WEAPN.V1','rb').read()
bs = [i for i,b in enumerate(raw) if b==0x5C]
print("first 12 backslash offsets:", bs[:12])
print("deltas:", [bs[i+1]-bs[i] for i in range(11)])
# guess record length from repeating pattern
print("total file:", len(raw))
# try record len = bs[5]+? look at where second record's desc starts
print(repr(raw[0:90]))
print(repr(raw[228:320]))
EOF
Output
first 12 backslash offsets: [58, 90, 120, 131, 180, 258, 307, 338, 352, 413, 503, 530]
deltas: [32, 30, 11, 49, 78, 49, 31, 14, 61, 90, 27]
total file: 7296
b'A rather large candelabra sits in the middle of the table.\\                     candelabra'
b'ian obelisk lies on the floor.\\                                         obelisk\\            '
Thinking · Decoding record structure and architecting the response framework.
I'm trying to figure out how the backslash positions map to the record structure—it seems like records might be fixed-size chunks (record 1 from offset 0-227, record 2 starting at 228), with backslashes serving as field or string terminators that the program scans for after reading via random-access FIELD operations. Looking at the data, one record descriptor shows an offset of 180 bytes, and the next description appears to be cut off mid-text about an obsidian obelisk. Now I'm calculating the record stride: if the file is 7296 bytes total and there are roughly 32 records, that gives a stride of 228 bytes per record. The field layout appears to be roughly description (80 bytes), name (30 bytes), two key fields (15 bytes each), and examine text (88 bytes), which adds up to 228. I can see evidence of this in the byte offsets—the backslash at position 90 aligns with where the name field should end, and the weapon names like "candelabra" and "CANDELABRA" appear at the expected positions. There seem to be multiple sets of weapons (possibly 2 sets of 16, or more), with distinct items like obelisk, candy dish, leg of lamb, and others appearing sequentially through the records. Now I'm ready to write the explanation for the user. They want a breakdown of how the file structure works plus a brief take on the JavaScript versus Python question for the remaster. I'll keep it conversational and organized with some structure, since this is technical material, but lean toward prose rather than heavy formatting. I'll mention that JavaScript is better for instant browser playability and easy sharing with trivial text rendering, while Python excels at the data extraction tooling side—both have their roles in this project.

That's everything I need. Here's the full anatomy of Sleuth.

The big picture

Sleuth is a compiled BASIC program (IBM/Microsoft BASIC Compiler — the runtime error strings like "RETURN without GOSUB" and "Licensed Material - Program Property of IBM" are embedded in the EXE). That single fact explains the entire architecture, because 1983-era BASIC pushed Eric Miller toward a very clean separation:

The EXE is the engine; nearly all content lives in external text files. This is why your teenage name-hacking worked — you were doing legitimate data-driven modding twenty years before that was a buzzword. The engine reads sequential CRLF text files and fixed-width record files at startup, and the only content baked into the EXE is the system messages (menu text, "There are traces of blood on it! This must be the murder weapon!", the rank titles Apprentice/Professional/Holmesian, etc.).

The file roster, decoded

The .V1/.V2 suffix = two house layouts. The game ships two complete content sets and picks one, which is why files come in pairs.

RMDEF.V1/V2 — the house map. A 40×17 grid of integers, one per cell, where each cell is two characters wide on the 80-column screen. The encoding is elegant: 0 = wall, 1–16 = room IDs (1 dining, 2 secret passage, 3 living room … 12/13 lower/upper stairs, 14/15 the two halls, 16 the front stoop), and doors are room ID + 20 — so a 24 in the grid is a doorway into room 4 (kitchen). Both floors are drawn side-by-side on one screen, connected by the staircases at the bottom (hence the manual's warning about changing direction on stairs — you're crossing between the two halves). The secret passage genuinely has no door value in the grid; the "secret panel" (door code 22) is handled in code. After the 680 grid values comes a tail of coordinate pairs (anchor positions for placing people/objects in each room) and a 12-value permutation table used for randomization.

DESCR.V1/V2 — descriptions, examinables, and names. Four sections in one file: two-line room descriptions; doorway descriptions; short room names; then per-room examinable objects (room keyword, two object keywords, and a response line per object — z is a "no object" placeholder); and finally the suspect name triplets (First / Last / UPPERCASE match key) that you used to edit. Fun fossil: your copy of DESCR.V1 has a DOS EOF character (Ctrl-Z) mid-file with orphaned name fragments after it — physical evidence of a past hand-editing session that shortened the file.

WEAPN.V1/V2 — fixed-width weapon records, ~228 bytes each, ~32 per file: scene description ("A rather large candelabra sits in the middle of the table."), display name, two parser keywords (CANDELABRA / CANDLE), and an examine response. Roughly one weapon per room per set — the game picks which objects appear, and one of them secretly becomes the murder weapon.

ACT.V1/V2 — the "what people are doing" flavor text. Backslash-terminated fixed-width strings, two idle actions per room, in repeated blocks: "sits at the piano playing a somber concerto", "is hiding under the bed", and the wonderful era-spanning reading list (Gatsby, Valley of the Dolls, Catch-22, The Mousetrap — a wink, that one). When you enter a room, each occupant gets one of these.

ALIBI1 / ALIBI2 — the dialogue system. ALIBI1 holds ~70-byte reply framings ("turns angrily and hisses…", "draws you closer and whispers…") including the repeat-question responses ("As I told you before,"). ALIBI2 holds longer alibi paragraph templates with placeholder tokens: #+++++++++ substitutes the victim's name, @+++++++++ substitutes another suspect's name. The actual alibi logic — who claims to be with whom, in which room, and the one contradiction that fingers the murderer — is generated by the engine; these files are the prose skin. The embedded EXE strings show the skeleton: "I spent the entire evening with…", "…and I spent the entire evening together in the…".

SUNDRIES — the parser's vocabulary. Verb → action-ID table (EXAMINE,1 GET,3 GATHER,4 ACCUSE,7…), generic-noun → object-ID table (FLOOR,1 DOOR,5…), and canned refusals ("You can't take the floor no matter how hard you try!"). The parser is just: scan input for a known verb keyword, then for a known noun/name keyword. That's why Q VICTORIA works — "Q " is literally in the verb table.

SUSPICT.V0–V9 — and here's the best discovery — these aren't suspect portraits at all. They're BSAVE video-memory dumps of the house map screen: a 7-byte header (0xFD marker, segment B000h, offset, length) followed by raw character/attribute pairs. The game BLOADs the file straight into video RAM — instant full-screen paint instead of watching BASIC draw 1,360 cells one PRINT at a time on a 4.77 MHz 8088. Ten files = 2 layouts × 5 color schemes (the character bytes are identical within each layout; only attribute bytes differ — gray, white-on-blue, white-on-red, white-on-magenta, white-on-brown). SUSPICBW.V1/V2 are plain-text fallback maps (17 rows × 80 chars + CRLF) for displays where direct video-memory writes misbehave — which is almost certainly the SLEUTH vs SLEUTH2.EXE distinction the README hints at.

How a game session works

  1. At "PLEASE WAIT WHILE THE MAID TAKES CARE OF SOME HOUSECLEANING," the engine picks a layout/colors, seeds the randomizer, and secretly chooses: victim, murderer, murder room, murder weapon.
  2. It places six suspects, scatters the weapon set through the rooms, drops the magnifying glass, and generates a consistent set of alibis — pairing suspects together in rooms — except the murderer's claim, which contradicts a partner's.
  3. You wander (arrow keys move your @ around the BLOADed screen; room ID lookups come from the RMDEF grid), EXAMINE objects (magnifying glass gates real searching; the weapon reveals "traces of blood"), QUESTION/ALIBI people (moody RNG decides if they answer; ALIBI1 framings + ALIBI2 templates + name substitution produce dialogue), and watch for a guest to notice the murder room.
  4. Endgame state machine: carry weapon → reach murder room → GATHER → ACCUSE. Wrong elements get distinct failure responses (the strings for "right room, but I was the one who used the…" are all in the EXE). Dawdle too long while carrying the weapon and the murderer kills you.

On the remaster question (preview)

Short version for now: JavaScript in the browser is the right target for the game itself, and Python is the right tool for the data-extraction/build tooling — and conveniently, that split mirrors the original's engine/data separation. A browser remaster is instantly playable and shareable (your GitHub Pages site could host it), text-grid rendering is trivial in HTML/canvas, and there's no install friction for friends. Python scripts (like the parsers I just wrote ad hoc) would convert the original .V1/.V2 files into clean JSON — rooms, weapons, actions, alibi templates — giving you a documented, human-editable content format where custom layouts and weapons become "just add a JSON file." That's also the best learning path: the data pipeline teaches you file formats and parsing; the engine teaches you game loops, state machines, and the parser — each piece small enough to actually grok rather than receive.

When you're ready, a sensible phase plan would be: (1) build the extraction toolkit and produce a fully-documented JSON content set, (2) re-implement the engine in plain readable JS with the original data, (3) only then add the extensibility layer (custom houses, weapons, verbs). Want me to start with phase 1 and produce the extractor plus a written spec of every file format?

bash_toolAuto-detect record strides for all fixed-width record files
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
# Empirically detect record strides for the fixed-width files
def detect(fn):
    raw = open(fn,'rb').read()
    n = len(raw)
    results = []
    for S in range(40, 260):
        # score: every record slice should contain a '\' terminator,
        # and record starts shouldn't be padding bytes
        recs = n // S
        if recs < 4: break
        good = 0
        for k in range(recs):
            sl = raw[k*S:(k+1)*S]
            if b'\\' in sl and sl[0:1] not in (b'\x00',) :
                good += 1
        if good == recs and n % S == 0:
            results.append((S, recs, 'exact'))
        elif good == recs:
            results.append((S, recs, f'rem {n%S}'))
    return n, results

for fn in ('ACT.V1','ALIBI1','ALIBI2','WEAPN.V1'):
    n, res = detect(fn)
    print(fn, n, 'candidates:', res[:8])
EOF
Output
ACT.V1 6656 candidates: [(63, 105, 'rem 41'), (66, 100, 'rem 56'), (69, 96, 'rem 32'), (74, 89, 'rem 70'), (78, 85, 'rem 26'), (79, 84, 'rem 20'), (80, 83, 'rem 16'), (81, 82, 'rem 14')]
ALIBI1 2560 candidates: [(70, 36, 'rem 40'), (80, 32, 'exact'), (83, 30, 'rem 70'), (84, 30, 'rem 40'), (85, 30, 'rem 10'), (86, 29, 'rem 66'), (88, 29, 'rem 8'), (94, 27, 'rem 22')]
ALIBI2 10624 candidates: []
WEAPN.V1 7296 candidates: [(84, 86, 'rem 72'), (89, 81, 'rem 87'), (93, 78, 'rem 42'), (96, 76, 'exact'), (98, 74, 'rem 44'), (99, 73, 'rem 69'), (110, 66, 'rem 36'), (111, 65, 'rem 81')]
