Thinking · Orchestrated Phase 1 closure with deliverables and forward trajectory.
I should wrap up Phase 1 by summarizing what we've delivered and what we've learned, then point toward the next steps—keeping it brief since the real details are in the actual work.

Phase 1 complete. Here's what you've got:

extract_sleuth.py — the toolkit itself, ~450 lines of stdlib-only Python with heavy commenting, since reading it is the curriculum. The structure mirrors how the reverse-engineering actually went: low-level readers for the three physical formats (DOS text with Ctrl-Z handling, FIELD-style fixed records with \ terminators, BSAVE memory dumps), then one parser per logical file, then a driver that assembles everything. Run it as python3 extract_sleuth.py <sleuth_dir> <output_dir> against your own copy anytime.

FORMATS.md — the full spec: every file, every offset and stride, with confidence tags ([verified] / [inferred] / [open]) so you know which claims are proven and which are working hypotheses. The honest gaps are flagged too: 37 coordinate pairs in RMDEF's tail and the 12-value permutation table remain [open] — best decoded later by experiment in DOSBox (change a value, see what moves), or simply replaced by your own placement logic in the remaster.

sleuth_extracted.zip — the JSON content set plus renders: content_v1.json and content_v2.json (cast, rooms with examinables and NPC actions folded in, map grid, doors, weapons), content_shared.json (parser vocabulary, dialogue framings, alibi templates), ASCII map renders of both houses, the decoded BSAVE screens, and the engine message catalogs pulled from both EXEs.

Discoveries that emerged during validation, beyond what we'd found earlier: V2 isn't just a remixed floor plan — it's a second complete game, with its own cast (the Donleavys, Setons, and Winstons), its own mansion (vault, ballroom, butler's room, a central hall cross, front lawn), and a 17th location code V1 never uses. Each cast file holds seven names, not six — one becomes the victim each game. And each WEAPN file carries 33 objects for ~11 rooms, which is how the game stays fresh: a different subset is scattered every playthrough.

A few things worth reading first, in order: map_v1.txt next to screen_layout1.txt (the abstract grid vs. what players actually saw — same house, two representations), then the parse_weapn function alongside §4 of the spec (the fossil-data story is the single best lesson in the codebase about why "fixed-width with terminator" formats must be parsed strictly), then content_shared.json's alibi templates to see how far placeholder substitution stretched limited disk space.

When you're ready for Phase 2 — the JS engine — the natural first milestone is small and satisfying: render the map from content_v1.json in a browser and walk an @ around it with collision against walls and door transitions. That's maybe 150 lines, exercises the JSON immediately, and gives you something playable-feeling in one session before any mystery logic exists. Want to start there next time?

2026-06-12 06:08
You

Sure, let's proceed to phase 2 next.

2026-06-12 10:32
Claude
Thinking · Architecting a playable browser engine with embedded data separation.
For the first milestone, I want to create a self-contained HTML file that loads the map from the JSON and lets the user walk an `@` character around with collision detection and door transitions—roughly 150 lines to keep it digestible. The tricky part is that fetching a local JSON file from `file://` protocol hits CORS restrictions, so I need to figure out the best approach for someone learning: either embedding the JSON directly in the HTML, using a simple local server, or converting the data inline. The cleanest solution that preserves the engine/data separation (which mirrors the original design philosophy) is to create a separate `content_v1.js` file that exports the data as `window.SLEUTH_DATA`, since script tags load fine from `file://` without a server. This keeps the files separate for learning purposes while remaining practical. Before I start building the UI, I should check the frontend-design skill documentation to make sure I'm following best practices. My plan is to create the HTML engine with a DOS-style text grid renderer, keyboard controls, collision detection, and room transitions with a status line, then generate the data files from the JSON. I'll keep the code well-commented and around 150-250 lines so it's digestible in one sitting, and add a house switcher (maybe toggled with Tab) to demonstrate the data-driven architecture. I'll also write a learning guide with exercises like changing the player glyph or adding a minimap to help learners understand how to modify the engine. For the renderer, I'm deciding between a simple `<pre>` rebuild each frame versus a screen buffer abstraction that mirrors DOS text-mode video memory—a 2D array of character/foreground/background objects with put and print functions. The buffer approach is more educational and scalable, teaching the concept of emulating the B000h text buffer while keeping the 80×25 DOS dimensions authentic. For movement and collision, I'm handling arrow keys with a simple check: cells with value 0 are blocked, anything above 0 is walkable, and door cells (>20) let you step through into the next room. When entering a new room, I'll print its description to the message area. The secret passage in room 2 is a known gap for now—I'll note it as Phase 2b and keep it unreachable for this milestone. The player starts on the front stoop or lawn (room 16 or 17 depending on version), and I need to find a valid spawn cell in that room. When the player moves, I'll restore whatever character was underneath before drawing the new position. For rendering, walls become block characters in gray, floors are dots or spaces, doors get a distinct shade, and the player is @—straightforward grid-based display rather than trying to replicate the original's box-drawing complexity. For the visual style, I'm aiming for authentic DOS aesthetics with a black background and monospace font (sticking with system fonts like Courier New or ui-monospace to avoid external dependencies). The original game supported multiple color palettes, so I could include those five schemes as a toggle—that ties directly back to the SUSPICT discovery about how the game cycles through attribute combinations. Now I'm thinking through the deliverables: I need to generate the engine files and data files, but there's a practical constraint with how artifacts render. If I separate the HTML from the data files, the preview won't load external scripts. So I'm considering building a single self-contained HTML file that has all the data inlined, which would make it immediately playable in Claude's preview while still keeping the source files organized separately for download. For the data itself, I'm deciding whether to include everything upfront or slim it down for this milestone. The full content is substantial, but since Phase 2 will need the complete data anyway, I'll keep it all in the source files and let the build process handle what gets inlined for the playable version. I'm also planning a small Python build script that converts the extracted JSON into JavaScript data files and generates the single-file playable version — this demonstrates the full pipeline from original files through extraction and transformation to the final web build. Setting up the engine structure with HTML that includes CSS variables for styling, a text-mode screen buffer... Defining the screen dimensions and buffer structure with character and attribute pairs, then implementing functions to clear the screen, place characters, print strings, and render the buffer as HTML with spans grouped by attribute runs for efficient styling. Mapping the five SUSPICT color schemes to palette objects and emulating VGA attributes properly so that attribute bytes map to foreground and background colors from the 16-color table, with each scheme using its own attribute value to control the palette. Drawing the house from the data by iterating through grid cells... Now I'm laying out the message area at the bottom for wrapped text and a simple log of the last few lines, plus status and title sections. For movement, each grid cell spans two screen columns, so the player '@' gets positioned at the left column of its cell. Walkability checks whether a cell value is greater than zero to determine if the player can move there. I need to handle doors specially—they're walkable cells with values above 20, and stepping on one should display the room description. Room detection works by checking if the cell value falls between 1 and 20 to identify which room the player is in, then printing the room name and description if it changes. I'm also building a word-wrap utility for the message area, adding a Tab toggle to switch between house datasets and respawn at the starting position, and a C key to toggle the color palette. The starting position gets determined by finding the first cell of the start room, collecting all cells in that room, and picking the median one—data-driven so it works across different house versions. For the README, I'm documenting the architecture, reading order, and a series of exercises like modifying glyphs, adding a visited-room tracker, implementing secret panels, adding a minimap, and showing entry descriptions. I'll include instructions for running the project and outline next milestones around NPCs, objects with an examine parser, mystery generation, dialogue, and endgame content. I'm also handling the color system by mapping the attribute byte to CSS using a classic 16-color palette—the lower 4 bits give foreground color and the upper 3 bits give background color, then rendering each run of text with the appropriate span styling. Now I need to build the web data pipeline: a script that reads the extracted content files and generates the JavaScript data files for both versions, then create the playable HTML engine. I'll do a syntax check with Node if available and manually review the logic carefully, then package everything as a zip with the playable HTML and updated README.

Phase 2, milestone 1: a walkable house in the browser. Let me check the frontend skill first since I'm building UI:

viewRequired before frontend work: read the frontend-design skill
📄 /mnt/skills/public/frontend-design/SKILL.md
Output
     1	---
     2	name: frontend-design
     3	description: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
     4	license: Complete terms in LICENSE.txt
     5	---
     6	
     7	# Frontend Design
     8	
     9	Approach this as the design lead at a small studio known for giving every client a visual identity that could not be mistaken for anyone else's. This client has already rejected proposals that felt templated, and is paying for a distinctive point of view: make deliberate, opinionated choices about palette, typography, and layout that are specific to this brief, and take one real aesthetic risk you can justify.
    10	
    11	## Ground it in the subject
    12	
    13	If the brief does not pin down what the product or subject is, pin it yourself before designing: name one concrete subject, its audience, and the page's single job, and state your choice. If there's any information in your memory about the human's preferences, context about what they're building, or designs you've made before – use that as a hint. The subject's own world, its materials, instruments, artifacts, and vernacular, is where distinctive choices come from. Build with the brief's real content and subject matter throughout.
    14	
    15	## Design principles
    16	
    17	For web designs, the hero is a thesis. Open with the most characteristic thing in the subject's world, in whatever form makes sense for it: a headline, an image, an animation, a live demo, an interactive moment. Be deliberate with your choice: a big number with a small label, supporting stats, and a gradient accent is the template answer, only use if that's truly the best option.
    18	
    19	Typography carries the personality of the page. Pair the display and body faces deliberately, not the same families you would reach for on any other project, and set a clear type scale with intentional weights, widths, and spacing. Make the type treatment itself a memorable part of the design, not a neutral delivery vehicle for the content.
    20	
    21	Structure is information. Structural devices, numbering, eyebrows, dividers, labels, should encode something true about the content, not decorate it. Many generic designs use numbered markers (01 / 02 / 03), but that's only appropriate if the content actually is a sequence - like a real process or a typed timeline where order carries information the reader needs. Question if choices like numbered markers actually make sense before incorporating them.
    22	
    23	Leverage motion deliberately. Think about where and if animation can serve the subject: a page-load sequence, a scroll-triggered reveal, hover micro-interactions, ambient atmosphere. An orchestrated moment usually lands harder than scattered effects; choose what the direction calls for. However, sometimes less is more, and extra animation contributes to the feeling that the design is AI-generated.
    24	
    25	Match complexity to the vision. Maximalist directions need elaborate execution; minimal directions need precision in spacing, type, and detail. Elegance is executing the chosen vision well.
    26	
    27	Consider written content carefully. Often a design brief may not contain real content, and it's up to you to come up with copy. Copy can make a design feel as templated as the design itself. See the below section on writing for more guidance.
    28	
    29	## Process: brainstorm, explore, plan, critique, build, critique again
    30	
    31	For calibration: AI-generated design right now clusters around three looks: (1) a warm cream background (near #F4F1EA) with a high-contrast serif display and a terracotta accent; (2) a near-black background with a single bright acid-green or vermilion accent; (3) a broadsheet-style layout with hairline rules, zero border-radius, and dense newspaper-like columns. All three are legitimate for some briefs, but they are defaults rather than choices, and they appear regardless of subject. Where the brief pins down a visual direction, follow it exactly — the brief's own words always win, including when it asks for one of these looks. Where it leaves an axis free, don't spend that freedom on one of these defaults. Just like a human designer who's hired, there's often a careful balance between doing what you're good at and taking each project as a chance to experiment and learn.
    32	
    33	Work in two passes. First, brainstorm a short design plan based on the human's design brief: create a compact token system with color, type, layout, and signature. Color: describe the palette as 4–6 named hex values. Type: the typefaces for 2+ roles (a characterful display face that's used with restraint, a complementary body face, and a utility face for captions or data if needed). Layout: a layout concept, using one-sentence prose descriptions and ASCII wireframes to ideate and compare. Signature: the single unique element this page will be remembered by that embodies the brief in an appropriate way.
    34	
    35	Then review that plan against the brief before building: if any part of it reads like the generic default you would produce for any similar page (work through a similar prompt to see if you arrive somewhere similar) rather than a choice made for this specific brief — revise that part, say what you changed and why. Only after you've confirmed the relative uniqueness of your design plan should you start to write the code, following the revised plan exactly and deriving every color and type decision from it.
    36	
    37	When writing the code, be careful of structuring your CSS selector specificities. It's easy to generate CSS classes that cancel each other out (especially with a type-based selector like .section and a element-based selector like .cta). This can happen often with paddings/margins between sections.
    38	
    39	Try to do a lot of this planning and iteration in your thinking, and only show ideas to the user when you have higher confidence it'll delight them.
    40	
    41	## Restraint and self-critique
    42	
    43	Spend your boldness in one place. Let the signature element be the one memorable thing, keep everything around it quiet and disciplined, and cut any decoration that does not serve the brief. Not taking a risk can be a risk itself! Build to a quality floor without announcing it: responsive down to mobile, visible keyboard focus, reduced motion respected. Critique your own work as you build, taking screenshots if your environment supports it – a picture is worth 1000 tokens. Consider Chanel's advice: before leaving the house, take a look in the mirror and remove one accessory. Human creators have memory and always try to do something new, so if you have a space to quickly jot down notes about what you've tried, it can help you in future passes.
    44	
    45	## More on writing in design
    46	
    47	Words appear in a design for one reason: to make it easier to understand, and therefore easier to use. They are design material, not decoration. Bring the same intentionality to copy that you would bring to spacing and color. Before writing anything, ask what the design needs to say, and how it can best be said to help the person navigate the experience.
    48	
    49	Write from the end user's side of the screen. Name things by what people control and recognize, never by how the system is built. A person manages notifications, not webhook config. Describe what something does in plain terms rather than selling it. Being specific is always better than being clever.
    50	
    51	Use active voice as default. A control should say exactly what happens when it's used: "Save changes," not "Submit." An action keeps the same name through the whole flow, so the button that says "Publish" produces a toast that says "Published." The vocabulary of an interface is the signposting for someone navigating the product. Cohesion and consistency are how people learn their way around.
    52	
    53	Treat failure and emptiness as moments for direction, not mood. Explain what went wrong and how to fix it, in the interface's voice rather than a person's. Errors don't apologize, and they are never vague about what happened. An empty screen is an invitation to act.
    54	
    55	Keep the register conversational and tuned: plain verbs, sentence case, no filler, with tone matched to the brand and the audience. Let each element do exactly one job. A label labels, an example demonstrates, and nothing quietly does double duty.
    56
Thinking · Architecting web engine and inlining playable artifact.
I'm thinking through the design approach here: the core is an authentic 80×25 text-mode display that emulates the original 1983 DOS game, using the five color palettes extracted directly from the game's BSAVE attribute bytes. The surrounding interface should be minimal and understated—a dark workshop aesthetic that frames this as a restoration project rather than a polished product showcase, though there's an interesting tension in how to present that without losing impact. The visual strategy is to treat the running game like a museum specimen: the screen sits in a restrained bezel with a small label identifying what's loaded (the house, palette, source files), and I'm being careful to avoid scanline overload—just a subtle effect on the screen itself, respecting reduced-motion preferences. The color palette for the chrome is deliberately muted: near-black slate backgrounds, a slightly lighter bezel, pale ink, and a single accent color (phosphor amber) reserved only for the specimen label keys, keeping the authentic VGA 16-color palette as the content layer. Typography stays monospace throughout—IBM Plex Mono or system fallbacks—with the chrome labels using small caps and tracking to evoke a lab-plate aesthetic, and the layout centers the screen with a thin bezel frame. Now I'm setting up the build pipeline: a Python script converts the extracted JSON data into web-ready JavaScript files that populate the house and palette data, then inlines everything into a playable HTML file. The engine itself models the screen as an 80×25 grid of cells, each holding a character and a PC attribute byte that drives the VGA palette lookup. The layout divides the screen into distinct zones—a status line at the top in inverse video, the map occupying rows 1–17, a separator and room name on row 18, and a six-line message area below—and rendering groups character runs by their attribute bytes into spans within a `<pre>` element. For the map itself, walls render as solid blocks using the current scheme's foreground color against its background, floor tiles stay empty to show the background, and doors appear as gaps (keeping the authentic look, though the exercise could have players make them visible). The player character draws as '@' in bright yellow or the scheme's bright foreground, positioned at the appropriate screen coordinates based on grid location. Movement works by checking if the target cell is walkable (value greater than zero), and when stepping onto a door or entering a new room, the game displays the appropriate entry description or room name. The front door is coded as 36 pointing to room 16 (the stoop), and the player starts in the highest-numbered room. Now I'm setting up the UI controls: Tab cycles between houses, C cycles color palettes, and ? redisplays the key bindings, with arrow key scrolling prevented. The message area shows the last six wrapped lines of text in rows 19-24, the status bar at the top displays the game title with current house and palette info plus movement controls, and the room name appears in uppercase on row 18. The data file will include metadata, room definitions with their descriptions and entry text, and the map grid, keeping the structure lean for this milestone. Milestones will extend the data generation later, but staying slim now keeps the playable file small and the learning curve manageable. The grid handles door codes 34 and 35 that connect to rooms 14 and 15, plus room 17, all covered by generic logic. When stepping through a door into a new room or spawning outdoors, I need to announce the room name. For the HTML, I'm creating a bezel container with a plate beneath it, listening for keyboard input on the window object rather than trying to manage focus on the screen element itself. Accessibility-wise, I'll add a simple aria-label to the pre element and set role="application", skipping animations since there aren't any to worry about. Then I'll write out the files and validate the JavaScript. Actually, I can structure this better by extracting the engine into a separate engine.js file alongside the data files, then write a single-file build that inlines everything. This way I can validate the engine with node and even run a quick headless test by stubbing the DOM methods—simulating moves and checking that room announcements fire correctly. I'll document the architecture and reading order in a README, keeping the engine itself around 250 lines with good comments.
Thinking · Thought process
I've settled on the design—an emulated 80×25 text-mode display using the actual attribute bytes from the BSAVE files, with authentic 1986 color schemes cycling on a key. Now I'll build the data converter to process the pipeline.
create_fileCreate the JSON-to-web data converter and single-file builder
📄 /home/claude/gen_web_data.py
python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
gen_web_data.py — Phase 2 build step: JSON content -> browser data files
=========================================================================

    python3 gen_web_data.py <extracted_dir> <web_dir>

Reads the Phase 1 JSON (content_v1.json / content_v2.json) and emits:

  web/data_v1.js, web/data_v2.js   — engine-consumable data modules
  web/sleuth_playable.html         — single-file build (engine + data
                                     inlined) that runs from file://
                                     with no server and no fetch()

Why .js data modules instead of fetch()ing the JSON directly?
Browsers block fetch() of local files (file:// CORS rules), so a
freshly-cloned project would need a web server just to start. A plain
<script> tag has no such restriction. We keep the engine/data split —
the same architecture Sleuth itself used — but package the data as
scripts so double-clicking index.html just works.

Milestone 1 only needs rooms + map, so that is all we emit. Later
milestones extend SLIM_KEYS (weapons, NPC actions, dialogue) — the
engine and this build step grow together.
"""

import json
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def slim_house(content: dict) -> dict:
    """Reduce a full content_vN.json to what the milestone-1 engine uses."""
    rooms = {}
    for rid, r in content["rooms"].items():
        rooms[rid] = {
            "id": r["id"],
            "name": r["name"],
            "description": r["description"],
            "entry_description": r.get("entry_description", ""),
        }
    return {
        "label": "House 1 — the Brisbane estate"
                 if content["meta"]["variant"] == "V1"
                 else "House 2 — the Donleavy mansion",
        "variant": content["meta"]["variant"],
        "rooms": rooms,
        "grid": content["map"]["cells"],     # 17 rows x 40 cols of ints
    }


def main(extracted_dir: str, web_dir: str) -> None:
    src, out = Path(extracted_dir), Path(web_dir)
    out.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    data_js = []
    for variant in ("v1", "v2"):
        content = json.loads((src / f"content_{variant}.json").read_text())
        house = slim_house(content)
        js = ("// generated by gen_web_data.py — do not edit by hand;\n"
              "// edit the JSON (or the original DOS files) and rebuild\n"
              "window.SLEUTH_HOUSES = window.SLEUTH_HOUSES || [];\n"
              f"window.SLEUTH_HOUSES.push({json.dumps(house, ensure_ascii=False)});\n")
        (out / f"data_{variant}.js").write_text(js)
        data_js.append(js)
        print(f"data_{variant}.js: {len(house['rooms'])} rooms, "
              f"{len(house['grid'])}x{len(house['grid'][0])} grid")

    # ---- single-file build: inline data + engine into one HTML ------
    html = (out / "index.html").read_text()
    engine = (out / "engine.js").read_text()
    inlined = html.replace(
        '<script src="data_v1.js"></script>\n'
        '<script src="data_v2.js"></script>\n'
        '<script src="engine.js"></script>',
        "<script>\n" + "\n".join(data_js) + "\n" + engine + "\n</script>")
    (out / "sleuth_playable.html").write_text(inlined)
    print("sleuth_playable.html: single-file build written")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 3:
        sys.exit(f"usage: {sys.argv[0]} <extracted_dir> <web_dir>")
    main(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2])
Output
File created successfully: /home/claude/gen_web_data.py

Now the engine itself — the file meant to be read top to bottom:

create_fileCreate the milestone-1 game engine JavaScript
📄 /home/claude/web/engine.js
javascript
/* =====================================================================
   SLEUTH remaster — engine.js — Phase 2, Milestone 1
   =====================================================================
   A walkable house: load a house from data, render it on an emulated
   DOS text-mode screen, move an @ around with collision and room/door
   detection.

   The architecture deliberately mirrors the 1983 original:

     data (window.SLEUTH_HOUSES)  ->  engine (this file)  ->  screen

   and the screen is modelled the way the real machine worked: an 80x25
   grid of (character, attribute) cells — a software copy of the text-
   mode video memory at B000h that Sleuth BLOADed its map into. We
   "render" by converting that buffer to HTML, but everything above the
   render function thinks in cells, rows and columns, exactly like the
   original program did.

   Reading order: SCREEN -> PALETTES -> MAP -> MESSAGES -> PLAYER ->
   INPUT -> INIT. Each section only uses things defined above it.
   ===================================================================== */

"use strict";

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   1. SCREEN — an emulated text-mode video buffer
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   80 columns x 25 rows. Each cell holds a character and an "attribute"
   byte in the original IBM PC format:

        bit:  7  6 5 4   3 2 1 0
              |  background  foreground
              blink (unused here)

   Foreground is 0-15, background 0-7, indexed into the classic
   16-colour palette below. Sleuth's data files store these exact
   bytes; we honour them. */

const COLS = 80, ROWS = 25;

const VGA = [  // the canonical 16-colour text-mode palette
  "#000000", "#0000aa", "#00aa00", "#00aaaa",
  "#aa0000", "#aa00aa", "#aa5500", "#aaaaaa",
  "#555555", "#5555ff", "#55ff55", "#55ffff",
  "#ff5555", "#ff55ff", "#ffff55", "#ffffff",
];

// the buffer: ROWS x COLS cells of {ch, attr}
const screen = [];
for (let r = 0; r < ROWS; r++) {
  screen.push(Array.from({ length: COLS }, () => ({ ch: " ", attr: 0x07 })));
}

function put(row, col, ch, attr) {
  if (row < 0 || row >= ROWS || col < 0 || col >= COLS) return;
  const cell = screen[row][col];
  cell.ch = ch;
  cell.attr = attr;
}

function print(row, col, text, attr) {
  for (let i = 0; i < text.length; i++) put(row, col + i, text[i], attr);
}

function clearRow(row, attr) {
  for (let c = 0; c < COLS; c++) put(row, c, " ", attr);
}

/* Convert the buffer to HTML. Adjacent cells sharing an attribute are
   grouped into one <span> — the only DOM-aware code in the engine. */
function render() {
  let html = "";
  for (let r = 0; r < ROWS; r++) {
    let runAttr = null, run = "";
    for (let c = 0; c < COLS; c++) {
      const { ch, attr } = screen[r][c];
      if (attr !== runAttr) {
        if (run) html += spanFor(run, runAttr);
        runAttr = attr; run = "";
      }
      run += ch;
    }
    html += spanFor(run, runAttr) + "\n";
  }
  document.getElementById("screen").innerHTML = html;
}

function spanFor(text, attr) {
  const fg = VGA[attr & 0x0f], bg = VGA[(attr >> 4) & 0x07];
  const safe = text.replace(/&/g, "&amp;").replace(/</g, "&lt;");
  return `<span style="color:${fg};background:${bg}">${safe}</span>`;
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   2. PALETTES — the five colour schemes recovered from SUSPICT.V0-V9
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   The original shipped its map screen in five attribute variants; these
   are the literal bytes found in those files. C cycles through them. */

const SCHEMES = [
  { name: "0x07 mono",            map: 0x07 },
  { name: "0x17 white on blue",   map: 0x17 },
  { name: "0x47 white on red",    map: 0x47 },
  { name: "0x57 white on magenta",map: 0x57 },
  { name: "0x67 white on brown",  map: 0x67 },
];

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   3. MAP — drawing a house grid onto the screen
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   The grid is 40x17 ints; one grid cell = TWO screen columns (40x2=80),
   the same trick the original used to get square-ish rooms out of tall
   text cells. Grid row r appears on screen row r+1 (row 0 is status).

     value 0      wall            -> solid blocks
     value 1..20  room floor      -> background colour
     value >20    door into room (value-20) -> a gap, just like the
                  original screens: doors are openings, not symbols. */

const MAP_TOP = 1;                       // screen row of grid row 0

const state = {
  houseIdx: 0,        // index into window.SLEUTH_HOUSES
  schemeIdx: 0,       // index into SCHEMES
  row: 0, col: 0,     // player position, in GRID coordinates
  roomId: null,       // room the player is currently in
  lastDoor: null,     // door cell we last announced (avoid repeats)
};

const house = () => window.SLEUTH_HOUSES[state.houseIdx];
const cellAt = (r, c) => {
  const g = house().grid;
  return (r >= 0 && r < g.length && c >= 0 && c < g[0].length) ? g[r][c] : 0;
};

function drawHouse() {
  const attr = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map;
  const g = house().grid;
  for (let r = 0; r < g.length; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < g[0].length; c++) {
      const ch = g[r][c] === 0 ? "\u2588" : " ";   // █ wall / open floor
      put(r + MAP_TOP, c * 2, ch, attr);
      put(r + MAP_TOP, c * 2 + 1, ch, attr);
    }
  }
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   4. MESSAGES — the text area under the map
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   Rows 19-24 hold the last six lines of narration, like the original's
   scrolling message region. say() wraps long text to the screen. */

const MSG_TOP = 19, MSG_ROWS = 6;
const log = [];

function wrap(text, width) {
  const words = text.split(/\s+/), lines = [];
  let line = "";
  for (const w of words) {
    if ((line + " " + w).trim().length > width) { lines.push(line); line = w; }
    else line = (line + " " + w).trim();
  }
  if (line) lines.push(line);
  return lines;
}

function say(text) {
  for (const line of wrap(text, COLS - 2)) {
    log.push(line);
    while (log.length > MSG_ROWS) log.shift();
  }
  for (let i = 0; i < MSG_ROWS; i++) {
    clearRow(MSG_TOP + i, 0x07);
    if (log[i]) print(MSG_TOP + i, 1, log[i], 0x07);
  }
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   5. PLAYER — movement, collision, room and door detection
   --------------------------------------------------------------------- */

function spawn() {
  // Start outdoors: the highest room id present in the grid is the
  // stoop (V1: 16) or the front lawn (V2: 17). Stand on its middle cell.
  const g = house().grid, cells = [];
  let startRoom = 0;
  for (const row of g) for (const v of row) if (v > startRoom && v <= 20) startRoom = v;
  for (let r = 0; r < g.length; r++)
    for (let c = 0; c < g[0].length; c++)
      if (g[r][c] === startRoom) cells.push([r, c]);
  [state.row, state.col] = cells[Math.floor(cells.length / 2)];
  state.roomId = null;
  state.lastDoor = null;
  log.length = 0;
  redraw();
  say("It is a dark and stormy night. A murder has been committed...");
  describeCell(true);
}

function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;
  if (cellAt(r, c) === 0) return;          // walls are walls
  state.row = r; state.col = c;
  redraw();
  describeCell(false);
}

function describeCell(force) {
  const v = cellAt(state.row, state.col);
  const rooms = house().rooms;

  if (v > 20) {                            // standing in a doorway
    const target = rooms[String(v - 20)];
    if (state.lastDoor !== `${state.row},${state.col}` && target) {
      say(target.entry_description ||
          `This is the entrance to the ${target.name}.`);
      state.lastDoor = `${state.row},${state.col}`;
    }
    return;
  }
  state.lastDoor = null;

  if (v !== state.roomId || force) {       // crossed into a new room
    state.roomId = v;
    const room = rooms[String(v)];
    if (room) say(room.description);
  }
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   6. CHROME — status line, room-name line, full redraw
   --------------------------------------------------------------------- */

function redraw() {
  // status line (inverse video, like a classic DOS status bar)
  clearRow(0, 0x70);
  print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \u2014 engine milestone 1", 0x70);
  print(0, 34, house().label, 0x70);
  const keys = "\u2190\u2191\u2193\u2192 move  TAB house  C colours";
  print(0, COLS - keys.length - 1, keys, 0x70);

  drawHouse();

  // the player. Bright yellow on the scheme's background reads on all
  // five schemes — and yes, you are the @: this predates roguelike
  // convention being a convention.
  const bg = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map & 0x70;
  put(state.row + MAP_TOP, state.col * 2, "@", bg | 0x0e);

  // room-name line between map and messages
  clearRow(18, 0x07);
  const room = house().rooms[String(state.roomId)];
  const label = room ? room.name.toUpperCase()
                     : SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].name;
  print(18, 1, label, 0x0f);

  // re-paint the message log (drawHouse never touches it, but a full
  // redraw after house/palette switches needs it back)
  say("");                                  // re-renders log rows
  render();
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   7. INPUT + INIT
   --------------------------------------------------------------------- */

window.addEventListener("keydown", (e) => {
  const k = e.key;
  if (k === "ArrowUp")        { tryMove(-1, 0); }
  else if (k === "ArrowDown") { tryMove(1, 0); }
  else if (k === "ArrowLeft") { tryMove(0, -1); }
  else if (k === "ArrowRight"){ tryMove(0, 1); }
  else if (k === "Tab") {
    state.houseIdx = (state.houseIdx + 1) % window.SLEUTH_HOUSES.length;
    spawn();
  }
  else if (k === "c" || k === "C") {
    state.schemeIdx = (state.schemeIdx + 1) % SCHEMES.length;
    redraw();
  }
  else return;                              // not ours — let it through
  e.preventDefault();                       // ours — stop page scrolling
});

spawn();
Output
File created successfully: /home/claude/web/engine.js
create_fileCreate the index.html shell with the workshop chrome
📄 /home/claude/web/index.html
html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8">
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
<title>Sleuth — engine milestone 1</title>
<style>
  /* Chrome tokens: quiet workshop framing around the artifact.
     The screen's own colours come from authentic attribute bytes
     in engine.js — nothing here restyles game content. */
  :root {
    --bench: #16181d;       /* room the machine sits in            */
    --bezel: #23262e;       /* monitor housing                     */
    --bezel-edge: #2e323c;
    --ink: #c8ccd6;         /* plate engraving                     */
    --ink-dim: #7a8090;
    --amber: #e0a458;       /* one accent: indicator-lamp amber    */
  }
  * { box-sizing: border-box; }
  html, body { height: 100%; }
  body {
    margin: 0;
    background: var(--bench);
    color: var(--ink);
    font-family: ui-monospace, "Cascadia Mono", Consolas, Menlo, monospace;
    display: flex; flex-direction: column;
    align-items: center; justify-content: center;
    gap: 14px; padding: 16px;
  }
  .bezel {
    background: var(--bezel);
    border: 1px solid var(--bezel-edge);
    border-radius: 10px;
    padding: 18px 20px 16px;
    box-shadow: 0 18px 50px rgba(0,0,0,.5), inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,.04);
  }
  /* The screen: 80x25 cells. Font size scales with viewport so the
     whole display always fits; the pre stays exactly 80ch wide. */
  #screen {
    margin: 0;
    font-family: inherit;
    font-size: clamp(8px, 1.12vw, 15px);
    line-height: 1.18;
    letter-spacing: 0;
    background: #000;
    padding: 8px 10px;
    border-radius: 4px;
    box-shadow: inset 0 0 24px rgba(0,0,0,.85);
    white-space: pre;
  }
  /* engraved plate beneath the monitor */
  .plate {
    display: flex; gap: 1.6em; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center;
    font-size: 11px; letter-spacing: .14em; text-transform: uppercase;
    color: var(--ink-dim);
  }
  .plate b { color: var(--amber); font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: .14em; }
  .plate span::before { content: "\2022\00a0\00a0"; color: var(--bezel-edge); }
  .plate span:first-child::before { content: ""; }
  @media (max-width: 720px) {
    .plate { font-size: 10px; gap: 1em; }
  }
</style>
</head>
<body>
  <div class="bezel"><pre id="screen" aria-label="Sleuth game screen"></pre></div>
  <div class="plate">
    <span><b>Sleuth</b> 1983 / remaster engine</span>
    <span>milestone 1 — a walkable house</span>
    <span>palettes recovered from <b>SUSPICT.V0–V9</b></span>
  </div>
<script src="data_v1.js"></script>
<script src="data_v2.js"></script>
<script src="engine.js"></script>
</body>
</html>

Thinking · Architected headless engine testing strategy with stubs and assertions.
I should set up a headless test of the engine logic in Node, stubbing out the DOM elements and then running through the core mechanics—loading data, simulating key inputs, and verifying that room transitions and rendering work as expected.

Syntax is clean. Now a headless smoke test — stub the DOM in Node, simulate a walk, and verify collision, room announcements, and door behavior actually work:

bash_toolHeadless smoke test of engine logic with DOM stub
bash
cd /home/claude/web && node << 'EOF'
// --- minimal DOM/browser stub ---
const handlers = {};
global.window = {
  SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; },
};
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };

// load data + engine the way the browser would
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_v1.js','data_v2.js']) {
  // data files reference window.SLEUTH_HOUSES
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.'));
}
const engineSrc = fs.readFileSync('engine.js','utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.');
eval(engineSrc);

const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const plain = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'');

// --- assertions ---
const assert = (cond, msg) => { if(!cond){ console.error("FAIL:", msg); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:", msg); };

assert(global.window.SLEUTH_HOUSES.length === 2, "two houses loaded");
assert(plain().includes("@"), "player rendered");
assert(plain().includes("dark and stormy night"), "intro narration shown");
assert(plain().includes("front stoop") || plain().includes("FRONT STOOP"), "spawned outdoors (V1 stoop)");

// V1: stoop is at grid rows ~16; front door (36) is above. Walk up through it.
let before = plain();
for (let i=0;i<3;i++) key("ArrowUp");
assert(plain().includes("front door") || plain().includes("MAIN HALL") || plain().includes("main hall"),
  "walked through front door toward the main hall");
for (let i=0;i<3;i++) key("ArrowUp");
assert(plain().toLowerCase().includes("main hall"), "now in the main hall, description shown");

// try walking into a wall repeatedly: position must clamp (no crash, no escape)
for (let i=0;i<30;i++) key("ArrowLeft");
assert(plain().includes("@"), "player still on screen after wall-bashing");

// walk into the dining room via its door (move up the hall then left)
for (let i=0;i<10;i++) key("ArrowUp");
for (let i=0;i<6;i++) key("ArrowLeft");
console.log("current location line:", plain().split("\n")[18].trim());

// palette cycle
key("c"); key("c");
assert(plain().length > 0, "palette cycle renders");

// house switch
key("Tab");
assert(plain().includes("Donleavy"), "switched to house 2");
assert(plain().toLowerCase().includes("front lawn"), "spawned on V2 front lawn");
key("Tab");
assert(plain().includes("Brisbane"), "switched back to house 1");
console.log("\nALL SMOKE TESTS PASSED");
EOF
Output
ok: two houses loaded
ok: player rendered
stderr
FAIL: intro narration shown
exit 1
Thinking · Verifying grid coordinates and door placement logic.
I'm tracking down a rendering bug where the intro narration isn't displaying. The issue is that `spawn()` calls `redraw()` which triggers `say("")` to clear the log, then calls `render()` to convert it to HTML. But then `spawn()` calls `say(intro)` and `describeCell(true)` without calling `render()` again afterward, so the narration text stays in the buffer and never gets painted to the screen. The fix is to make `say()` automatically trigger a render, or ensure the calling paths end with `render()`. I'm also checking edge cases like what happens when `say("")` wraps empty text—it should just push nothing to the lines array and run the repaint loop cleanly. And I'm verifying the door logic: the player spawns at the middle of the stoop cells (row 16, columns 10-12, so position 16,11), and moving up should land on the front door at (15,11) which maps to room entry "You are standing at the front door." I'm double-checking the grid layout to make sure the coordinates line up correctly. So moving up from the stoop at (16,11) takes me through (15,11) which is the front door, then up to (14,11) in the main hall—all walkable. Now I need to figure out how to get from the hall into the dining room, which has its door at position 21 around (2,8). I'm tracing through the actual render output from the original map to verify the exact cell contents at row 12, checking what characters appear in each column position to understand the layout boundaries. So moving up from row 14 takes me to around row 4, and then moving left from there gets blocked by a wall at column 8, keeping me at column 9 in the main hall. The location text prints correctly. I'm also checking that the first upward movement lands on the front door with the right entry message, and I need to make sure the say() function actually renders before redraw() is called.
Thinking · Debugging text truncation issues in display rendering.
Found the issue — `say()` writes to the buffer but doesn't call `render()`, so text stays invisible after the initial draw. I'll fix that now.
str_replaceMake say() present the buffer after painting message rows
📄 /home/claude/web/engine.js
Replace
javascript
function say(text) {
  for (const line of wrap(text, COLS - 2)) {
    log.push(line);
    while (log.length > MSG_ROWS) log.shift();
  }
  for (let i = 0; i < MSG_ROWS; i++) {
    clearRow(MSG_TOP + i, 0x07);
    if (log[i]) print(MSG_TOP + i, 1, log[i], 0x07);
  }
}
With
javascript
function say(text) {
  for (const line of wrap(text, COLS - 2)) {
    log.push(line);
    while (log.length > MSG_ROWS) log.shift();
  }
  for (let i = 0; i < MSG_ROWS; i++) {
    clearRow(MSG_TOP + i, 0x07);
    if (log[i]) print(MSG_TOP + i, 1, log[i], 0x07);
  }
  render();   // painting the buffer isn't showing it — present it too
}
Output
Successfully replaced string in /home/claude/web/engine.js

assert(plain().includes("Brisbane"), "back to house 1");

Thinking · Navigating door mechanics and restructuring status line layout.
The V2 house renders correctly and the front lawn spawn works, but there's a text overlap issue in the status line—the "Donleavy mansion" label at column 34 is getting overwritten by the keys string that starts at column 48, since the keys string is 31 characters long and extends past where the label ends. I'm working through layout options: shortening the title to "SLEUTH · milestone 1" (20 chars instead of 28) frees up space, then repositioning the house label to start at column 24 with a max width constraint, and trimming the keys string to "←↑↓→ TAB house C colours" (24 chars) so it starts at column 55—this should eliminate the overlap while keeping everything readable. There's also a separate issue with the room-name display: when stepping onto a door, the label still shows the previous room because the roomId doesn't change on the door tile itself, but the entry text needs to update. I need to adjust the test sequence: after moving up, I'll go down twice to reach row 2, then move left to hit the door. I'll also fix the status layout and run the test again.

Thinking · Debugged rendering order and layout spacing conflicts.
The dump shows the @ symbol is in the dining room but the room name still displays as "MAIN HALL" — that's because redraw() runs before describeCell() in tryMove, so the room-name line gets painted using the old state.roomId before describeCell updates it. I need to flip the order: call describeCell first to update the state, then redraw to paint everything with the correct values. There's a minor rendering artifact where say() inside describeCell creates an intermediate frame before redraw renders the final one, but it's imperceptible and acceptable for now. The door label showing the previous room while standing in a doorway is also fine. For the status line, the label and keys are overlapping — "Brisbane estate" ends at column 48 but the keys start at 46. I need to trim the keys string to fit within 30 characters so it starts at column 51 instead, which gives enough clearance.
Thinking · Thought process
I found two bugs in the walkthrough: the room name lags one move behind due to an ordering issue in the redraw logic, and the status line still has a 3-column collision. I'll fix both now.
bash_toolFix ordering and status budget, rebuild, final smoke test
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
# fix 1: update game state BEFORE painting the frame that displays it
src = src.replace('''  state.row = r; state.col = c;
  redraw();
  describeCell(false);''',
'''  state.row = r; state.col = c;
  describeCell(false);   // update roomId/narration FIRST...
  redraw();              // ...so this frame paints the new state''')
# fix 2: keys legend within the 80-col budget (label ends col 48, keys start col 51)
src = src.replace('''  const keys = "\\u2190\\u2191\\u2193\\u2192 move \\u00b7 TAB house \\u00b7 C colours";''',
'''  const keys = "\\u2190\\u2191\\u2193\\u2192 \\u00b7 TAB house \\u00b7 C colours";''')
open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("patched")
EOF

let lastHTML = "";

const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const plain = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'');
const assert = (cond, msg) => { if(!cond){ console.error("FAIL:", msg); console.log(plain()); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:", msg); };

assert(plain().includes("dark and stormy night"), "intro narration");
key("ArrowUp");
assert(plain().includes("standing at the front door"), "front-door entry text");
key("ArrowUp"); key("ArrowUp");
assert(plain().toLowerCase().includes("main hall"), "main hall described");
for (let i=0;i<14;i++) key("ArrowUp");
key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowDown");
key("ArrowLeft"); key("ArrowLeft"); key("ArrowLeft");
assert(plain().includes("doorway leads into the dining room"), "dining doorway text");
key("ArrowLeft");
assert(plain().split("\n")[18].includes("DINING ROOM"), "room-name line updated same frame");
const status = plain().split("\n")[0];
assert(status.includes("Brisbane estate") && status.includes("C colours"), "status line fits 80 cols");
key("c"); key("Tab");
assert(plain().split("\n")[0].includes("Donleavy mansion"), "house 2 label");
assert(plain().toLowerCase().includes("front lawn"), "V2 lawn spawn");
key("Tab");
console.log("\nALL SMOKE TESTS PASSED");
console.log(plain().split("\n")[0]);
EOF

create_fileWrite the Phase 2 learning notes and roadmap
📄 /home/claude/web/README_PHASE2.md
markdown
# SLEUTH Remaster — Phase 2, Milestone 1: A Walkable House

You can now walk around both original houses in a browser, with the
authentic 1983 floor plans, room descriptions, doorway texts, and the
five color schemes recovered from the SUSPICT BSAVE files.

## Running it

Two ways:

- **Instant:** open `sleuth_playable.html`. Engine + data inlined in
  one file; works from a double-click, no server.
- **The project:** open `index.html` in the same folder as `engine.js`,
  `data_v1.js`, `data_v2.js`. This is the version you'll edit. It also
  works from file://, because the data ships as `<script>` modules
  instead of fetch()ed JSON — browsers block fetch() of local files,
  and that design choice is explained at the top of `gen_web_data.py`.

Keys: **arrows** move, **Tab** switches house, **C** cycles the five
original palettes.

## The pipeline so far

```
original DOS files ──▶ extract_sleuth.py ──▶ content_*.json   (Phase 1)
content_*.json     ──▶ gen_web_data.py   ──▶ data_*.js + playable build
data_*.js          ──▶ engine.js         ──▶ pixels            (Phase 2)
```

Every arrow is a file you can read, run, and re-run. Change a room
description in the JSON, run `python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web`,
refresh the browser: that's the whole modding loop already working —
the grown-up version of editing DESCR.V1 in a text editor.

## How to read engine.js (top to bottom, ~250 lines)

The file is ordered so each section only uses what's above it:

1. **SCREEN** — the heart of the design. We don't draw "a game UI"; we
   emulate an 80×25 text-mode video buffer of (character, attribute)
   cells, the same model as the real video memory at B000h that Sleuth
   BLOADed its screens into. `put()` and `print()` write cells;
   `render()` is the *only* function that knows the DOM exists.
   That separation is why the engine was testable headlessly in Node
   with a five-line DOM stub before a browser ever opened it.
2. **PALETTES** — five attribute bytes, copied verbatim from
   SUSPICT.V0–V9. Archaeology made visible.
3. **MAP** — the 40×17 grid, one cell = two screen columns (40×2 = 80,
   the original's trick for square-ish rooms). Walls are `0`, floors
   are room IDs, doors are `room + 20` and render as gaps in walls —
   exactly like the original screens.
4. **MESSAGES** — `wrap()` + `say()`, a six-line scrolling narration
   region like the original's.
5. **PLAYER** — `spawn()` (start outdoors: highest room ID = stoop or
   lawn), `tryMove()` (collision = "is the target cell 0?"), and
   `describeCell()` (door cells show entry text once; crossing into a
   new room ID announces its description).
6. **CHROME** — status line, room-name line, `redraw()`.
7. **INPUT + INIT** — one keydown listener, then `spawn()`.

## Two bugs worth studying (they're in this code's history)

These were caught by the headless smoke test, and both are classic:

- **Paint vs. present.** `say()` wrote text into the screen buffer but
  never called `render()`, so narration silently didn't appear. Any
  system with a buffer/display split (game engines, double-buffered
  GUIs, even `printf` without `\n` flushing) has this failure mode.
- **State before paint.** `tryMove()` originally redrew the frame and
  *then* updated which room you're in, so the room-name line lagged
  one step behind reality. Update state first, then render state.

## Exercises (roughly in order of difficulty)

1. Change the player glyph and color (one line in `redraw()`).
2. Make doors visible — render door cells as `▒` instead of a gap.
   Then decide: is it better gameplay? The original said no.
3. Show the room name *of the doorway's target* on the room-name line
   while standing in a doorway.
4. Add a sixth palette of your own design to `SCHEMES`.
5. Track visited rooms and dim unvisited ones until first entry —
   fog-of-war the 1983 hardware couldn't afford.
6. Implement the secret panel: the passageway (room 2) has no door in
   either layout. Pick a wall cell adjacent to it and let walking into
   that wall, only there, step through — then print the original's
   line: "You have stepped through a secret panel in the wall."
7. Harder: load `content_shared.json`'s verb table and add a one-line
   text input under the screen that recognizes EXAMINE/LOOK AT.
   (This is the bridge to Milestone 2.)

## Roadmap

- **M2 — Things:** scatter weapons from `weapons[]` using the per-room
  anchors; EXAMINE/GET/DROP; the magnifying glass gate.
- **M3 — People:** place the six suspects; idle actions from
  `npc_actions`; movement between rooms on a timer.
- **M4 — The mystery:** the generator — pick victim/murderer/room/
  weapon; build consistent alibis with exactly one contradiction;
  QUESTION and ALIBI dialogue from the framing + template pools.
- **M5 — Endgame:** GATHER, ACCUSE, the suspicion timer, win/lose,
  ranks (Apprentice / Professional / Holmesian).
- **M6 — Your game:** the custom-content layer — new houses as JSON,
  new casts, new weapons. The original engine read everything from
  files; ours will too, so this is mostly *documentation*, not code.
Output
File created successfully: /home/claude/web/README_PHASE2.md

cd /home/claude && cp web/sleuth_playable.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/ && cp gen_web_data.py web/ && cd web && zip -q /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m1.zip index.html engine.js data_v1.js data_v2.js gen_web_data.py README_PHASE2.md sleuth_playable.html && unzip -l /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m1.zip

Output
[local_resource block]
[local_resource block]

Milestone 1 is done — the session limit hit right after the final smoke test passed, so all that remained was packaging.

sleuth_playable.html — open it and you're standing on the Brisbane estate's front stoop on a dark and stormy night. Arrow keys to move, Tab to switch to the Donleavy mansion, C to cycle the five color schemes — and those aren't an aesthetic choice of mine, they're the literal attribute bytes from SUSPICT.V0–V9, so what you see in "white on blue" is what someone saw on a CGA monitor in 1986. Walk up through the front door and the original room descriptions fire as you cross thresholds.

sleuth_engine_m1.zip — the actual project: index.html + engine.js + data_v1.js/data_v2.js + gen_web_data.py + README_PHASE2.md. The engine is ~250 commented lines ordered for top-to-bottom reading, built around one idea worth internalizing: it doesn't draw "a UI," it emulates an 80×25 text-mode video buffer — a software copy of the B000h memory the original BLOADed into. render() is the only function that knows the DOM exists, which is exactly why I could test the whole thing headlessly in Node with a five-line DOM stub before any browser opened it.

The README documents two bugs the smoke tests caught, because they're genuinely instructive: say() wrote narration into the buffer but never presented it (the paint-vs-present failure mode that haunts every double-buffered system), and tryMove() originally painted the frame before updating which room you were in, so the room-name line lagged one step behind reality. State first, then render — you'll meet both of these again in your own debugging.

The modding loop you had as a teenager is also already back, upgraded: edit a description in content_v1.json, run python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web, refresh. The README closes with graded exercises — the best one is implementing the secret panel, since the passageway genuinely has no door in either layout's data and the rule lives in engine logic, exactly where the original kept it.

When you're ready for Milestone 2, we scatter the 33 weapons through the rooms using those per-room anchor coordinates from RMDEF's tail, and add EXAMINE/GET/DROP with the magnifying-glass gate — which means wiring up the first piece of the parser. That's where it starts feeling like Sleuth.

2026-06-12 10:46
You

Nice. I've saved everything for now. I'll have to work through the exercises later. Proceed with milestone 2.

2026-06-13 01:45
Claude
Thinking · Architecting object placement, inventory mechanics, and magnifying glass functionality.
For the next milestone, I need to extend the data generation to emit weapons and parser vocabulary from the shared content, plus room examinables so the EXAMINE command works properly. Then I'm adding a text input system to the engine alongside the existing movement controls, and figuring out how to place a subset of objects throughout the rooms using per-room anchor points—plus implementing the magnifying glass gate mechanic. I'm checking whether the room anchors are already in the content JSON (they are, under `map.room_anchors` as screen coordinates), so I'll need to include those in the web data output. For display, I'm deciding whether to show objects as text descriptions when entering a room or draw small markers at the anchor positions—the original game described objects in scene text rather than drawing them on the map, but using the anchors to place visual markers could be a nice touch for the web version. Now I'm converting the anchor coordinates from the original screen format (where the map started at row 2) to our 0-indexed system. I also need to pull authentic messages from the extracted engine strings for actions like take, drop, examine, and the magnifying glass mechanic—where examining objects without the glass gives a generic response, but with it reveals blood on the murder weapon. For the parser, I'm wiring up the SUNDRIES verb table from the content files: EXAMINE/LOOK/SEARCH map to action 1, GET/TAKE/PICK to action 3, DROP to action 8, plus the room examinables already stored in content_vN.json so that examining specific objects like the silver candlestick returns the right response. The input UI is next. es; arrows still move regardless. When the command buffer is empty, Tab and C trigger shortcuts, but once typing starts, letter keys feed into the buffer and C just types 'c'. I'll always display the "> _" prompt at row 24, shrinking the message area to rows 19-23. Now I need to handle the data plumbing: gen_web_data has to emit weapons with their scene text, display names, and keywords, plus room examinables and keywords, map anchors, and a shared parser vocabulary of verbs and nouns with responses, then update the build to include data_shared.js. For object placement, I'm working through how 33 weapons distribute across 11 rooms—the original used a room_order_table permutation, but I'm thinking the weapons might be grouped as 3 candidates per room based on their sequence in the file, with items like the candelabra in the dining room, the leg of lamb in the kitchen, and so on. So each room 1-11 has exactly one weapon in the first set, arranged in order. For the remaining two sets, I need to randomly distribute three candidates per room. The magnifying glass is the question mark — I need to verify whether it's part of the 33 weapons or if the engine has it separately. Let me search the engine strings for references to the magnifying glass and blood traces to understand how it's handled, then extend the smoke test to cover examining objects with and without the glass, getting items from wrong rooms, and carrying the candelabra. I'll need to figure out whether to seed the RNG for deterministic placement during testing. For scope, I'm keeping this milestone focused on objects, the parser, the glass mechanic, and the blood reveal—suspects and alibis can wait for later milestones. I'm also considering whether to draw subtle markers at object anchor positions in rooms, though that might clutter the display, so I'll keep it minimal for now. On the technical side, I need to handle the coordinate conversion between the original screen layout and the grid system: the anchors are stored as 1-indexed screen coordinates, so I'm converting them to 0-indexed grid positions using the formula grid_row = anchor_row - 2 and grid_col = (anchor_col - 1) / 2. I'm checking which JSON file contains the room anchors (should be a list of 16 coordinate pairs in content_vN.json), then I'll update gen_web_data.py to convert these anchors and integrate the weapons list, examinables, and room keys properly. For the engine itself, I need to add the RNG system with a seeded random generator, manage game state for object locations and what's being carried, implement the scatter function to place items, draw markers on the house visualization, build a command-line parser that matches verbs by prefix and nouns by context (weapons, room-specific examinables, generic items), handle actions like examine/get/drop/help, and display scene text when entering rooms. I'm also uncertain about the original inventory limit—whether you could only carry one item total or one weapon at a time—so I'll keep it simple and aligned with the original Sleuth mechanics. Now I need to check the extracted strings and weapon list to confirm the inventory behavior, then rebuild with headless tests using the seeded RNG to verify everything works before packaging.

Milestone 2: things you can examine, take, and drop — plus the magnifying glass gate. Before writing any code, let me consult the recovered engine strings so the new mechanics use authentic messages, and verify my hypothesis about how the 33 weapons map to rooms:

import json
w = json.load(open('extracted/content_v1.json'))['weapons']
for i, x in enumerate(w):
    print(f'{i:2d} room{i%11+1:2d} set{i//11}  {x[\"display_name\"]}')"

Thinking · Pinpointing carry mechanics and examining object interaction rules.
The pattern is elegant: 33 weapons organized as 3 sets across 11 rooms, with each item perfectly matched to its location—the kitchen has practical tools like a bottle opener and leg of lamb, the pantry holds delicacies like caviar and salmon, the conservatory features musical instruments and composers' names, and even the secret passage gets a thematically appropriate blunt instrument. Eric Miller clearly had fun with the thematic consistency. Now I'm recovering the actual game strings that drive the mechanics: the magnifying glass is essential for identifying the murder weapon through bloodstains, and the game tracks whether you're carrying it, the weapon itself, or neither. The murder room reveals itself through visible bloodstains on the floor when you enter, and examining objects without the magnifying glass gives you generic responses, but with it you can spot the telltale blood traces that confirm the weapon. The game also prevents you from dropping the magnifying glass and blocks further investigation once you've already found the murder weapon.
Thinking · Reconstructing authentic game rules for milestone two.
I've found the actual carry mechanic — you transport the magnifying glass and murder weapon, with bloodstains marking the crime scene. Now I'll pull the full message context to reconstruct the complete rules.

cd /home/claude && sed -n '80,125p' extracted/engine_strings_sleuth.txt
