Can you look into that?

Then proceed with milestone 3 too.

2026-07-02 02:02
Claude
Thinking · Deciphering staircase teleportation mechanics and landing triggers.
The user spotted a real issue — the staircases in the current version don't actually connect the two floors. Looking back at V1, the map has stairs positioned at the bottom middle (rooms 12 and 13) with a lower staircase and upper staircase side by side, each with their own landing and door codes. The ground floor occupies the left half while the upper floor is on the right, and these staircases bridge them together at the bottom. Let me examine the V1 map layout more carefully. I'm seeing the lower staircase (room 12) positioned around column 9 in rows 12-14, with its landing (code 32) at row 15, column 9. The upper staircase (room 13) sits around column 31 in the same rows, with landing 33 at row 15, column 31. The original design uses a clever mechanic where the two staircases are actually the same staircase in the fiction — walking down the lower staircase on the ground floor side puts you at the upper staircase on the upstairs side, creating the floor transition. The landings at row 15 are the key: stepping onto landing 32 (bottom of the lower staircase) teleports you to landing 33 (bottom of the upper staircase), and vice versa. This fits the "change direction" constraint perfectly — you're heading downward when you hit the landing, and after the teleport you must head upward to climb into the upstairs hall. The landing cells themselves are the trigger points for this transition. Looking at the room layout from the test dump, the main hall spans across the middle section of the grid while the upstairs hall is positioned further to the right, and I'm trying to pin down the exact column positions for each room to understand the spatial structure properly. The original movement system worked by having the player walk down the lower staircase to its landing at row 15, where the engine would then teleport them to the bottom of the upper staircase so they could walk up into the upstairs hall—effectively changing direction at each transition. Now I'm examining where exactly that teleport happens and how the staircase shafts connect to the main hall. I'm noticing the RMDEF aux pairs might actually encode the teleport endpoints themselves. Looking at the undecoded pairs like (16,18) and (16,62), screen row 16 maps to grid row 14, and the column calculations suggest these could be destination coordinates—though the math isn't quite clean yet, possibly off by some conversion factor. The pair (17,22) appearing twice is interesting, especially since screen row 17 corresponds to the landing row, so these might be marking the exact teleport locations. Rather than getting lost in the coordinate decoding, I should focus on implementing the core mechanic: stepping onto landing cell 32 teleports to landing cell 33's position and vice versa. To understand why the user couldn't reach the second floor, I need to check the actual V1/V2 grids and verify connectivity—specifically whether the upper staircase shaft connects to the upstairs hall, and whether both shafts are dead-ends at the row 15 landings with no passage between them. The teleport is the missing link that bridges those disconnected landings. In the current implementation, stepping on codes 32/33 just displays the staircase entry descriptions without actually teleporting. I need to check V2's door list to see if codes 34/35 (doors to halls 14/15) also function as teleports, since V2's doors don't include 32/33. Let me examine both grids programmatically to map out all cells with codes above 20, trace their connectivity with flood fill, and identify which door code pairs actually connect the floors. Let me flood-fill both house layouts to find what's actually unreachable, then use the data to identify the bridging mechanism—likely the landing 32 ↔ 33 teleport in V1, or possibly a different pairing in V2 involving the door codes or stair shaft connections. Once I've confirmed how the two halves connect, I'll move into Milestone 3: placing the six suspects in rooms, setting up their idle actions and movement patterns on the turn clock, and implementing the KNOCK and LIST verbs. For the suspect rendering, I'm trying to figure out the anchor system—the original game drew suspects on the map, and those first 16 anchor pairs likely correspond to one position per room, but with multiple suspects potentially occupying the same room, the additional 37 pairs might represent second and third occupant slots. The grouping doesn't quite add up (16 + 18 + 19 ≠ 48), but that sentinel (0,0) at pair 16 suggests the room 16 stoop is unused, so maybe the pairing structure is different than I initially thought. r" — so the format varies. I need to check the actual ACT strings to see if they're complete sentences or fragments. For M3, I'll go with: when entering a room, display each suspect's name paired with their action string, formatted as "FirstName [action]" where the action comes directly from the ACT data. On the map itself, show just their first initial at the room anchor position. For movement, I'm setting up a turn clock where each player action advances time by one turn. Each turn, suspects have a chance to move to an adjacent room through the door network. I'll derive the room adjacency graph from the grid layout by examining which rooms connect via door cells, then use that to implement a simple random walk where suspects teleport to a connected room with some probability per turn. Stairs between floors work the same way — suspects can move between the first and second floors as part of their random movement. Now I need to pick one of the seven cast members as the victim and have the remaining six wander around. The intro text should announce the murder with the exact wording from the original game. I'm also implementing the LIST command to show the suspect names, the KNOCK command for door interactions, and selecting the murderer at the same time during the initial scatter phase. When suspects move between rooms, I'll display brief messages like "X leaves the room" or "X enters the room" when they transition in or out of the player's current location. The QUESTION command will remain stubbed for now but should eventually indicate whether a suspect is present in the room. I need to check the original engine strings for the exact murder intro text, KNOCK responses, LIST output, and how suspects gather, then implement those. I should also look into fixing the staircase issue in the M3 build that the user mentioned. My plan is to first inspect the grid door positions and flood-fill reachability to confirm the disconnect and find the pairing rule for the two victim rooms, then implement the stairs teleport with tests. After that, I'll search the strings for M3 messages, extend the web data generator to include character data and NPC actions (since slim_house currently drops those), and finally build out the engine's M3 logic with cast placement, random walks on the turn clock, room adjacency tracking, occupant rendering with initials, entry occupant listing with actions, and the LIST and KNOCK verbs.

Ha — death by quail. Very Sleuth. And good catch on the stairs: let me investigate how the original actually connected the floors before I guess. Flood-fill both grids from the spawn point and see what's reachable and where the door codes sit:

bash_toolFlood-fill both houses to map the staircase disconnect
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import json
for variant in ('v1','v2'):
    c = json.load(open(f'extracted/content_{variant}.json'))
    g = c['map']['cells']; H, W = len(g), len(g[0])
    rooms = {int(k): v['name'] for k,v in c['rooms'].items()}
    # door/special cells
    print(f"===== {variant.upper()} special cells (>20):")
    for r in range(H):
        for col in range(W):
            v = g[r][col]
            if v > 20:
                print(f"  code {v} at grid ({r},{col}) -> room {v-20} = {rooms.get(v-20,'?')}")
    # flood fill from spawn (highest room id)
    start_room = max(v for row in g for v in row if 0 < v <= 20)
    seen = set()
    stack = [(r,c2) for r in range(H) for c2 in range(W) if g[r][c2]==start_room][:1]
    seen.add(stack[0])
    while stack:
        r,c2 = stack.pop()
        for dr,dc in ((1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)):
            nr,nc = r+dr,c2+dc
            if 0<=nr<H and 0<=nc<W and (nr,nc) not in seen and g[nr][nc]>0:
                seen.add((nr,nc)); stack.append((nr,nc))
    reached_rooms = sorted({g[r][c2] for r,c2 in seen if 0<g[r][c2]<=20})
    all_rooms = sorted({v for row in g for v in row if 0<v<=20})
    unreachable = [x for x in all_rooms if x not in reached_rooms]
    print(f"  reachable rooms: {reached_rooms}")
    print(f"  UNREACHABLE: {unreachable} = {[rooms.get(x) for x in unreachable]}")
EOF

bash_toolFlood-fill both houses to find the staircase disconnect
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import json
for variant in ('v1','v2'):
    c = json.load(open(f'extracted/content_{variant}.json'))
    g = c['map']['cells']; H, W = len(g), len(g[0])
    rooms = {int(k): v['name'] for k,v in c['rooms'].items()}
    print(f"===== {variant.upper()} special cells (>20):")
    for r in range(H):
        for col in range(W):
            v = g[r][col]
            if v > 20:
                print(f"  code {v} at grid ({r},{col}) -> room {v-20} = {rooms.get(v-20,'?')}")
    start_room = max(v for row in g for v in row if 0 < v <= 20)
    cells = [(r,c2) for r in range(H) for c2 in range(W) if g[r][c2]==start_room]
    seen = {cells[0]}; stack = [cells[0]]
    while stack:
        r,c2 = stack.pop()
        for dr,dc in ((1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)):
            nr,nc = r+dr,c2+dc
            if 0<=nr<H and 0<=nc<W and (nr,nc) not in seen and g[nr][nc]>0:
                seen.add((nr,nc)); stack.append((nr,nc))
    reached = sorted({g[r][c2] for r,c2 in seen if 0<g[r][c2]<=20})
    allr = sorted({v for row in g for v in row if 0<v<=20})
    unreachable = [x for x in allr if x not in reached]
    print(f"  reachable rooms: {reached}")
    print(f"  UNREACHABLE: {unreachable} = {[rooms.get(x) for x in unreachable]}")
EOF

Thinking · Deciphering secret panel mechanics and teleport triggers.
So the layout becomes clear: V1 has the ground floor and main hall accessible, but everything upstairs is blocked off. The two staircases—lower and upper—are completely separate shafts, but they're connected through a teleport pair at their respective landings, so descending the lower stairs deposits you at the upper staircase's landing, letting you walk up into the upstairs area. V2 is different—the floors are already fully connected. The east and west halls link through the lawn via doors 34 and 35, and the stairs themselves are adjacent-connected, so you can walk between wings naturally. The teleport rule only kicks in where those paired landing doors exist. The secret passage in V1 remains unreachable as expected, and the secret panel is still engine logic for later. But room 2 itself is one of the 11 named rooms with weapons, so the passage must be enterable eventually. The entry description "You have stepped through a secret panel in the wall" is tied to room 2's doors section, confirming there's a secret panel mechanic. Looking at the grid coordinates and screen positions, the passage sits around row 7, columns 1-7, but the exact panel location isn't obvious from the aux pairs—it might be approached from the dining room above or the living room below. The original manual probably explains how to trigger it, though I'm fuzzy on whether you just walk into the wall or if there's a specific interaction. For the M3 implementation, I'll treat the secret panel as a data-informed guess: any wall cell directly bordering room 2 could serve as the entry point when the player moves into it from an adjacent room. to the passage). So suspects can move through the passage via the secret panel in the fiction, and I need to wire that into the movement graph for NPC pathfinding while also making the player-facing panel mechanic work — it's essential for the mystery setup. I'm going to implement the secret panel as engine logic by finding the wall cells that border room 2 and an adjacent room, picking one deterministically (the first in reading order), and making it so walking into that wall from either side steps you through into the passage with the authentic entry message. This replaces whatever the original rule was, and I'll document it as a remaster decision. Actually, wait — let me think about whether the original even had you entering from a specific direction... For simplicity and to match how I remember discovering it as a kid by bumping walls, I'll make the entire shared wall between room 2 and its adjacent rooms permeable as the secret panel. Any wall cell along that boundary acts as the entry point, which feels faithful to the original and is easy to stumble upon. I'll note this in the README and refine it later if DOSBox testing shows something different. For the stairs, stepping onto door cell 32 teleports you to cell 33 and vice versa, using the entry descriptions from rooms 12 and 13 to describe the stairs themselves. After the teleport, I need to describe the new room. The tricky part is preventing an infinite loop: when the player enters 32 and teleports to 33, arriving at 33 shouldn't immediately teleport them back to 32. I'll track whether the teleport just happened so it only triggers once per movement, then the player can walk off normally to continue exploring. Now I'm checking the geometry — landing 33 sits at (15,31) and the upper shaft occupies rows 12-14 in column 31, so the upstairs hall should connect above that. The flood fill shows the upper rooms form one connected component through the doors, and the shaft must touch the hall to make the whole upstairs accessible. For NPC movement, I'll use a room-based graph rather than cell-by-cell pathfinding. Suspects teleport room-to-room each turn along doors, stairs, and panels with some probability — simpler and fits the period. Now I need to dig through the strings for the milestone 3 messages: victim intro, KNOCK response, LIST command, and the format for listing occupants.
Thinking · Architecting staircase teleportation mechanics and milestone progression.
I've figured out the staircase teleportation mechanic—the disconnected upper and lower shafts swap you between floors when you change direction—and confirmed V2's layout is fully connected except for the secret passage, which I'll make accessible via a permeable wall since its trigger is lost to the RMDEF tail. Next I'm analyzing the M3 message archaeology.
bash_toolFind victim intro, KNOCK, and LIST guest messages in EXE strings

cd /home/claude && grep -inE "murder|knock|guest|list|invit|hostess|host |weekend|body|evening|dinner" extracted/engine_strings_sleuth.txt | grep -vE "murder weapon|scene of the|ACCUSE" | head -20 && echo "=== cast + intro block ===" && sed -n '1,40p' extracted/engine_strings_sleuth.txt

Thinking · Excavated game assets and identified staircase trigger mechanism.
I'm looking at the opening setup for a murder mystery game—it starts with an atmospheric scene of a crime at an estate, then introduces the houseguests who are present at the location, with the estate itself named after the victim's surname for added dramatic effect. There are a couple of interesting details: a magnifying glass appears in two slightly different locations (next to the doorway versus on the floor), and there's a "nobody here" question that triggers despite people being present, plus threat messages that escalate as the investigation progresses. I also notice Eric Miller left debug strings visible in the executable showing murderer information and location tracking. I'm trying to track down the knock response and door-related interactions—my grep searches aren't turning up the knock handler, so I need to search for related terms like "Come in," "opens the door," "enters," or "leaves" to find how occupants respond to being approached.
Thinking · Cataloging game mechanics and parser responses.
I've found that the EXE reveals the victim's surname, the LIST header, and Eric Miller's debug overlay in the game files. Now I'll hunt down the remaining M3 messages.

Thinking · Architecting suspect rendering and proximity mechanics for gameplay.
I'm finding some great authentic lines from the game—the KNOCK response about not needing common courtesies, the LIST command format for displaying houseguests, and a better parser fallback message that feels more natural than my original version. The HELP text section appears to be cut off, but I've got the key dialogue pieces to work with. The rogue footman mechanic is a brilliant detail—when you drop an object, a footman catches it and it disappears, which means I need to rethink my drop logic entirely. Instead of relocating items, dropping the murder weapon (or anything else) should trigger this footman behavior where the object vanishes or returns to its original location. I'll implement this by having dropped items reset to their starting room slots rather than staying where they're dropped. There are also several dialogue and proximity mechanics to account for: suspects need to be gathered before you can accuse, there's distance-based questioning where you can't hear someone from across the room, and proximity checks for searching or examining things near people. For the map rendering, suspects will occupy specific cell positions at room anchors, and I'll display them using their first-initial letters. The 37 undecoded position pairs likely represent the 16 room anchors plus additional standing spots within rooms to accommodate multiple suspects in the same space. For suspect placement in M3, I'll position them at anchors with small offsets per index to keep multiple occupants within room bounds. The ACT system generates 5 variant action blocks across 12 locations with 2 actions each, so when entering a room with suspects, I'll print each one's name with a deterministically-chosen action. Movement happens each turn with suspects migrating to adjacent rooms at roughly 15% probability, and when a suspect enters the player's room, I'll announce the encounter rather than silently showing them appear on the map. For the room graph, I'm connecting rooms through door cells by finding neighbors of each door and linking all adjacent room pairs, handling stairs as a special case that teleports between rooms 12 and 13, and computing panel connections between rooms that share walls or have direct open transitions without doors. For NPC movement, I'm restricting suspects to indoor rooms (1-15 in V1, 1-16 in V2) by excluding the outdoor spawn room, which is the highest room ID. The player can bump through walls between the passage and adjacent rooms to step through with entry text, and the NPC graph includes the passage room for their pathfinding. When the player steps onto the stairs (room 32), they teleport to room 33's coordinates and vice versa, with a guard to prevent looping back immediately—I'll display the target stairs room's entry description. This only applies in V1 since V2 doesn't have those room codes. I'm also updating the help text and intro sequence: starting with "It is a dark and stormy night. A murder is being committed..." at scatter, then the victim line describing the body removal and the player standing at the open front door of the estate. For the LIST verb, I'll show the six houseguests currently at the estate, and add a courtesy line for KNOCK. On the data side, I need to ensure characters and NPC actions are properly structured—the rooms already have NPC actions in the content files, but I need to add a top-level landing_npc_actions field and set up room anchors that convert screen coordinates to grid coordinates using the formula grid_row = row-2 and grid_col = (col-1)//2. I'm also picking the victim and murderer at random from the cast, then distributing the remaining suspects across rooms with their positions tracked, rendering them with their first initial and placing them at room anchors or nearby walkable cells if the anchor is occupied. For the QUESTION command in M3, I'll add a check so if a named suspect isn't in the room, it responds with "But there's nobody here!" rather than proceeding. I need to write the engine modifications using a Python patch script to handle several targeted replacements: adding the CAST and NPC sections, building the room graph, integrating stairs and panel logic into movement, adding occupant narration to room descriptions, rendering suspects on the map, implementing LIST and KNOCK verbs, dropping the footman, and updating help text.
Thinking · Orchestrating engine patches and implementing staircase teleportation mechanics.
I've extracted the key game mechanics from the original code—the authentic KNOCK response, HELP text, the footman's drop-recovery system, and proximity-based dialogue—which means I can now replace my placeholder logic with the real implementations. Next I'll build out M3 with the stairs, secret panel, cast members, and dynamic suspect behavior.
bash_toolExtend data pipeline with cast, anchors, and NPC actions
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
src = open('gen_web_data.py').read()

src = src.replace('''    return {
        "label": "House 1 \\u00b7 Brisbane estate"
                 if content["meta"]["variant"] == "V1"
                 else "House 2 \\u00b7 Donleavy mansion",
        "variant": content["meta"]["variant"],
        "rooms": rooms,
        "grid": content["map"]["cells"],     # 17 rows x 40 cols of ints
        "weapons": weapons,
    }''',
'''    # room anchors: stored as 1-indexed SCREEN coords in RMDEF
    # (screen row = grid row + 2, screen col = grid col*2 + 1);
    # convert to grid coords here so the engine never does coordinate
    # arithmetic. (0,0) = no anchor for that room.
    anchors = {}
    for i, (row, col) in enumerate(content["map"]["room_anchors"]):
        rid = i + 1
        anchors[rid] = None if (row, col) == (0, 0) else \\
            [row - 2, (col - 1) // 2]
    # NPC idle actions, folded per room in Phase 1 (5 variant blocks of
    # [action, action] per room), plus the staircase-landing set
    for rid, r in content["rooms"].items():
        if r.get("npc_actions"):
            rooms[rid]["npc_actions"] = r["npc_actions"]
    return {
        "label": "House 1 \\u00b7 Brisbane estate"
                 if content["meta"]["variant"] == "V1"
                 else "House 2 \\u00b7 Donleavy mansion",
        "variant": content["meta"]["variant"],
        "rooms": rooms,
        "grid": content["map"]["cells"],     # 17 rows x 40 cols of ints
        "weapons": weapons,
        "characters": content["characters"],   # 7 names; 1 becomes victim
        "anchors": anchors,
        "landing_actions": content.get("landing_npc_actions", []),
    }''')
open('gen_web_data.py','w').write(src)
print("gen_web_data.py extended for M3")
EOF
python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web
Output
gen_web_data.py extended for M3
data_shared.js: 20 verbs, 12 nouns
data_v1.js: 16 rooms, 17x40 grid
data_v2.js: 17 rooms, 17x40 grid
sleuth_playable.html: single-file build written
Thinking · Architecting secret panel mechanics and milestone three implementation.
Now I'm working through the engine.js patch, starting with updating the header comment and adding the PEOPLE section that tracks the cast, victim, murderer, suspects with their positions, the room graph, and movement mechanics. Then I'll modify tryMove to handle the stairs teleport, secret panel interactions, and clock turning, update describeCell to list occupants, and revise redraw to show suspect letters, the turn counter, and milestone labels. Finally I'll add or refine the verb handlers for LIST, KNOCK, and foot-related actions. I'm also planning to replace the doDrop weapon logic with footman behavior, update doHelp and the default response to use authentic text, and split the stub cases so LIST and KNOCK get proper implementations while QUESTION gets a partial stub and cases 4 and 7 remain stubbed. The spawn function needs new intro text mentioning the victim and estate, describeCell needs to append occupant listings after objects and glass, redraw needs to display suspects and turn counts with the milestone 3 label, and the world object needs to track victim, murderer, suspects array, and turn count. I'm also precomputing the secret panel mechanics per house by mapping wall cell coordinates to their entry and exit points. For the panel system itself, I'm checking if a target cell is a wall that separates room 2 from another walkable room — if so, the player steps through to the far side and sees the appropriate passage description, then the destination room's description when they arrive. For stairs, when stepping onto a stair cell (code 32 or 33) with a partner staircase existing, I teleport the player to the partner's coordinates, display that room's entry description, and set a guard to prevent repeated teleportation until they step off the stairs entirely. Writing movement logic... I'm realizing the panel rule might let you pass through walls between rooms that shouldn't connect—but actually the condition requires either the destination or origin to be the passage cell, so you can't just bump through a thick wall between two regular rooms. The logic checks out as long as I'm careful about what counts as a valid transition. Now I'm setting up the core game loop: each turn increments the counter and moves the suspects around the house. I'm building a room graph structure that gets constructed once when the house loads, mapping out which rooms connect to each other based on the grid layout. Building the movement graph... Excluding the outdoor room from NPC movement targets, then handling panel edges by connecting cells where one side is a panel and the other is a regular room. For suspect movement, each tick they have a 15% chance to move to an adjacent room, and if they enter the player's room it triggers the first action for that location. When redrawing, suspects get positioned in their rooms with anchor points and offsets, and each room has up to 10 idle action options that suspects can perform. For rooms without dedicated actions like halls and stairs, I'm falling back to landing actions instead. When describing a cell to the player, I list each suspect in that room with their current action using a seeded random picker. Suspects are drawn with their first initial as a glyph, and since the player draws last, they'll always appear on top. For anchor positioning, if a room doesn't have a defined anchor point, I'll use the first walkable floor cell in that room, then place each occupant at the anchor column plus an offset of 2 cells per person, scanning for walkable cells if needed. For the QUESTION verb, I'm checking if there are any suspects in the player's room—if not, they get a "nobody here" response, otherwise it's a stub. GATHER and ACCUSE are also stubbed but with flavor text like "you haven't gathered all suspects yet" for ACCUSE. LIST displays the houseguests' names, KNOCK is just a courtesy line. For DROP, when the player drops a weapon, the footman catches it and returns it to its original scattered location, which I'm tracking via a weaponHome property on the world object. For HELP, I'm using two authentic lines adapted for arrow keys with a note. The fallback message for unrecognized commands is "I'm sorry, but I can't seem to make out what you're trying to say." On game spawn, I'm printing the atmospheric opening about the dark stormy night and murder, then the standing intro describing the victim and the estate entrance, keeping a small hint about typing HELP. Now I'm adding a turn counter for milestone 3. I'm placing it at the far right of row 18 to avoid collision with the seed display, showing it as a short "t 42" format. The victim and murderer exist in the world, and I'm adding a SLEUTH_DEBUG flag that exposes debug information similar to Eric's approach. I need to restructure the spawn logic so scatter handles the picks and spawn just prints the text, then write the patch carefully. I'm tracking lastDoor and lastStairs in the state object, and I need to update tryMove to call describeCell and redraw after movement. Now I'm finishing the spawn text block with the stormy night message and help prompt, then calling describeCell to show the initial room. I'm also appending the cast picks at the end of scatter, adding the necessary fields to the world definition, and implementing the weapon drop logic so the player can leave weapons in rooms between 1 and 11. I'm noticing a potential bug in how I'm handling object state — when the player picks up a weapon, I'm setting carrying but leaving the object data in the weaponRoom slot, which means matchObjectKeyword still finds it there. The display gets suppressed through the carrying checks, but on drop I need to make sure the object relocates properly and the weaponRoom reference updates correctly, especially when the footman returns the weapon to its home location.

Now the M3 engine patch — stairs, panel, cast, wandering suspects, and the authentic messages replacing my M2 stand-ins:

bash_toolApply the full milestone 3 engine patch
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
R = []
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:60]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

# ---- header + milestone labels ----
rep("SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 2",
    "SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 3")
rep('print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 2", 0x70);',
    'print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 3", 0x70);')

# ---- world gains people + clock ----
rep('''  carrying: null,     // name of carried weapon (null = none)
  sceneFound: false,
};''',
'''  carrying: null,     // name of carried weapon (null = none)
  sceneFound: false,
  victim: null,       // one of the 7 cast members died before we arrived
  murderer: null,     // one of the remaining 6 (secret until M5)
  suspects: [],       // the living six: {first, last, key, room}
  turn: 0,
};''')

# ---- scatter picks the cast ----
rep('''  world.hasGlass = false;
  world.weaponKnown = false;
  world.carrying = null;
  world.sceneFound = false;
}''',
'''  world.hasGlass = false;
  world.weaponKnown = false;
  world.carrying = null;
  world.sceneFound = false;
  // the cast: seven names in DESCR; each game one is the victim and
  // the remaining six are the houseguests (one secretly the murderer)
  const cast = [...house().characters];
  world.victim = cast.splice(Math.floor(rng() * cast.length), 1)[0];
  world.murderer = pick(cast);
  world.suspects = cast.map((p) => ({ ...p, room: pick(roomIds) }));
  world.turn = 0;
  buildRoomGraph();
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   5b. PEOPLE — the room graph and the wandering houseguests
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   Suspects move room-to-room, not cell-to-cell: each turn (= each
   player step) a suspect may wander to an adjacent room. Adjacency is
   derived from the grid itself: two rooms connect wherever their
   floor cells touch or a door cell joins them, plus the staircase
   pair (landing codes 32<->33) and the secret panel into room 2. */

let ROOM_GRAPH = {};

function buildRoomGraph() {
  const g = house().grid, H = g.length, W = g[0].length;
  const norm = (v) => (v === 0 ? 0 : v > 20 ? v - 20 : v);
  const connect = (a, b) => {
    if (!a || !b || a === b) return;
    (ROOM_GRAPH[a] = ROOM_GRAPH[a] || new Set()).add(b);
    (ROOM_GRAPH[b] = ROOM_GRAPH[b] || new Set()).add(a);
  };
  ROOM_GRAPH = {};
  for (let r = 0; r < H; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < W; c++) {
      const a = norm(g[r][c]);
      if (c + 1 < W) connect(a, norm(g[r][c + 1]));
      if (r + 1 < H) connect(a, norm(g[r + 1][c]));
      // secret panel: a 1-thick wall with the passageway (room 2) on
      // one side and another room on the other joins them
      if (g[r][c] === 0) {
        if (c > 0 && c + 1 < W &&
            (norm(g[r][c-1]) === 2) !== (norm(g[r][c+1]) === 2))
          connect(norm(g[r][c-1]), norm(g[r][c+1]));
        if (r > 0 && r + 1 < H &&
            (norm(g[r-1][c]) === 2) !== (norm(g[r+1][c]) === 2))
          connect(norm(g[r-1][c]), norm(g[r+1][c]));
      }
    }
  }
  if (findCell(32) && findCell(33)) connect(12, 13);   // the staircase
}

function findCell(code) {
  const g = house().grid;
  for (let r = 0; r < g.length; r++)
    for (let c = 0; c < g[0].length; c++)
      if (g[r][c] === code) return [r, c];
  return null;
}

// outdoors (the highest room id: stoop / front lawn) is off-limits to
// the guests — they lurk, indoors
function outdoorRoom() {
  const g = house().grid;
  let m = 0;
  for (const row of g) for (const v of row) if (v > m && v <= 20) m = v;
  return m;
}

function idleAction(roomId) {
  const room = house().rooms[String(roomId)];
  const pool = (room && room.npc_actions && room.npc_actions.length)
    ? room.npc_actions.flat()
    : house().landing_actions.flat();
  return pool.length ? pick(pool) : "is standing around.";
}

function tick() {
  world.turn++;
  const outdoor = outdoorRoom();
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (rng() < 0.15) {
      const options = [...(ROOM_GRAPH[s.room] || [])]
        .filter((r) => r !== outdoor && r <= 20);
      if (options.length) {
        const from = s.room;
        s.room = pick(options);
        // a guest arriving where the player stands is worth a line
        if (s.room === state.roomId && from !== state.roomId)
          say(`${s.first} ${idleAction(s.room)}`);
      }
    }
  }
}''')

# ---- tryMove: clock + stairs + panel ----
rep('''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;
  if (cellAt(r, c) === 0) return;
  state.row = r; state.col = c;
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}''',
'''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;

  if (cellAt(r, c) === 0) {
    // the secret panel: bump a 1-thick wall that separates the
    // passageway (room 2) from another room, and you step through.
    // (The original's exact trigger cell lives in RMDEF's undecoded
    // tail; whole-wall permeability is our documented stand-in.)
    const beyond = cellAt(r + dr, c + dc);
    const here = cellAt(state.row, state.col);
    if (beyond > 0 && beyond <= 20 && (beyond === 2 || here === 2)) {
      state.row = r + dr; state.col = c + dc;
      if (beyond === 2)
        say(house().rooms["2"].entry_description);
      tick();
      describeCell(beyond !== 2);   // announce the room we emerge into
      redraw();
    }
    return;
  }

  state.row = r; state.col = c;

  // the staircase: stepping onto a landing (32/33) carries you to its
  // partner landing on the other flight — whereupon you must change
  // direction to keep climbing, just as the manual warns
  const code = cellAt(r, c);
  if ((code === 32 || code === 33) &&
      state.lastStairs !== `${r},${c}`) {
    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      [state.row, state.col] = partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;
      const stairRoom = house().rooms[String((code === 32 ? 33 : 32) - 20)];
      if (stairRoom && stairRoom.entry_description)
        say(stairRoom.entry_description);
      tick();
      redraw();
      return;
    }
  }
  if (code <= 20) state.lastStairs = null;

  tick();
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}''')

# ---- state gains the stairs guard ----
rep('''  roomId: null,
  lastDoor: null,
};''',
'''  roomId: null,
  lastDoor: null,
  lastStairs: null,
};''')

# ---- describeCell: list the occupants ----
rep('''    const obj = objectHere();
    if (obj && obj.name !== world.carrying) say(obj.scene);
    if (state.roomId === world.glassRoom)
      say("A magnifying glass is lying on the floor.");
  }
}''',
'''    const obj = objectHere();
    if (obj && obj.name !== world.carrying) say(obj.scene);
    if (state.roomId === world.glassRoom)
      say("A magnifying glass is lying on the floor.");
    for (const s of world.suspects)
      if (s.room === state.roomId)
        say(`${s.first} ${s.last} ${idleAction(s.room)}`);
  }
}''')

# ---- spawn: the authentic intro ----
rep('''  redraw();
  say("It is a dark and stormy night. A murder has been committed...");
  say("(Type HELP for commands.)");
  describeCell(true);''',
'''  redraw();
  if (newGame || world.turn === 0) {
    say("It is a dark and stormy night.  A murder is being committed ...");
    say(`${world.victim.first} ${world.victim.last} was brutally murdered ` +
        "only hours ago.  The body was removed by persons unknown from " +
        "the murder scene.  You are now standing at the front door of " +
        `the ${world.victim.last} estate.  The door is open.`);
    say("(Type HELP for commands.)");
  }
  describeCell(true);''')

# ---- suspects drawn on the map ----
rep('''  const bg = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map & 0x70;
  put(state.row + MAP_TOP, state.col * 2, "@", bg | 0x0e);''',
'''  const bg = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map & 0x70;

  // houseguests: first initial at the room's anchor (the coordinate
  // table recovered from RMDEF's tail), nudged right for roommates
  const seatTaken = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    let pos = house().anchors[String(s.room)];
    if (!pos) pos = findCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    let [ar, ac] = pos;
    while (seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`]) {
      ac += 1;
      if (cellAt(ar, ac) === 0) { ar += 1; ac = pos[1]; }
      if (cellAt(ar, ac) === 0) break;
    }
    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;
    put(ar + MAP_TOP, ac * 2, s.first[0].toUpperCase(), bg | 0x0f);
  }

  put(state.row + MAP_TOP, state.col * 2, "@", bg | 0x0e);''')

# ---- turn counter on row 18 ----
rep('''  const inv = [world.hasGlass ? "glass" : null, world.carrying]
    .filter(Boolean).join(" + ");
  if (inv) print(18, COLS - inv.length - 12, "carrying: " + inv, 0x08);''',
'''  const inv = [world.hasGlass ? "glass" : null, world.carrying]
    .filter(Boolean).join(" + ");
  if (inv) print(18, COLS - inv.length - 22, "carrying: " + inv, 0x08);
  const t = `turn ${world.turn}`;
  print(18, COLS - t.length - 1, t, 0x08);''')

# ---- the rogue footman replaces drop-relocation ----
rep('''  if (world.carrying && matchObjectKeyword(input, world.objects[world.weaponRoom])) {
    // put the weapon down in the current room: swap it with this
    // room's object slot if we're in a room that can hold one
    say(`You put the ${world.carrying} down. Better not forget where you left it.`);
    if (state.roomId >= 1 && state.roomId <= 11) {
      world.objects[state.roomId] = { ...world.objects[world.weaponRoom] };
      world.weaponRoom = state.roomId;
    }
    world.carrying = null;
    return;
  }
  say("You aren't carrying anything like that!");''',
'''  if (world.carrying && matchObjectKeyword(input, world.objects[world.weaponRoom])) {
    // the original's answer to inventory management: staff
    say(`In the wink of an eye a rogue footman rushes up behind you, ` +
        `catches the ${world.carrying} as it falls, and soundlessly ` +
        `disappears.`);
    world.carrying = null;   // the footman returns it whence it came
    return;
  }
  say("You aren't carrying anything like that!");''')

# ---- authentic HELP ----
rep('''function doHelp() {
  say("Arrows move. Commands: EXAMINE <thing>, GET <thing>, DROP <thing>, " +
      "HELP, RESTART. Find the magnifying glass, the murder scene, and " +
      "the weapon. QUESTION, GATHER and ACCUSE arrive in later milestones.");
}''',
'''function doHelp() {
  say("To move around the house use the four arrow keys.  Objects can " +
      "be EXAMINED and TAKEN.  You can QUESTION people or ask them for " +
      "an ALIBI.  When you have solved the crime, pick up the murder " +
      "weapon, move to the murder room, ASSEMBLE the suspects, and " +
      "ACCUSE the guilty party.  Good Luck!");
  say("(This build: LIST guests, KNOCK, RESTART. QUESTION/ALIBI arrive " +
      "in milestone 4; GATHER/ACCUSE in milestone 5.)");
}''')

# ---- verbs: LIST, KNOCK, QUESTION presence check, authentic fallback ----
rep('''    case 2: case 4: case 7: case 12: case 13:
      say("(That verb is wired in a later milestone \\u2014 the suspects " +
          "haven't arrived yet.)"); break;
    case 6: say("(You can simply close the tab \\u2014 no DOS prompt awaits.)"); break;
    default:
      say("I don't understand. (HELP lists what I know.)");''',
'''    case 13: {                                // LIST the houseguests
      say(`The houseguests now lurking about the ${world.victim.last} ` +
          "estate are:");
      say(world.suspects.map((s) => `${s.first} ${s.last}`).join(", ") + ".");
      break;
    }
    case 12:                                  // KNOCK
      say("There's no need to observe common courtesies in this house.");
      break;
    case 2: {                                 // QUESTION (full in M4)
      const here = world.suspects.filter((s) => s.room === state.roomId);
      if (!here.length) say("But there's nobody here!");
      else say(`(${here[0].first} has plenty to say \\u2014 in milestone 4.)`);
      break;
    }
    case 4: case 7:
      say("But you haven't even gathered all of the suspects together yet!");
      break;
    case 6: say("(You can simply close the tab \\u2014 no DOS prompt awaits.)"); break;
    default:
      say("I'm sorry, but I can't seem to make out what you're trying " +
          "to say.");''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("engine.js patched to M3")
PATCH_EOF
node --check web/engine.js && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web > /dev/null && echo "built"

AssertionError: NOT FOUND:     case 2: case 4: case 7: case 12: case 13:

Thinking · Debugging unicode escape sequences in patched engine file.
I'm trying to trace through what happened with the script execution — the assertion failed at the last replacement, but the earlier replacements shouldn't have written anything since the script exits before opening the file in write mode. The confusing part is that "engine.js patched to M3" never printed, yet the output shows "built" and the node check passed, which suggests the command chain with `&&` should have short-circuited when Python exited with a non-zero status. Looking at the actual command structure, I realize the issue: after the heredoc terminator, the next line runs unconditionally because there's a newline instead of `&&` connecting them. So engine.js was never actually modified, and the build used the old M2 engine instead. The real bug is in my patch string itself — I used `\\u2014` (literal backslash-u-2014) when searching for the em dash, but the actual file contains the real em dash character. In Python, I need a single backslash to interpret the unicode escape properly, not a double backslash. Since none of the earlier replacements actually persisted to the file, I need to rerun the entire patch with the corrected final replacement after verifying what's actually in engine.js.
Thinking · Rectifying em dash encoding in Python patch.
The patch script failed on a final replacement due to an escaping mismatch, leaving engine.js untouched. I'll check the actual bytes in the stub case and re-run the corrected patch.

466:      say("(That verb is wired in a later milestone M-bM-^@M-^T the suspects " +$
Thinking · Debugged Unicode escape sequences in patch strings.
I see the issue with the em dash encoding — the file contains a literal UTF-8 em dash character, so my search pattern needs to match that exactly. I mistakenly wrote `\\u2014` with double backslashes in the Python heredoc, which treats it as literal text rather than an escape sequence. The fix is to use single-backslash escapes like `"\u2014"` in the Python source code, which will be interpreted as the actual em dash character, or I can just paste the em dash directly into the patch. Now I need to re-run the full patch with the corrected single-backslash `\u2014` in both search strings that contain em dashes — particularly the header replacement for "SLEUTH remaster — engine.js — Phase 2, Milestone 2". The replacement text with `\u2014` in JavaScript strings is fine since JS interprets those escapes correctly. I should also verify the doHelp search string matches the actual help text from line 449 of the M2 file.
Thinking · Debugging script mismatch between file and search text.
I found the issue — the file uses a real em dash while my search string was looking for the Unicode escape sequence. Let me run the patch again with the corrected search.
bash_toolRe-apply the corrected milestone 3 engine patch
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:60]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

rep("SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 2",
    "SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 3")
rep('print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 2", 0x70);',
    'print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 3", 0x70);')

rep('''  carrying: null,     // name of carried weapon (null = none)
  sceneFound: false,
};''',
'''  carrying: null,     // name of carried weapon (null = none)
  sceneFound: false,
  victim: null,       // one of the 7 cast members died before we arrived
  murderer: null,     // one of the remaining 6 (secret until M5)
  suspects: [],       // the living six: {first, last, key, room}
  turn: 0,
};''')

rep('''  world.hasGlass = false;
  world.weaponKnown = false;
  world.carrying = null;
  world.sceneFound = false;
}''',
'''  world.hasGlass = false;
  world.weaponKnown = false;
  world.carrying = null;
  world.sceneFound = false;
  // the cast: seven names in DESCR; each game one is the victim and
  // the remaining six are the houseguests (one secretly the murderer)
  const cast = [...house().characters];
  world.victim = cast.splice(Math.floor(rng() * cast.length), 1)[0];
  world.murderer = pick(cast);
  world.suspects = cast.map((p) => ({ ...p, room: pick(roomIds) }));
  world.turn = 0;
  buildRoomGraph();
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   5b. PEOPLE — the room graph and the wandering houseguests
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   Suspects move room-to-room, not cell-to-cell: each turn (= each
   player step) a suspect may wander to an adjacent room. Adjacency is
   derived from the grid itself: two rooms connect wherever their
   floor cells touch or a door cell joins them, plus the staircase
   pair (landing codes 32<->33) and the secret panel into room 2. */

let ROOM_GRAPH = {};

function buildRoomGraph() {
  const g = house().grid, H = g.length, W = g[0].length;
  const norm = (v) => (v === 0 ? 0 : v > 20 ? v - 20 : v);
  const connect = (a, b) => {
    if (!a || !b || a === b) return;
    (ROOM_GRAPH[a] = ROOM_GRAPH[a] || new Set()).add(b);
    (ROOM_GRAPH[b] = ROOM_GRAPH[b] || new Set()).add(a);
  };
  ROOM_GRAPH = {};
  for (let r = 0; r < H; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < W; c++) {
      const a = norm(g[r][c]);
      if (c + 1 < W) connect(a, norm(g[r][c + 1]));
      if (r + 1 < H) connect(a, norm(g[r + 1][c]));
      // secret panel: a 1-thick wall with the passageway (room 2) on
      // one side and another room on the other joins them
      if (g[r][c] === 0) {
        if (c > 0 && c + 1 < W &&
            (norm(g[r][c-1]) === 2) !== (norm(g[r][c+1]) === 2))
          connect(norm(g[r][c-1]), norm(g[r][c+1]));
        if (r > 0 && r + 1 < H &&
            (norm(g[r-1][c]) === 2) !== (norm(g[r+1][c]) === 2))
          connect(norm(g[r-1][c]), norm(g[r+1][c]));
      }
    }
  }
  if (findCell(32) && findCell(33)) connect(12, 13);   // the staircase
}

function findCell(code) {
  const g = house().grid;
  for (let r = 0; r < g.length; r++)
    for (let c = 0; c < g[0].length; c++)
      if (g[r][c] === code) return [r, c];
  return null;
}

// outdoors (the highest room id: stoop / front lawn) is off-limits to
// the guests — they lurk, indoors
function outdoorRoom() {
  const g = house().grid;
  let m = 0;
  for (const row of g) for (const v of row) if (v > m && v <= 20) m = v;
  return m;
}

function idleAction(roomId) {
  const room = house().rooms[String(roomId)];
  const pool = (room && room.npc_actions && room.npc_actions.length)
    ? room.npc_actions.flat()
    : house().landing_actions.flat();
  return pool.length ? pick(pool) : "is standing around.";
}

function tick() {
  world.turn++;
  const outdoor = outdoorRoom();
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (rng() < 0.15) {
      const options = [...(ROOM_GRAPH[s.room] || [])]
        .filter((r) => r !== outdoor && r <= 20);
      if (options.length) {
        const from = s.room;
        s.room = pick(options);
        // a guest arriving where the player stands is worth a line
        if (s.room === state.roomId && from !== state.roomId)
          say(`${s.first} ${idleAction(s.room)}`);
      }
    }
  }
}''')

rep('''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;
  if (cellAt(r, c) === 0) return;
  state.row = r; state.col = c;
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}''',
'''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;

  if (cellAt(r, c) === 0) {
    // the secret panel: bump a 1-thick wall that separates the
    // passageway (room 2) from another room, and you step through.
    // (The original's exact trigger cell lives in RMDEF's undecoded
    // tail; whole-wall permeability is our documented stand-in.)
    const beyond = cellAt(r + dr, c + dc);
    const here = cellAt(state.row, state.col);
    if (beyond > 0 && beyond <= 20 && (beyond === 2 || here === 2)) {
      state.row = r + dr; state.col = c + dc;
      if (beyond === 2)
        say(house().rooms["2"].entry_description);
      tick();
      describeCell(beyond !== 2);   // announce the room we emerge into
      redraw();
    }
    return;
  }

  state.row = r; state.col = c;

  // the staircase: stepping onto a landing (32/33) carries you to its
  // partner landing on the other flight — whereupon you must change
  // direction to keep climbing, just as the manual warns
  const code = cellAt(r, c);
  if ((code === 32 || code === 33) &&
      state.lastStairs !== `${r},${c}`) {
    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      [state.row, state.col] = partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;
      const stairRoom = house().rooms[String((code === 32 ? 33 : 32) - 20)];
      if (stairRoom && stairRoom.entry_description)
        say(stairRoom.entry_description);
      tick();
      redraw();
      return;
    }
  }
  if (code <= 20) state.lastStairs = null;

  tick();
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}''')

rep('''  roomId: null,
  lastDoor: null,
};''',
'''  roomId: null,
  lastDoor: null,
  lastStairs: null,
};''')

rep('''    const obj = objectHere();
    if (obj && obj.name !== world.carrying) say(obj.scene);
    if (state.roomId === world.glassRoom)
      say("A magnifying glass is lying on the floor.");
  }
}''',
'''    const obj = objectHere();
    if (obj && obj.name !== world.carrying) say(obj.scene);
    if (state.roomId === world.glassRoom)
      say("A magnifying glass is lying on the floor.");
    for (const s of world.suspects)
      if (s.room === state.roomId)
        say(`${s.first} ${s.last} ${idleAction(s.room)}`);
  }
}''')

rep('''  redraw();
  say("It is a dark and stormy night. A murder has been committed...");
  say("(Type HELP for commands.)");
  describeCell(true);''',
'''  redraw();
  say("It is a dark and stormy night.  A murder is being committed ...");
  say(`${world.victim.first} ${world.victim.last} was brutally murdered ` +
      "only hours ago.  The body was removed by persons unknown from " +
      "the murder scene.  You are now standing at the front door of " +
      `the ${world.victim.last} estate.  The door is open.`);
  say("(Type HELP for commands.)");
  describeCell(true);''')

rep('''  const bg = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map & 0x70;
  put(state.row + MAP_TOP, state.col * 2, "@", bg | 0x0e);''',
'''  const bg = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map & 0x70;

  // houseguests: first initial at the room's anchor (the coordinate
  // table recovered from RMDEF's tail), nudged right for roommates
  const seatTaken = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    let pos = house().anchors[String(s.room)];
    if (!pos) pos = findCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    let [ar, ac] = pos;
    while (seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] && cellAt(ar, ac + 1) > 0) ac += 1;
    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;
    put(ar + MAP_TOP, ac * 2, s.first[0].toUpperCase(), bg | 0x0f);
  }

  put(state.row + MAP_TOP, state.col * 2, "@", bg | 0x0e);''')

rep('''  const inv = [world.hasGlass ? "glass" : null, world.carrying]
    .filter(Boolean).join(" + ");
  if (inv) print(18, COLS - inv.length - 22, "carrying: " + inv, 0x08);''',
'''  const inv = [world.hasGlass ? "glass" : null, world.carrying]
    .filter(Boolean).join(" + ");
  if (inv) print(18, COLS - inv.length - 22, "carrying: " + inv, 0x08);
  const t = `turn ${world.turn}`;
  print(18, COLS - t.length - 1, t, 0x08);''')

rep('''  if (world.carrying && matchObjectKeyword(input, world.objects[world.weaponRoom])) {
    // put the weapon down in the current room: swap it with this
    // room's object slot if we're in a room that can hold one
    say(`You put the ${world.carrying} down. Better not forget where you left it.`);
    if (state.roomId >= 1 && state.roomId <= 11) {
      world.objects[state.roomId] = { ...world.objects[world.weaponRoom] };
      world.weaponRoom = state.roomId;
    }
    world.carrying = null;
    return;
  }
  say("You aren't carrying anything like that!");''',
'''  if (world.carrying && matchObjectKeyword(input, world.objects[world.weaponRoom])) {
    // the original's answer to inventory management: staff
    say(`In the wink of an eye a rogue footman rushes up behind you, ` +
        `catches the ${world.carrying} as it falls, and soundlessly ` +
        `disappears.`);
    world.carrying = null;   // the footman returns it whence it came
    return;
  }
  say("You aren't carrying anything like that!");''')

rep('''function doHelp() {
  say("Arrows move. Commands: EXAMINE <thing>, GET <thing>, DROP <thing>, " +
      "HELP, RESTART. Find the magnifying glass, the murder scene, and " +
      "the weapon. QUESTION, GATHER and ACCUSE arrive in later milestones.");
}''',
'''function doHelp() {
  say("To move around the house use the four arrow keys.  Objects can " +
      "be EXAMINED and TAKEN.  You can QUESTION people or ask them for " +
      "an ALIBI.  When you have solved the crime, pick up the murder " +
      "weapon, move to the murder room, ASSEMBLE the suspects, and " +
      "ACCUSE the guilty party.  Good Luck!");
  say("(This build: LIST guests, KNOCK, RESTART. QUESTION/ALIBI arrive " +
      "in milestone 4; GATHER/ACCUSE in milestone 5.)");
}''')

rep('''    case 2: case 4: case 7: case 12: case 13:
      say("(That verb is wired in a later milestone \u2014 the suspects " +
          "haven't arrived yet.)"); break;
    case 6: say("(You can simply close the tab \u2014 no DOS prompt awaits.)"); break;
    default:
      say("I don't understand. (HELP lists what I know.)");''',
'''    case 13: {                                // LIST the houseguests
      say(`The houseguests now lurking about the ${world.victim.last} ` +
          "estate are:");
      say(world.suspects.map((s) => `${s.first} ${s.last}`).join(", ") + ".");
      break;
    }
    case 12:                                  // KNOCK
      say("There's no need to observe common courtesies in this house.");
      break;
    case 2: {                                 // QUESTION (full in M4)
      const here = world.suspects.filter((s) => s.room === state.roomId);
      if (!here.length) say("But there's nobody here!");
      else say(`(${here[0].first} has plenty to say \u2014 in milestone 4.)`);
      break;
    }
    case 4: case 7:
      say("But you haven't even gathered all of the suspects together yet!");
      break;
    case 6: say("(You can simply close the tab \u2014 no DOS prompt awaits.)"); break;
    default:
      say("I'm sorry, but I can't seem to make out what you're trying " +
          "to say.");''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("engine.js patched to M3")

Thinking · Debugged search mismatch and identified conditional runner restructuring needs.
I'm realizing the search string in my new script has `- 22` but the actual file still contains `- 12` from the original version, since my previous attempt never successfully wrote the changes. The script is dying at the inventory rep step, which happens before the file write at the end, so nothing gets persisted. I need to fix the search pattern back to `- 12` and try again. This time everything before the inventory rep matched correctly and the assertions passed. I'm also verifying the replacements that come after it — the drop-footman search matches the original text, help checks out, and the stub-case em dash is properly handled both in the file and in my Python escape sequence. I should also refactor the runner to make the commands actually conditional instead of running unconditionally.
Thinking · Scrutinizing roomIds reference inconsistency in scatter function.
I found two issues: the search text was wrong (looking for `- 22` when the file has `- 12`) and the build command isn't properly reporting failures. I'll fix both now.
bash_toolApply corrected M3 patch with proper failure gating
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - > /tmp/patch.log 2>&1 << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:60]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

rep("SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 2",
    "SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 3")
rep('print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 2", 0x70);',
    'print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 3", 0x70);')

rep('''  carrying: null,     // name of carried weapon (null = none)
  sceneFound: false,
};''',
'''  carrying: null,     // name of carried weapon (null = none)
  sceneFound: false,
  victim: null,       // one of the 7 cast members died before we arrived
  murderer: null,     // one of the remaining 6 (secret until M5)
  suspects: [],       // the living six: {first, last, key, room}
  turn: 0,
};''')

rep('''  world.hasGlass = false;
  world.weaponKnown = false;
  world.carrying = null;
  world.sceneFound = false;
}''',
'''  world.hasGlass = false;
  world.weaponKnown = false;
  world.carrying = null;
  world.sceneFound = false;
  // the cast: seven names in DESCR; each game one is the victim and
  // the remaining six are the houseguests (one secretly the murderer)
  const cast = [...house().characters];
  world.victim = cast.splice(Math.floor(rng() * cast.length), 1)[0];
  world.murderer = pick(cast);
  world.suspects = cast.map((p) => ({ ...p, room: pick(roomIds) }));
  world.turn = 0;
  buildRoomGraph();
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   5b. PEOPLE \u2014 the room graph and the wandering houseguests
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   Suspects move room-to-room, not cell-to-cell: each turn (= each
   player step) a suspect may wander to an adjacent room. Adjacency is
   derived from the grid itself: two rooms connect wherever their
   floor cells touch or a door cell joins them, plus the staircase
   pair (landing codes 32<->33) and the secret panel into room 2. */

let ROOM_GRAPH = {};

function buildRoomGraph() {
  const g = house().grid, H = g.length, W = g[0].length;
  const norm = (v) => (v === 0 ? 0 : v > 20 ? v - 20 : v);
  const connect = (a, b) => {
    if (!a || !b || a === b) return;
    (ROOM_GRAPH[a] = ROOM_GRAPH[a] || new Set()).add(b);
    (ROOM_GRAPH[b] = ROOM_GRAPH[b] || new Set()).add(a);
  };
  ROOM_GRAPH = {};
  for (let r = 0; r < H; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < W; c++) {
      const a = norm(g[r][c]);
      if (c + 1 < W) connect(a, norm(g[r][c + 1]));
      if (r + 1 < H) connect(a, norm(g[r + 1][c]));
      // secret panel: a 1-thick wall with the passageway (room 2) on
      // one side and another room on the other joins them
      if (g[r][c] === 0) {
        if (c > 0 && c + 1 < W &&
            (norm(g[r][c-1]) === 2) !== (norm(g[r][c+1]) === 2))
          connect(norm(g[r][c-1]), norm(g[r][c+1]));
        if (r > 0 && r + 1 < H &&
            (norm(g[r-1][c]) === 2) !== (norm(g[r+1][c]) === 2))
          connect(norm(g[r-1][c]), norm(g[r+1][c]));
      }
    }
  }
  if (findCell(32) && findCell(33)) connect(12, 13);   // the staircase
}

function findCell(code) {
  const g = house().grid;
  for (let r = 0; r < g.length; r++)
    for (let c = 0; c < g[0].length; c++)
      if (g[r][c] === code) return [r, c];
  return null;
}

// outdoors (the highest room id: stoop / front lawn) is off-limits to
// the guests \u2014 they lurk, indoors
function outdoorRoom() {
  const g = house().grid;
  let m = 0;
  for (const row of g) for (const v of row) if (v > m && v <= 20) m = v;
  return m;
}

function idleAction(roomId) {
  const room = house().rooms[String(roomId)];
  const pool = (room && room.npc_actions && room.npc_actions.length)
    ? room.npc_actions.flat()
    : house().landing_actions.flat();
  return pool.length ? pick(pool) : "is standing around.";
}

function tick() {
  world.turn++;
  const outdoor = outdoorRoom();
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (rng() < 0.15) {
      const options = [...(ROOM_GRAPH[s.room] || [])]
        .filter((r) => r !== outdoor && r <= 20);
      if (options.length) {
        const from = s.room;
        s.room = pick(options);
        // a guest arriving where the player stands is worth a line
        if (s.room === state.roomId && from !== state.roomId)
          say(`${s.first} ${idleAction(s.room)}`);
      }
    }
  }
}''')

rep('''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;
  if (cellAt(r, c) === 0) return;
  state.row = r; state.col = c;
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}''',
'''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;

  if (cellAt(r, c) === 0) {
    // the secret panel: bump a 1-thick wall that separates the
    // passageway (room 2) from another room, and you step through.
    // (The original's exact trigger cell lives in RMDEF's undecoded
    // tail; whole-wall permeability is our documented stand-in.)
    const beyond = cellAt(r + dr, c + dc);
    const here = cellAt(state.row, state.col);
    if (beyond > 0 && beyond <= 20 && (beyond === 2 || here === 2)) {
      state.row = r + dr; state.col = c + dc;
      if (beyond === 2)
        say(house().rooms["2"].entry_description);
      tick();
      describeCell(beyond !== 2);   // announce the room we emerge into
      redraw();
    }
    return;
  }

  state.row = r; state.col = c;

  // the staircase: stepping onto a landing (32/33) carries you to its
  // partner landing on the other flight \u2014 whereupon you must change
  // direction to keep climbing, just as the manual warns
  const code = cellAt(r, c);
  if ((code === 32 || code === 33) &&
      state.lastStairs !== `${r},${c}`) {
    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      [state.row, state.col] = partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;
      const stairRoom = house().rooms[String((code === 32 ? 33 : 32) - 20)];
      if (stairRoom && stairRoom.entry_description)
        say(stairRoom.entry_description);
      tick();
      redraw();
      return;
    }
  }
  if (code <= 20) state.lastStairs = null;

  tick();
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}''')

rep('''  roomId: null,
  lastDoor: null,
};''',
'''  roomId: null,
  lastDoor: null,
  lastStairs: null,
};''')

rep('''    const obj = objectHere();
    if (obj && obj.name !== world.carrying) say(obj.scene);
    if (state.roomId === world.glassRoom)
      say("A magnifying glass is lying on the floor.");
  }
}''',
'''    const obj = objectHere();
    if (obj && obj.name !== world.carrying) say(obj.scene);
    if (state.roomId === world.glassRoom)
      say("A magnifying glass is lying on the floor.");
    for (const s of world.suspects)
      if (s.room === state.roomId)
        say(`${s.first} ${s.last} ${idleAction(s.room)}`);
  }
}''')

rep('''  redraw();
  say("It is a dark and stormy night. A murder has been committed...");
  say("(Type HELP for commands.)");
  describeCell(true);''',
'''  redraw();
  say("It is a dark and stormy night.  A murder is being committed ...");
  say(`${world.victim.first} ${world.victim.last} was brutally murdered ` +
      "only hours ago.  The body was removed by persons unknown from " +
      "the murder scene.  You are now standing at the front door of " +
      `the ${world.victim.last} estate.  The door is open.`);
  say("(Type HELP for commands.)");
  describeCell(true);''')

rep('''  const bg = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map & 0x70;
  put(state.row + MAP_TOP, state.col * 2, "@", bg | 0x0e);''',
'''  const bg = SCHEMES[state.schemeIdx].map & 0x70;

  // houseguests: first initial at the room's anchor (the coordinate
  // table recovered from RMDEF's tail), nudged right for roommates
  const seatTaken = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    let pos = house().anchors[String(s.room)];
    if (!pos) pos = findCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    let [ar, ac] = pos;
    while (seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] && cellAt(ar, ac + 1) > 0) ac += 1;
    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;
    put(ar + MAP_TOP, ac * 2, s.first[0].toUpperCase(), bg | 0x0f);
  }

  put(state.row + MAP_TOP, state.col * 2, "@", bg | 0x0e);''')

rep('''  if (inv) print(18, COLS - inv.length - 12, "carrying: " + inv, 0x08);''',
'''  if (inv) print(18, COLS - inv.length - 22, "carrying: " + inv, 0x08);
  const t = `turn ${world.turn}`;
  print(18, COLS - t.length - 1, t, 0x08);''')

rep('''  if (world.carrying && matchObjectKeyword(input, world.objects[world.weaponRoom])) {
    // put the weapon down in the current room: swap it with this
    // room's object slot if we're in a room that can hold one
    say(`You put the ${world.carrying} down. Better not forget where you left it.`);
    if (state.roomId >= 1 && state.roomId <= 11) {
      world.objects[state.roomId] = { ...world.objects[world.weaponRoom] };
      world.weaponRoom = state.roomId;
    }
    world.carrying = null;
    return;
  }
  say("You aren't carrying anything like that!");''',
'''  if (world.carrying && matchObjectKeyword(input, world.objects[world.weaponRoom])) {
    // the original's answer to inventory management: staff
    say(`In the wink of an eye a rogue footman rushes up behind you, ` +
        `catches the ${world.carrying} as it falls, and soundlessly ` +
        `disappears.`);
    world.carrying = null;   // the footman returns it whence it came
    return;
  }
  say("You aren't carrying anything like that!");''')

rep('''function doHelp() {
  say("Arrows move. Commands: EXAMINE <thing>, GET <thing>, DROP <thing>, " +
      "HELP, RESTART. Find the magnifying glass, the murder scene, and " +
      "the weapon. QUESTION, GATHER and ACCUSE arrive in later milestones.");
}''',
'''function doHelp() {
  say("To move around the house use the four arrow keys.  Objects can " +
      "be EXAMINED and TAKEN.  You can QUESTION people or ask them for " +
      "an ALIBI.  When you have solved the crime, pick up the murder " +
      "weapon, move to the murder room, ASSEMBLE the suspects, and " +
      "ACCUSE the guilty party.  Good Luck!");
  say("(This build: LIST guests, KNOCK, RESTART. QUESTION/ALIBI arrive " +
      "in milestone 4; GATHER/ACCUSE in milestone 5.)");
}''')

rep('''    case 2: case 4: case 7: case 12: case 13:
      say("(That verb is wired in a later milestone \u2014 the suspects " +
          "haven't arrived yet.)"); break;
    case 6: say("(You can simply close the tab \u2014 no DOS prompt awaits.)"); break;
    default:
      say("I don't understand. (HELP lists what I know.)");''',
'''    case 13: {                                // LIST the houseguests
      say(`The houseguests now lurking about the ${world.victim.last} ` +
          "estate are:");
      say(world.suspects.map((s) => `${s.first} ${s.last}`).join(", ") + ".");
      break;
    }
    case 12:                                  // KNOCK
      say("There's no need to observe common courtesies in this house.");
      break;
    case 2: {                                 // QUESTION (full in M4)
      const here = world.suspects.filter((s) => s.room === state.roomId);
      if (!here.length) say("But there's nobody here!");
      else say(`(${here[0].first} has plenty to say \u2014 in milestone 4.)`);
      break;
    }
    case 4: case 7:
      say("But you haven't even gathered all of the suspects together yet!");
      break;
    case 6: say("(You can simply close the tab \u2014 no DOS prompt awaits.)"); break;
    default:
      say("I'm sorry, but I can't seem to make out what you're trying " +
          "to say.");''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("PATCH_COMPLETE")
PATCH_EOF
cat /tmp/patch.log && grep -q PATCH_COMPLETE /tmp/patch.log && node --check web/engine.js && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web > /dev/null && echo "BUILD OK"
Output
PATCH_COMPLETE
BUILD OK
Thinking · Tracing staircase navigation and teleportation mechanics.
The patch is working, but I'm noticing that `scatter()` now depends on `roomIds` being in scope. In the original M2 version, `roomIds` was created early in the function and remained accessible throughout, including in the later `pick()` calls. That should still work fine since everything's in the same function scope. I'm also checking the initialization chain: `scatter()` picks a victim, then `spawn(false)` prints the intro. When the Tab key switches houses, it calls `spawn(true)`, which internally calls `scatter()` to rebuild the room graph for the new house. On restart (case 9), it re-seeds and calls `spawn(true)` the same way. The intro prints on every spawn, but that's correct since each spawn represents either the initial game or a new game state. Now I'm tracing through the M3 test suite, specifically how the player navigates to the lower staircase. The path goes: stoop → front door → main hall, then from the main hall I need to figure out how to reach the stairs shaft. The hall extends up to row 11 at columns 9-11, and the shaft top is at (12,9), so the player walks up the hall to row 11 at column 9, then moves down into the shaft. Then continuing left through the hall at row 11 and down the shaft to the landing, which teleports me to the upper level — I'll verify the room transitions by checking the actual cellAt values during navigation and confirming the final room description matches the upstairs hall. After that, I can optionally test reaching the bathroom through the hall after the teleport. For the secret panel test, I'm placing the player next to the passage entrance in the dining room and moving down through the wall into the passage itself, checking that the secret panel message appears, then continuing down to exit into the living room and verify that transition. I also need to confirm all six suspects are placed and that the LIST command shows their names plus the estate name, with the victim excluded from the suspect list. Finally, I'm checking that suspect initials render on screen by counting uppercase letters at their anchor positions and verifying at least a few initials appear in the plain text output. For the remaining tests, I'm setting up a suspect in a room and having the player enter to verify their name appears in the occupants list, testing the "nobody here" message for empty rooms, checking KNOCK and DROP commands with fallback text, and running the suspect movement system through multiple ticks to confirm they actually change rooms when moving through hallways.
Thinking · Debugging the list command's output parsing.
Patch applied successfully. Now I'll run the M3 test suite to verify the staircase route works as expected.
bash_toolRun the milestone 3 test suite: stairs, panel, cast, wandering
bash
cd /home/claude/web && node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 42, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const plain = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/&amp;/g,'&');
const flat = () => plain().replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(plain()); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;

// --- cast & intro
assert(world.suspects.length === 6, "six suspects");
assert(!world.suspects.find(s => s.first === world.victim.first), "victim is not a suspect");
assert(flat().includes(`${world.victim.first} ${world.victim.last} was brutally murdered`), "victim intro");
assert(flat().includes(`front door of the ${world.victim.last} estate`), "estate named after victim");

// --- THE STAIRCASE (V1): stoop -> hall -> down lower flight -> teleport -> up
for (let i=0;i<6;i++) key("ArrowUp");        // through front door, up hall col 11
for (let i=0;i<2;i++) key("ArrowLeft");      // hall col 9 (row must be <=11)
// ensure we're on col 9 above the shaft, then descend
while (state.col > 9) key("ArrowLeft");
while (state.row > 8) key("ArrowUp");
for (let i=0;i<8;i++) key("ArrowDown");      // down shaft onto landing 32
assert(state.col === 31 || D.house().grid[state.row][state.col] === 33 || state.col > 20,
       `teleported across (now at ${state.row},${state.col})`);
for (let i=0;i<5;i++) key("ArrowUp");        // climb the upper flight
const upRoom = D.house().grid[state.row][state.col];
assert([13,14].includes(upRoom), `now on upper floor (room ${upRoom})`);
for (let i=0;i<3;i++) key("ArrowUp");
assert(flat().toLowerCase().includes("upstairs hall") || [14].includes(D.house().grid[state.row][state.col]),
       "reached the upstairs hall");
// go visit the bathroom to prove upstairs rooms open up
while (state.row > 1) key("ArrowUp");
console.log("  upstairs position:", state.row, state.col, "room", D.house().grid[state.row][state.col]);

// --- THE SECRET PANEL: dining (above passage) bump-through
state.row = 5; state.col = 3; state.roomId = 1;   // south edge of dining
key("ArrowDown");                                  // bump the wall -> step through
assert(D.house().grid[state.row][state.col] === 2, "stepped through into the passageway");
assert(flat().includes("secret panel in the wall"), "authentic panel entry text");
key("ArrowDown");                                  // bump south wall -> living room
assert(D.house().grid[state.row][state.col] === 3, "stepped out into the living room");

// --- LIST / KNOCK / QUESTION / fallback
type("list");
assert(flat().includes(`lurking about the ${world.victim.last} estate`), "LIST header");
assert(world.suspects.every(s => flat().includes(s.first)), "LIST shows all six");
type("knock");
assert(flat().includes("common courtesies"), "KNOCK response");
world.suspects.forEach(s => s.room = 10);          // clear the living room
state.roomId = 3;
type("question maude");
assert(flat().includes("But there's nobody here!"), "QUESTION empty room");
world.suspects[0].room = 3;
type("question " + world.suspects[0].first.toLowerCase());
assert(flat().includes("milestone 4"), "QUESTION stub with someone present");
type("xyzzy");
assert(flat().includes("can't seem to make out"), "authentic parser fallback");

// --- occupant narration on entry + initials on map
world.suspects[0].room = 1;
state.row = 2; state.col = 9; state.roomId = 15;   // hall outside dining door
key("ArrowLeft");                                   // onto door 21
key("ArrowLeft");                                   // into dining
assert(flat().includes(world.suspects[0].first + " " + world.suspects[0].last),
       "occupant announced with idle action");
const anchor = D.house().anchors["1"];
const gridRow = plain().split("\n")[anchor[0] + 1];
assert(gridRow[anchor[1] * 2] === world.suspects[0].first[0].toUpperCase(),
       "suspect initial drawn at the recovered room anchor");

// --- footman
world.hasGlass = true;
const wkey = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
state.roomId = world.weaponRoom;
const pos = (() => { const g=D.house().grid; for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===world.weaponRoom) return [r,c]; })();
[state.row, state.col] = pos;
type("examine " + wkey.toLowerCase());
type("get " + wkey.toLowerCase());
assert(world.carrying, "carrying the weapon");
type("drop " + wkey.toLowerCase());
assert(flat().includes("rogue footman") && !world.carrying, "the rogue footman");

// --- suspects wander over turns (seeded)
const before = world.suspects.map(s => s.room).join(",");
for (let i=0;i<60;i++) { key("ArrowUp"); key("ArrowDown"); }
const after = world.suspects.map(s => s.room).join(",");
assert(before !== after, "suspects wander on the turn clock");
assert(world.turn > 100, "turn counter ticking: " + world.turn);

// --- V2 sanity: no stairs codes, graph still works, no crash
key("Escape"); key("Tab");
assert(flat().includes("Donleavy") || flat().includes("estate"), "house 2 loads with cast");
for (let i=0;i<20;i++) key("ArrowUp");
assert(plain().includes("@"), "V2 walkable, no crash");
console.log("\nALL M3 SMOKE TESTS PASSED");
EOF

ok: authentic panel entry text
ok: stepped out into the living room

██              ██      ██              ██████████████████      ██              
██              ██      ████████  ████████              ██      ██              
██████████████████      ██              ██              ██      ██              
██              ██      ██              ██                      ████████████████
██████████████████      ██              ██              ██      ██              
██    @         ██      ██████████████████              ██                      
██                      ██              ██████████████████      ██              
██              ██      ██    E         ██              ██      ██

██              ██  ██  ██              ██                  ██  ██
