}
console.log("SOLVE-BOT REGRESSION PASSED");
EOF
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
r = open('web/README_PHASE2.md').read()
r = r.replace('''## The mystery engine (M4 design notes)''',
'''## Guest distribution (M4.5 field notes)

A playtester recalled guests appearing one at a time, in rooms — not
clustering in corridors. The data agrees, conclusively: there are
exactly **12 seats for 12 eligible locations** (rooms 1–11 plus the
staircase), the ACT idle-action pools cover exactly those 12 and
nothing else — the halls have no seat and not one scripted sentence
for a guest standing in them — and every landing action is written
for a single passerby. So the model is now: **one guest per location,
never in halls**, with halls and the outdoors acting as transit in a
contracted movement graph (upstairs rooms reach downstairs rooms via
the staircase in two hops). A pleasant consequence falls out for
free: QUESTION is never ambiguous, because at most one guest can be
present — which is why the original never needed a disambiguation
prompt. The per-turn movement rate remains the one thing static
analysis cannot recover (it is a constant in compiled code); ours is
a tunable, currently 0.12, refinable by timing DOSBox sessions.

## The mystery engine (M4 design notes)''')
open('web/README_PHASE2.md','w').write(r)
print("README updated")
EOF
cd web && cp sleuth_playable.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/ && zip -qo /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m4.zip index.html engine.js data_shared.js data_v1.js data_v2.js gen_web_data.py README_PHASE2.md sleuth_playable.html && echo packaged

Output
[local_resource block]
[local_resource block]

Your recollection was correct, and better still, it was provable from the files. The evidence stacks three deep:

  1. The seat table is exactly 12 anchors — rooms 1–11 plus one staircase seat. If two guests could share a room, one seat per location wouldn't suffice.
  2. The ACT idle-action pools cover exactly those same 12 locations and nothing else. The halls have no seat and not a single scripted sentence for someone standing in them — the game literally has no words for a guest idling in a corridor.
  3. Every landing action is written for one passerby ("bumps into you on the landing," "walks past you in a great hurry") — singular encounters, exactly as you remembered.
So the model is now: one guest per location, twelve eligible locations, halls and outdoors are transit only. Movement runs on a contracted graph where upstairs rooms reach downstairs ones through the staircase in two hops, moves require an unoccupied destination, and both flights count as one staircase for meetings. Verified over 3,000 turns per house: never two guests in one place, never a guest standing in a hall, and all twelve locations still get roamed. The solve-bot regression passed three fresh mysteries under the new model.

And notice what fell out for free: QUESTION is never ambiguous, structurally. At most one guest can be present, so a bare QUESTION (or Q) simply addresses whoever's there — which is why the original never needed a disambiguation prompt. The design constraint you remembered is the parser design. I also dropped my seat-nudging code entirely; it was solving a problem the original never permitted.

The one thing static analysis genuinely can't recover is the per-turn movement rate — that's a compiled-in constant. Ours is a named tunable (NPC_MOVE_CHANCE, currently 0.12), and if you ever feel like timing guest movements across a few DOSBox sessions, that's the last behavioral parameter we could calibrate against ground truth.

Both files refreshed. M5 — GATHER, ACCUSE, the banked confession payoffs, the stalking-murderer timer, and the Holmesian rank — remains the final milestone whenever you're ready.

Thinking · Mapping endgame mechanics and threat escalation sequences.
I'm working through the endgame mechanics, specifically the GATHER/ASSEMBLE action that brings all suspects together. The game hints suggest this happens before the final accusation, and it seems like it should only work in the murder room where the climactic confrontation takes place. Now I need to dig back into the strings file to find all the endgame-related messages—the accusation responses, how the murderer reacts when suspicion escalates, and the death scene where they lock the door and reveal themselves with the weapon. Implementing the threat system... The murderer physically pursues you when stalking begins, and death triggers when they reach your location at threat level 2 or higher. The death scene uses specific lock strings like "locks the door behind you, grabs the" combined with the weapon name to create that dramatic moment. I'm noticing there are multiple lock variants for different kill scenarios, and a separate locked-door message that appears during the accusation phase. Now I need to trace through the endgame flow: once everyone gathers for accusation, the door locks to prevent escape. I'm looking for the exact string wording to understand how the GATHER mechanic works and what conditions must be met to trigger it. There's also a ranking system based on turns and questions asked—Apprentice, Professional, Holmesian—that I need to implement alongside the core endgame states like gathered, threat level, and gameOver. For the GATHER command, it seems players can assemble suspects from anywhere, and when triggered, all six suspects relocate to the player's location in a dramatic sequence. Once gathered, they stay put until the accusation or restart happens. The ACCUSE command then validates a sequence of conditions: first checking if suspects are actually gathered, then verifying the player has the murder weapon in their possession. I need to search the strings file around those line numbers to see the full endgame dialogue block, particularly the "stalking" context.

The finale. First, let me recover the complete endgame text block from the EXE — accusation responses, the threat escalation, the death scene, and the ranks:

Thinking · Deciphering accusation mechanics and taunt responses.
I'm looking at the game flow for the ACCUSE mechanic—the player needs to identify one of six people in the room, and the system handles various outcomes: when they haven't specified a name clearly, when they guess wrong but have the right room and weapon combination, and the accused person's angry response to being wrongly implicated. So when you accuse the murderer in the right room but with the wrong weapon, they reveal the actual weapon they used. And if you accuse them in the wrong room, they taunt you by revealing both the correct weapon and the correct location. Either way, an incorrect accusation ends the game with the murderer mocking your failure and exposing what you got wrong. I'm settling on the response structure: accusing an innocent triggers an angry denial and you lose, accusing the murderer in the right room with the right weapon leads to a confession and victory, and accusing them in the wrong room produces a mocking admission that they committed the crime elsewhere. For the edge cases, I realize our game engine only allows picking up the confirmed murder weapon, so you can't actually carry a wrong weapon to make an accusation—that scenario doesn't exist in our implementation. But there's a question of whether you can accuse someone without having found the weapon at all, which might trigger a different failure message about needing to gather evidence first. I'm thinking the design should be: ACCUSE requires everyone gathered (with the authentic message if they're not), and if you're not carrying the weapon or you're in the wrong room, the accusation still proceeds but fails with the appropriate taunt—either the "wrong room" message or something about how the murderer used the weapon. If you accuse an innocent person, they always respond with the same denial regardless. For the GATHER action, there's a precondition message about needing to assemble all the suspects, and then the gathering scene plays out with everyone waiting. After gathering, the door locks to prevent leaving—you're forced to make your accusation. I need to block movement out of the room with a message about the locked door, and any attempt to leave triggers that response. There's also a note about a blind corridor where typing commands while blind produces a specific line about no talking being allowed. I'm setting up a threat escalation system with three levels—suspicion, active stalking, and a confrontation where the murderer catches the player. The death scenes have two variants with the murderer revealing their guilt and trapping the player in a room. I'll also add a gathered scene description where guests are staring at the floor for use in group narration. The murderer's location is tracked separately. I'm thinking through the escalation model: a global countdown timer starts at game begin, and as it depletes, the murderer progresses from suspicious to actively stalking to attempting a kill. Taking the murder weapon could accelerate this deadline, making the threat more immediate. The exact thresholds and timing values are tunable parameters I'd need to nail down. Once the case is solved, the player gets ranked based on how many moves it took—Apprentice, Professional, or Holmesian—with move count thresholds determining which tier they achieve. After the game ends, a post-game prompt asks whether they want to quit or restart, disabling further movement and only accepting those two commands. When the player makes the correct accusation, the murderer confesses using a template that combines their name with a confession line, which can start with descriptive phrases like "grinning malevolently" or "in a state of shock." The case closes after N moves with a rank announcement, followed by the quit/restart prompt. I'm also noticing those coordinate sequences in the data—they're drawing paths that trace out the letters S-L-E-U-T-H on the title screen, probably animated as part of the intro or the murderer's walk animation. The hex string "&h8b55..." is inline machine code for a sound routine called from BASIC. The original game actually prompted for a random number seed to personalize each playthrough, which is a nice touch. Now I'm sketching out the implementation: I need to add world state properties for whether clues are gathered, a deadline counter, a threat level, and a game-over flag. The deadline starts at 220 moves (tunable), threat level at zero, and the game is active. Each turn, after the player moves, I check if the deadline is approaching—if threat level is still zero and fewer than 90 moves remain, escalate to level one with a warning message; if fewer than 40 moves remain, push to level two. Once threat level two kicks in, the murderer starts stalking the player, moving one step closer each turn along the shortest path through the room graph. If the murderer reaches the player's room, it's death—either variant one or three depending on whether the player has the weapon. If the deadline expires entirely, the murderer finds the player regardless. I also need to handle the case where the player walks into the murderer's room while being stalked, which triggers death variant two. Picking up the weapon accelerates the deadline by capping it at 70 moves from now. After gathering all clues, the player gets locked in place and can't move. When the gather command executes, I'm setting the gathered flag and storing the room location, then displaying an authentic message about the suspects assembling with the door now locked. I need to render all the gathered guests in the room by positioning them around an anchor point with spacing between them, reusing the nudging logic from before. For idle narration when gathered, I can use the existing line about staring at the floor, and then for the accuse command I'll check that guests have actually been gathered before allowing an accusation. Now I'm handling the accuse verb itself — first checking if a suspect was named, then determining if they're innocent or guilty. If innocent, they angrily deny it and the player loses. If guilty, I check whether the player is in the murder room and carrying the weapon; if both conditions are met, the player wins with a confession and ranking, otherwise the murderer taunts them. Once the game ends, I'm disabling most commands and only allowing restart or quit, with a prompt asking the player what they want to do next. I'm calculating the player's rank based on how many turns it took—under 100 moves earns "Holmesian," up to 180 is "Professional," and anything longer is "Apprentice"—then displaying a message with the final turn count and rank. The murderer hunts the player using pathfinding when stalking is active, calculating the shortest route each turn and moving one step closer. If the player hides in the hallways where the murderer can't navigate, they're safe from pursuit but the deadline still looms as a backstop. When the player is blind in the corridor, any key press triggers a beep, displays a message, and teleports them away. I need to freeze suspect movement once they're all gathered in one location, and the stalker threat should also pause since an accusation is imminent. During the gathered state, proximity checks for questioning should work fine since everyone shares the same room anchor. Let me verify the victim and estate descriptions are complete, then run through the test cases: checking that accusations before gathering produce the right dialogue, and that gathering requires all suspects in the player's location with movement blocked afterward.

The complete endgame, verbatim — and richer than expected:

  • Three death variants: the murderer locks the door and monologues ("I'm sick and tired of your snooping..."), ambushes you as you enter their room ("Let me satisfy your curiosity, inspector..."), and — if you're carrying the weapon — "grabs the «weapon» from your hands, and coshes you over the head . . . . I guess that wraps up this investigation!"
  • A literal Deadline: debug field — the threat is a turn countdown, with Murderer loc: tracked (the stalking is real pursuit).
  • Wrong-accusation taunts that reveal what you got wrong: right person/wrong room gets "You fool!! Yes, I used the «weapon» to get rid of «victim». But I did it in the «room», not here in the «room»!" — right room/no weapon gets "You've got the right room, but I was the one who used the «weapon». Very slipshod of you inspector."
  • The gathered scene: "Everyone is waiting with baited breath to hear your accusation!" and "The door is now locked."
  • The rank line: "The case is closed after «N» moves. You now rank as a «rank»."
  • Bonus fossils: "No talking is allowed in this corridor" (blind-corridor flavor!), the title-screen letter-drawing coordinate paths, embedded machine code (the sound routine), and the original's own "Random Number Seed" prompt — Eric Miller was seeding mysteries in 1983 exactly like our ?seed=.
Building it:
bash_toolImplement the complete endgame: gather, accuse, threat machine, ranks
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - > /tmp/p.log 2>&1 << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:70]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

rep("SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 4",
    "SLEUTH remaster \u2014 engine.js \u2014 Phase 2, Milestone 5")
rep('print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 4", 0x70);',
    'print(0, 1, "SLEUTH \\u00b7 milestone 5", 0x70);')

# ---- world state for the endgame ----
rep('''  alibis: {},         // suspect key -> {withKey, withName, room, told}
};''',
'''  alibis: {},         // suspect key -> {withKey, withName, room, told}
  gathered: false,    // ASSEMBLE has been called; the door is locked
  gatherRoom: null,
  deadline: 0,        // turn on which the murderer acts (EXE: "Deadline:")
  threat: 0,          // 0 calm, 1 suspicious, 2 stalking
  over: null,         // null | "won" | "lost"
};''')

rep('''  world.blind = false;
  generateAlibis();
}''',
'''  world.blind = false;
  world.gathered = false;
  world.gatherRoom = null;
  world.deadline = 220;        // tunable; the original's is compiled-in
  world.threat = 0;
  world.over = null;
  generateAlibis();
}

/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   5d. THE ENDGAME — the deadline, the stalking, three ways to die,
       GATHER, ACCUSE, and the ranks
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------
   The EXE's debug overlay ("Murderer: / Murder room: / Weapon: /
   Murderer loc: / Deadline:") tells us the original tracked a turn
   deadline and the murderer's position. Reconstruction: as the
   deadline nears, the murderer grows suspicious, then stalks — moving
   toward you — and if the clock runs out or the stalker corners you,
   one of the three recovered death scenes plays. Taking the murder
   weapon shortens your remaining time. */

const weaponName = () => world.objects[world.weaponRoom].name;
const murdererS = () => world.suspects.find((s) => s.key === world.murderer.key);

function gameOver(result) {
  world.over = result;
  say("Do you want to quit or to restart?");
  redraw();
}

function die(variant) {
  const m = murdererS(), w = weaponName(), v = world.victim.first;
  if (variant === "cosh") {
    say(`In a flash, ${m.first} locks the door behind you, grabs the ` +
        `${w} from your hands, and coshes you over the head . . . .`);
    say("I guess that wraps up this investigation!");
  } else if (variant === "ambush") {
    say(`${m.first} quietly locks the door behind you as you enter the ` +
        `${roomName(locOf(state.roomId))}.  "Let me satisfy your ` +
        `curiosity inspector.  I killed ${v} with the ${w} and now I ` +
        `intend to dispose of you!  Goodbye, inspector!!"`);
  } else {
    say(`${m.first} locks the door and turns toward you.  "I'm sick and ` +
        `tired of your snooping.  I killed ${v} with the ${w} and now ` +
        `I'm going to get rid of you!  I'm afraid that your ` +
        `investigation is over!!"`);
  }
  gameOver("lost");
}

// one BFS step along the guest graph, toward the player
function stalkStep() {
  const m = murdererS();
  const targetLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  if (!NPC_GRAPH[targetLoc]) return;          // player in a hall: no fix
  if (m.room === targetLoc) {
    die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "corner");
    return;
  }
  const prev = { [m.room]: null }, queue = [m.room];
  while (queue.length) {
    const n = queue.shift();
    if (n === targetLoc) break;
    for (const nb of NPC_GRAPH[n] || [])
      if (!(nb in prev)) { prev[nb] = n; queue.push(nb); }
  }
  if (!(targetLoc in prev)) return;
  let step = targetLoc;
  while (prev[step] !== m.room) step = prev[step];
  m.room = step;
  if (m.room === targetLoc) die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "corner");
}

function threatCheck() {
  const remaining = world.deadline - world.turn;
  if (world.threat < 1 && remaining <= 90) {
    world.threat = 1;
    say("The murderer has grown suspicious of your investigation!");
  }
  if (world.threat < 2 && remaining <= 40) {
    world.threat = 2;
    say("The murderer is now stalking you!");
  }
  if (remaining <= 0 && !world.over) { die("corner"); return; }
  if (world.threat >= 2 && !world.over) stalkStep();
}''')

# ---- tick: freeze when gathered/over; run the threat machine ----
rep('''function tick() {
  world.turn++;''',
'''function tick() {
  if (world.over) return;
  world.turn++;
  if (world.gathered) return;               // everyone waits, breath baited
  threatCheck();
  if (world.over) return;''')

# ---- movement/commands gated on endgame states ----
rep('''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;''',
'''function tryMove(dr, dc) {
  if (world.over) return;
  const r = state.row + dr, c = state.col + dc;
  // once the suspects are assembled, the door is locked
  if (world.gathered) {
    const v = cellAt(r, c);
    const dest = v === 0 ? 0 : locOf(v > 20 ? v - 20 : v);
    if (v !== 0 && dest !== world.gatherRoom) {
      say("The door is now locked.");
      redraw();
      return;
    }
  }''')

# ---- player walking into the stalker's room: the ambush ----
rep('''  tick();
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}

function describeCell(force) {''',
'''  tick();
  if (!world.over && world.threat >= 2 &&
      murdererS().room === locOf(state.roomId)) {
    die("ambush");
    return;
  }
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}

function describeCell(force) {''')

# ---- weapon pickup shortens the deadline ----
rep('''    else if (here === world.weaponRoom && world.weaponKnown) {
      world.carrying = obj.name;
      say("You are now carrying the murder weapon.");
    }''',
'''    else if (here === world.weaponRoom && world.weaponKnown) {
      world.carrying = obj.name;
      say("You are now carrying the murder weapon.");
      // the murderer notices: your time shortens
      world.deadline = Math.min(world.deadline, world.turn + 70);
    }''')

# ---- gathered guests: draw the assembly, calm narration ----
rep('''  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room !== locOf(state.roomId)) continue;   // at most one guest
    const pos = house().anchors[String(s.room)] || middleCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    const [ar, ac] = pos;
    if (ar <= 15) put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, ac * 2, "\\u263b", bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }''',
'''  const seatTaken = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room !== locOf(state.roomId)) continue;
    const pos = house().anchors[String(s.room)] || middleCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    let [ar, ac] = pos;
    while (seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] && cellAt(ar, ac + 1) > 0) ac += 1;
    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;                // assembled crowd
    if (ar <= 15) put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, ac * 2, "\\u263b", bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }''')

# ---- GATHER and ACCUSE replace their stubs ----
rep('''    case 4: case 7:
      say("But you haven't even gathered all of the suspects together yet!");
      break;''',
'''    case 4: doGather(); break;                // GATHER / ASSEMBLE
    case 7: doAccuse(input); break;           // ACCUSE''')

rep('''function doHelp() {''',
'''function doGather() {
  if (world.gathered) {
    say("Everyone is waiting with baited breath to hear your accusation!");
    return;
  }
  const loc = locOf(state.roomId);
  if (!(loc >= 1 && loc <= 12)) {
    say("There's no need to observe common courtesies in this house.");
    return;
  }
  for (const s of world.suspects) s.room = loc;
  world.gathered = true;
  world.gatherRoom = loc;
  say("One by one, the houseguests file in. Everyone is waiting with " +
      "baited breath to hear your accusation!  The door is now locked.");
  say(`${pick(world.suspects).first} is staring at the floor.`);
  redraw();
}

function rankFor(turns) {
  return turns <= 100 ? "Holmesian"
       : turns <= 180 ? "Professional" : "Apprentice";
}

function doAccuse(input) {
  if (!world.gathered) {
    say("But you haven't even gathered all of the suspects together yet!");
    return;
  }
  const accused = world.suspects.find((s) => input.includes(s.key));
  if (!accused) {
    say("You must ACCUSE one of the six people in the room:  I'm not " +
        "sure who it is that you're trying to accuse.");
    return;
  }
  const m = murdererS(), w = weaponName(), v = world.victim.first;
  const here = locOf(state.roomId);

  if (accused.key !== m.key) {               // an innocent, wrongly named
    say(`${accused.first} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!"`);
    say("Very slipshod of you inspector.");
    gameOver("lost");
    return;
  }
  if (here !== world.murderRoom) {           // right villain, wrong room
    say(`${m.first} stands up and exclaims, "You fool!!  Yes, I used ` +
        `the ${w} to get rid of ${v}.  But I did it in the ` +
        `${roomName(world.murderRoom)}, not here in the ` +
        `${roomName(here)}!"`);
    gameOver("lost");
    return;
  }
  if (!world.carrying) {                     // right room, empty hands
    say(`${m.first} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!  ` +
        `You've got the right room, but I was the one who used the ` +
        `${w}."`);
    say("Very slipshod of you inspector.");
    gameOver("lost");
    return;
  }
  // person, room, weapon: the confession
  say(`${m.first} ${fillTokens(pick(DIALOGUE.confessions), m)}`);
  say(`The case is closed after ${world.turn} moves.  You now rank ` +
      `as a ${rankFor(world.turn)}!`);
  gameOver("won");
}

function doHelp() {''')

# ---- post-game gating + blind-corridor no-talking flavor ----
rep('''function execute(raw) {
  const input = raw.trim().toUpperCase();
  if (!input) return;
  say("> " + raw.trim());
  const verb = matchVerb(input);''',
'''function execute(raw) {
  const input = raw.trim().toUpperCase();
  if (!input) return;
  say("> " + raw.trim());
  const verb = matchVerb(input);
  if (world.over && verb !== 9 && verb !== 6) {
    say("Do you want to quit or to restart?");
    return;
  }''')

rep('''  if (world.blind) {                     // any key: the drop
    beep();''',
'''  if (world.blind) {                     // any key: the drop
    if (k.length === 1 && /[a-z]/i.test(k))
      say("No talking is allowed in this corridor.");
    beep();''')

rep('''  say("(This build: QUESTION or Q <name> \\u2014 walk up to someone first; " +
      "GUESTS lists the houseguests; KNOCK, RESTART. GATHER/ACCUSE " +
      "arrive in milestone 5.)");''',
'''  say("(All original verbs are live. Solve it before the murderer " +
      "solves you \\u2014 and mind the deadline.)");''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("PATCH_COMPLETE")
PATCH_EOF
cat /tmp/p.log; grep -q PATCH_COMPLETE /tmp/p.log && sed -i 's|milestone 4 — everyone has a story|milestone 5 — the complete game|' web/index.html && node --check web/engine.js && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && echo "BUILD OK"
Output
PATCH_COMPLETE
BUILD OK
bash_toolTest the complete endgame: gather, all accusation outcomes, threat machine, deaths
bash
cd /home/claude/web && node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 777, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const flat = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/&amp;/g,'&').replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(flat().slice(-600)); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };
const g = D.house().grid;
const gotoRoom = (rid) => { outer: for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===rid){state.row=r;state.col=c;state.roomId=rid;break outer;} };

// --- preconditions
type("accuse maude");
assert(flat().includes("haven't even gathered all of the suspects"), "ACCUSE before GATHER blocked");

// --- gather: everyone assembles, door locks
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom === 4 ? 5 : 4);   // some non-murder room
type("gather");
assert(world.gathered && world.suspects.every(s => s.room === locOfTest()), "all six assembled here");
function locOfTest(){ return state.roomId === 13 ? 12 : state.roomId; }
assert(flat().includes("baited breath") && flat().includes("door is now locked"), "gathered scene text");
// try to leave
const before = [state.row, state.col];
for (const k of ["ArrowUp","ArrowDown","ArrowLeft","ArrowRight"]) key(k);
assert(flat().includes("The door is now locked."), "leaving blocked after gather");

// --- accuse an innocent: lose
const innocent = world.suspects.find(s => s.key !== world.murderer.key);
type("accuse " + innocent.key);
assert(flat().includes(`${innocent.first} rises and declares angrily`) &&
       flat().includes("You're wrong!!") && flat().includes("slipshod"), "innocent accusation: angry denial + slipshod");
assert(world.over === "lost" && flat().includes("quit or to restart"), "lost + prompt");
type("examine floor");
assert(flat().includes("Do you want to quit or to restart?"), "post-game commands gated");
type("restart");
assert(!world.over && !world.gathered, "restart clears endgame state");

// --- accuse murderer in the wrong room: the You fool taunt
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom === 4 ? 5 : 4);
type("gather"); type("accuse " + world.murderer.key);
assert(flat().includes("You fool!!") && flat().includes("But I did it in the " + 
       D.house().rooms[String(world.murderRoom)].name), "wrong room: taunt reveals real room");
assert(world.over === "lost", "wrong room loses");
type("restart");

// --- right room, empty hands
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("gather"); type("accuse " + world.murderer.key);
assert(flat().includes("right room, but I was the one who used the"), "right room, no weapon: slipshod taunt");
type("restart");

// --- THE FULL WIN: glass -> weapon -> murder room -> gather -> accuse
world.hasGlass = true;                      // (glass fetch tested in M2)
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wkey = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
type("examine " + wkey); type("get " + wkey);
assert(world.carrying, "carrying the weapon");
assert(world.deadline <= world.turn + 70, "deadline shortened on pickup");
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("gather");
type("accuse " + world.murderer.key);
assert(world.over === "won", "correct accusation wins");
assert(flat().includes(world.murderer.first) &&
       /I killed|game is over|never meant|Naturally I killed|found me out|suspect me|deeply regret/.test(flat()),
       "confession from the banked ALIBI2 pool");
assert(flat().includes("The case is closed after") && /rank as a (Holmesian|Professional|Apprentice)/.test(flat()), "rank awarded");
type("restart");

// --- the threat machine: suspicion, stalking, death by deadline
world.deadline = world.turn + 95;
key("ArrowUp"); key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowUp"); key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowUp"); key("ArrowDown");
assert(flat().includes("grown suspicious of your investigation"), "suspicion at deadline-90");
world.deadline = world.turn + 41;
key("ArrowUp"); key("ArrowDown");
assert(flat().includes("The murderer is now stalking you!"), "stalking at deadline-40");
// stalker closes in and kills (player idles in a room)
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom === 1 ? 3 : 1);
for (let i = 0; i < 60 && !world.over; i++) key(i % 2 ? "ArrowUp" : "ArrowDown");
assert(world.over === "lost", "the stalker gets you");
assert(/sick and tired of your snooping|Goodbye, inspector|coshes you over the head/.test(flat()), "authentic death scene");
type("restart");

// --- cosh variant: carrying the weapon when caught
world.hasGlass = true;
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wk2 = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
type("examine " + wk2); type("get " + wk2);
world.deadline = world.turn;                 // out of time now
key("ArrowUp");
assert(world.over === "lost" && flat().includes("coshes you over the head"), "cosh death when carrying");
console.log("\nALL M5 ENDGAME TESTS PASSED");
EOF
Output

ok: innocent accusation: angry denial + slipshod
ok: lost + prompt

ok: restart clears endgame state
ok: wrong room: taunt reveals real room
ok: wrong room loses
ok: right room, no weapon: slipshod taunt
ok: carrying the weapon
ok: deadline shortened on pickup
ok: correct accusation wins
ok: confession from the banked ALIBI2 pool
ok: rank awarded
ok: suspicion at deadline-90
ok: stalking at deadline-40
ok: the stalker gets you
ok: authentic death scene
█ █ █ █ █ █ █ █▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█ █▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█ █ █ █ █ █ █ █▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █───▄ █ █ █ ▄───█ █ █ █───█ █ █ █ █───█ █ █ █───█ █ █ █ █ █───█ █ ████████████████████ ████████████████ ███████████████████████████████████████ SECRET PASSAGEWAY carrying: glass + obelisk turn 1 The murderer is now stalking you! Edward locks the door and turns toward you. "I'm sick and tired of your snooping. I killed Victoria with the obelisk and now I'm going to get rid of you! I'm afraid that your investigation is over!!" Do you want to quit or to restart? > _
stderr
FAIL: cosh death when carrying
exit 1
Thinking · Debugging test failures and tracing deadline logic.
Most of the test cases are passing, but there's a logic issue with the game state after restart. When the game restarts, the turn resets to 0 and deadline gets set to 220, but then I'm executing commands like examining and picking up items—each of which should advance the turn counter. The problem is that I'm getting the "corner" variant instead of the expected "cosh" variant, which suggests the sequence of state changes isn't matching what the test expects. Looking at the trace, the issue is in how I handle deadline expiry: when the deadline runs out, I'm calling die("corner") unconditionally, but I should be checking whether the player is carrying a weapon first—if they are, it should trigger the "cosh" variant instead. The fix is straightforward: change the deadline-expiry death check to use die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "corner"), matching the logic already in place elsewhere. Let me apply that one-line fix and retest.

20 of 21 — the deadline-expiry death ignores what you're carrying. When time runs out with the weapon in hand, it should be the cosh. One line:

bash_toolFix cosh-at-deadline variant, verify both endings
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 -c "
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
src = src.replace('''  if (remaining <= 0 && !world.over) { die(\"corner\"); return; }''',
'''  if (remaining <= 0 && !world.over) {
    die(world.carrying ? \"cosh\" : \"corner\");
    return;
  }''')
open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print('fixed')" && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && cd web && node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 777, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const flat = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };
const g = D.house().grid;
const gotoRoom = (rid) => { outer: for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===rid){state.row=r;state.col=c;state.roomId=rid;break outer;} };

world.hasGlass = true;
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wk = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
type("examine " + wk); type("get " + wk);
world.deadline = world.turn;
key("ArrowUp");
assert(world.over === "lost" && flat().includes("coshes you over the head") &&
       flat().includes("wraps up this investigation"), "cosh death when carrying at deadline");
// and a final full-victory replay for good measure
type("restart");
world.hasGlass = true;
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wk2 = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
type("examine " + wk2); type("get " + wk2);
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("gather"); type("accuse " + world.murderer.key);
assert(world.over === "won" && /rank as a/.test(flat()), "full victory replay");
console.log("\nALL M5 TESTS GREEN");
EOF

Thinking · Debugged shell escaping issues and verified file modifications.
The replacement might not have worked because the shell is mangling the quotes — I'm using double quotes around the Python code with escaped inner quotes, which could cause issues. The fact that it printed "fixed" suggests the replace command ran but possibly didn't match anything since it's silent when there's no match. I should verify whether the file actually changed.
bash_toolCheck whether the deadline fix actually landed
bash
cd /home/claude && grep -n 'remaining <= 0' web/engine.js
Output
358:  if (remaining <= 0 && !world.over) {
Thinking · Diagnosed test failure root cause and devised iterative solution.
The fix did land with the multi-line form, so I'm tracing through why the test still failed. Starting fresh with seed 777, the sequence is cleaner than before—no restarts cluttering things up. I set world.hasGlass to true, move to the weapon room, examine and get the item. The examine correctly shows blood in the room with glass present, and the get succeeds, so I'm carrying it. Now the deadline gets set to the minimum of 220 or the current turn plus 70, and I'm checking what happens next. When I press the up arrow, tryMove attempts to move but might hit a wall immediately—and here's the issue: gotoRoom picks the first cell (top-left) of the room, so moving up from that position could be blocked by a wall above it. If tryMove returns early without ticking, there's no death, world.over stays null, and the test fails. The earlier long test passed because that particular seed and state sequence led to a different random number draw, giving a different weapon room layout with a walkable path upward. Since the fresh run uses the same seed but a different state sequence, the RNG draws differently and produces a different room configuration. The fix is to loop through different directions—down, up, left, right—until either the world ends or the turn counter increments, guaranteeing at least one successful move.
Thinking · Finalizing README, packaging milestone five, celebrating project completion.
The test confirmed the arrow key isn't triggering movement. I'll now test all directions to verify the input handling is broken across the board.
bash_toolRetest cosh death with guaranteed movement tick
bash
cd /home/claude/web && node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 777, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const flat = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(flat().slice(-400)); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };
const g = D.house().grid;
const gotoRoom = (rid) => { outer: for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===rid){state.row=r;state.col=c;state.roomId=rid;break outer;} };
const nudge = () => { const t = world.turn; for (const k of ["ArrowDown","ArrowUp","ArrowLeft","ArrowRight"]) { key(k); if (world.turn > t) return; } };

world.hasGlass = true;
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wk = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
type("examine " + wk); type("get " + wk);
assert(world.carrying, "carrying");
world.deadline = world.turn;
nudge();
assert(world.over === "lost" && flat().includes("coshes you over the head") &&
       flat().includes("wraps up this investigation"), "cosh death when carrying at deadline");
type("restart");
world.hasGlass = true;
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wk2 = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
type("examine " + wk2); type("get " + wk2);
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("gather"); type("accuse " + world.murderer.key);
assert(world.over === "won" && /rank as a/.test(flat()), "full victory replay");
console.log("\nALL M5 TESTS GREEN");
EOF
Output
ok: carrying
ok: cosh death when carrying at deadline
ok: full victory replay

ALL M5 TESTS GREEN
Thinking · Orchestrated final milestone steps and project completion arc.
Everything's working well. Now I'm wrapping up the final pieces: updating the README with the M5 section and noting Phase 2's completion, packaging M5, preparing to present, and closing out the project. This marks a real milestone—the full arc from Phase 1's extraction work through Phase 2's complete playable remaster, with Phase 3 (custom content) left mostly as documentation. I want to keep the tone warm and celebratory without overdoing it.

The game is complete. Final README, packaging:

bash_toolUpdate README with M5 notes and package the complete game
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
r = open('web/README_PHASE2.md').read()
r = r.replace('''**Milestone 4 — the mystery:**''',
'''**Milestone 5 — the endgame:** GATHER locks the door, ACCUSE has four
outcomes, the deadline machine escalates from suspicion to stalking to
three recovered death scenes, and victory earns a rank. The game is
complete and winnable — or losable, in every original way.

**Milestone 4 — the mystery:**''')
r = r.replace('''## Guest distribution (M4.5 field notes)''',
'''## The endgame (M5 design notes)

The EXE's own debug overlay gave away the architecture: `Murderer: /
Murder room: / Weapon: / Murderer loc: / Deadline:` — a turn deadline
and a tracked murderer position. Reconstruction: as the deadline
nears, "The murderer has grown suspicious of your investigation!",
then "The murderer is now stalking you!" — at which point the
murderer BFS-pursues you through the guest graph. Get cornered, walk
into their room, or run out the clock and one of three recovered
death scenes plays; the cosh variant ("grabs the «weapon» from your
hands…") fires if you're carrying. Taking the weapon shortens your
remaining time. The deadline length and thresholds are tunables — the
original's constants are compiled-in.

ACCUSE resolves four ways, all in the original's words: accuse an
innocent ("rises and declares angrily, 'You're wrong!!' … Very
slipshod of you inspector."), the murderer in the wrong room ("You
fool!! Yes, I used the «weapon» to get rid of «victim». But I did it
in the «room», not here…"), the right room empty-handed ("You've got
the right room, but I was the one who used the «weapon»."), or
everything right — which cashes in one of the seven banked ALIBI2
confessions, closes the case after N moves, and ranks you Holmesian,
Professional, or Apprentice. GATHER assembles everyone ("Everyone is
waiting with baited breath to hear your accusation!") and locks the
door — no leaving once the theatre begins. Also wired: "No talking is
allowed in this corridor," for anyone who tries to chat in the blind
passage. And a period footnote: the EXE contains a "Random Number
Seed" prompt — the original asked players for a seed in 1983, exactly
the mechanism our `?seed=` URL reproduces.

## Guest distribution (M4.5 field notes)''')
r = r.replace('''- **M5 — Endgame:** GATHER, ACCUSE, the suspicion timer, win/lose,
  ranks (Apprentice / Professional / Holmesian).
- **M6 — Your game:**''',
'''- ~~**M5 — Endgame**~~ — done. The remaster is a complete game.
- **M6 — Your game:**''')
open('web/README_PHASE2.md','w').write(r)
print("README updated")
EOF
cd web && cp sleuth_playable.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/ && zip -q /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m5.zip index.html engine.js data_shared.js data_v1.js data_v2.js gen_web_data.py README_PHASE2.md sleuth_playable.html && echo packaged && wc -l engine.js

packaged
1174 engine.js

json

  "filepaths": [
    "/mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_playable.html",
    "/mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m5.zip"
  ]
}
Output
[local_resource block]
[local_resource block]

Milestone 5 delivered — Sleuth is a complete, winnable, losable game again.

The endgame, from the EXE's own blueprint. The debug overlay Eric Miller left in the binary (Murderer loc: / Deadline:) gave away the architecture: a turn deadline and a physically tracked murderer. As time runs down, "The murderer has grown suspicious of your investigation!" escalates to "The murderer is now stalking you!" — at which point the murderer BFS-pursues you through the guest graph, and picking up the weapon shortens your clock. Get cornered, walk into their room, or run out of time, and one of the three recovered death scenes plays; carry the weapon into your doom and it's the cosh — "grabs the «weapon» from your hands, and coshes you over the head . . . . I guess that wraps up this investigation!"

ACCUSE resolves four ways, all in original words. Name an innocent and they rise angrily ("You're wrong!!" ... "Very slipshod of you inspector."). Accuse the murderer in the wrong room and they taunt you with the truth ("You fool!! Yes, I used the obelisk to get rid of Victoria. But I did it in the library, not here!"). Right room, empty hands: "You've got the right room, but I was the one who used the «weapon»." Get everything right — person, room, weapon in hand, suspects gathered behind a locked door with baited breath — and one of the seven banked ALIBI2 confessions finally pays off, the case closes after N moves, and you rank as Holmesian, Professional, or Apprentice.

Small touches that made it in: "No talking is allowed in this corridor" for anyone who tries chatting in the blind passage, and my favorite historical footnote — the EXE contains a "Random Number Seed (-32768 to 32767)" prompt. Eric Miller was asking players to seed their mysteries in 1983; our ?seed= URL is the same idea, forty-three years apart.

The tally: ~1,170 lines of engine driving content that is entirely 1983's own — every room, weapon, alibi, insult, confession, and death scene recovered from the original files, the screen blitted from the original video memory, corrected along the way by your DOSBox fieldwork. Phase 1's spec plus Phase 2's five milestones, and the thing your teenage self text-edited names into is now something you can read end to end, test headlessly, and reseed at will.

What remains is M6 — your game: mostly documentation, since the engine is already data-driven. A third house is a JSON file: draw a 40×17 grid, write descriptions, a cast of seven, 33 weapons in 3×11 sets, and register it. That's also where your original ambition lands — custom layouts, casts, and weapons — and honestly, where a Spider & Prey murder evening at a certain tavern in Spider City practically writes itself. Whenever you're ready.

2026-07-02 11:35
You

A few things that don't match the original game, see if you can check the original again:

1) The murderer doesn't ever jump the detective outside of a room. It's only when the detective enters a room that it happens.

2) The movement of the suspects/guests feels very abrupt in that they can vanish or change position with each step the detective takes.

I'm also wondering, is it possible for you to get DOSBox and run the game yourself and play it in your sandboxed environment to observe the behavior that may not be present in the static files, or is it something where I have to provide the environment and it's not doable in your present Claude.AI arrangement?

2026-07-03 00:01
Claude
Thinking · Thinking about correcting game behavior discrepancies and clarifying technical capabilities for running legacy software.
The user is asking whether I can run DOSBox in the sandbox, and I'm realizing that won't work since there's no network access to install it. I'm considering whether I could build a minimal 8086 emulator instead to execute the SLEUTH.EXE file, though that would be quite complex. Actually, the more practical approach is to leverage what's already possible: the user's own observations from running the game are essentially the dynamic analysis I'd need, and I could offer static analysis through disassembly of the compiled BASIC code instead. Their observational reports have been detailed enough that we can work with that. Let me check if DOSBox is even available in this environment first. For the behavioral fixes, the key insight is that the murderer's ambush only triggers when the detective enters a room—not while standing still or moving through corridors. So the death scenes (whether the stalking ambush or the "locks the door and turns toward you" variant) should only fire on room entry, meaning the murderer reaching your room doesn't immediately kill; instead, it arms a trap that executes the next time you enter any room. Refining the implementation... So the death trigger is strictly when the detective enters a room — corridors are safe. The V1 variant with the door-locking works for deadline expiry at the next room entry, while the ambush text applies when entering a room where the stalker is already waiting; the cosh variant handles cases where the murderer is carrying it. Now I'm thinking through how guest movement should feel — they shouldn't jitter visibly with every step like they're teleporting around; the original had a more measured, less frantic quality to their repositioning. I'm lowering the movement chance significantly and putting guests on a slower stochastic clock so they only consider moving every few turns. Guests in the detective's current room stay put while being questioned — they're engaged and visible, so movement happens off-screen where it won't feel abrupt. The stalking murderer is an exception and keeps hunting, though it won't kill until the detective re-enters a room. This preserves the original's feel of guests being relatively stable presences while still allowing them to wander naturally between rooms. For implementation, I'm modifying the tick function to skip movement checks for NPCs in the player's room and setting the movement chance to around 0.05. The stalkStep function becomes unnecessary since the murderer's pursuit is now integrated into the regular movement system with the same freezing logic. When the threat deadline expires, I'll set the world threat level to 3 to indicate the murderer is lying in wait, then defer the actual death until the player enters a room. The death check needs to move from the post-tick phase into the room-entry logic — I'll detect when the player's location changes into an actual room (1-12) and trigger death if threat is 2 or higher and the murderer is in that room. For threat level 3, the murderer intercepts regardless of position since he's been tracking the player, matching the original text about locking the door. Even the blind-corridor teleport counts as a room entry, so it could trigger the death condition too, which feels consistent and darkly amusing. I'll extract a helper function to check for ambush conditions that both the normal movement and blind-drop handlers can use. The stairs and landing are technically location 12 but shouldn't trigger ambush deaths — only rooms 1-11 are killable locations, keeping the murderer confined to actual rooms rather than corridors. Then I need to verify the behavior with tests: stalking in a room should kill on re-entry, deadline expiry mid-corridor should defer death until the next room, guests should stay put when in the player's room, and the win path should still work cleanly. For DOSBox, I'll check if it's available and report honestly that it can't be installed due to network restrictions.

Two good catches. Let me answer the DOSBox question honestly first (with a quick check), then verify what the static evidence says about your observations — because one of them, it turns out, was written down all along:

bash_toolCheck for emulators in sandbox; pull the death-scene trigger evidence
bash
which dosbox dosbox-x dosemu qemu-system-i386 2>&1; echo "---"; cd /home/claude 2>/dev/null && ls web/engine.js >/dev/null 2>&1 || (mkdir -p /home/claude/sleuth /home/claude/web && cd /home/claude && unzip -qo /mnt/user-data/uploads/sleuth.zip -d sleuth && cp /mnt/user-data/outputs/extract_sleuth.py . && cd web && unzip -qo /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m5.zip && cp gen_web_data.py .. && cd .. && python3 extract_sleuth.py sleuth extracted > /dev/null && echo RESTORED); cd /home/claude && grep -n "as you enter the\|locks the door and turns\|coshes" extracted/engine_strings_sleuth.txt

Thinking · Reconciling trigger mechanics with room-entry death conditions.
The network limitation means I can't test with emulators, so I'll work with what I can verify directly. The user's observation about the trigger is right there in the text—"quietly locks the door behind you as you enter"—so the death condition should only activate on room entry when there's a threat level of 2 or higher and the murderer is present, causing an ambush or attack. I'm implementing these two fixes now. For guest behavior, I'm freezing guests who are already in rooms and reducing their movement rate from 0.12 to 0.05 when they're moving between spaces. The murderer will hold their position when they're in the same room as the player, creating a stalking mechanic rather than constant pursuit.
Thinking · Identified erroneous mid-corridor assassin behavior and committed to corrections.
I found that line 150 shows the trigger condition is built into the death scene itself, confirming your observation was correct—and I've identified that I created a mid-corridor assassin the original didn't have. I'll fix both behaviors now.
bash_toolFix kill trigger to room-entry only and stabilize guest movement
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - > /tmp/p.log 2>&1 << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:70]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

# ---- 1. guests: slower clock, and never move while you watch ----
rep('''  // NPC_MOVE_CHANCE is a remaster tunable: the original's true rate is
  // compiled-in and only measurable by watching DOSBox sessions
  const NPC_MOVE_CHANCE = 0.12;
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (rng() < NPC_MOVE_CHANCE) {''',
'''  // NPC_MOVE_CHANCE is a remaster tunable: the original's true rate is
  // compiled-in and only measurable by watching DOSBox sessions.
  // Guests in YOUR room never move — all wandering happens off-screen,
  // so the only visible transition is an arrival. (Playtest note: the
  // original never showed guests blinking out from under you.)
  const NPC_MOVE_CHANCE = 0.05;
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room === locOf(state.roomId)) continue;   // engaged: stays put
    if (rng() < NPC_MOVE_CHANCE) {''')

# ---- 2. the stalker closes in but NEVER kills mid-stride ----
rep('''// one BFS step along the guest graph, toward the player
function stalkStep() {
  const m = murdererS();
  const targetLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  if (!NPC_GRAPH[targetLoc]) return;          // player in a hall: no fix
  if (m.room === targetLoc) {
    die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "corner");
    return;
  }
  const prev = { [m.room]: null }, queue = [m.room];
  while (queue.length) {
    const n = queue.shift();
    if (n === targetLoc) break;
    for (const nb of NPC_GRAPH[n] || [])
      if (!(nb in prev)) { prev[nb] = n; queue.push(nb); }
  }
  if (!(targetLoc in prev)) return;
  let step = targetLoc;
  while (prev[step] !== m.room) step = prev[step];
  m.room = step;
  if (m.room === targetLoc) die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "corner");
}''',
'''// one BFS step along the guest graph, toward the player. The stalker
// positions but never strikes here: the kill only ever happens when
// the DETECTIVE walks into a room — the death scene says so itself
// ("...locks the door behind you AS YOU ENTER the..."). Corridors,
// halls and the landing are safe ground.
function stalkStep() {
  const m = murdererS();
  const targetLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  if (!NPC_GRAPH[targetLoc] || m.room === targetLoc) return;   // waits
  const prev = { [m.room]: null }, queue = [m.room];
  while (queue.length) {
    const n = queue.shift();
    if (n === targetLoc) break;
    for (const nb of NPC_GRAPH[n] || [])
      if (!(nb in prev)) { prev[nb] = n; queue.push(nb); }
  }
  if (!(targetLoc in prev)) return;
  let step = targetLoc;
  while (prev[step] !== m.room) step = prev[step];
  m.room = step;
}''')

# ---- 3. deadline expiry arms a trap; it springs at your next room entry ----
rep('''  if (remaining <= 0 && !world.over) {
    die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "corner");
    return;
  }
  if (world.threat >= 2 && !world.over) stalkStep();''',
'''  if (world.threat < 3 && remaining <= 0) world.threat = 3;  // in wait
  if (world.threat >= 2 && !world.over) stalkStep();''')

# ---- 4. the single kill trigger: entering a room ----
rep('''  tick();
  if (!world.over && world.threat >= 2 &&
      murdererS().room === locOf(state.roomId)) {
    die("ambush");
    return;
  }
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}

function describeCell(force) {''',
'''  const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  tick();
  if (checkAmbush(prevLoc)) return;
  describeCell(false);
  redraw();
}

/* The one and only kill trigger: the detective ENTERING a room
   (1-11). At threat 2 the stalking murderer must be waiting inside;
   at threat 3 (deadline passed) the murderer intercepts wherever you
   enter next. Carrying the weapon always earns the cosh. */
function checkAmbush(prevLoc) {
  if (world.over || world.threat < 2) return false;
  const v = cellAt(state.row, state.col);
  const newLoc = locOf(v > 20 ? v - 20 : v);
  if (!(newLoc >= 1 && newLoc <= 11) || newLoc === prevLoc) return false;
  if (world.threat >= 3) {
    die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "corner");
    return true;
  }
  if (murdererS().room === newLoc) {
    die(world.carrying ? "cosh" : "ambush");
    return true;
  }
  return false;
}

function describeCell(force) {''')

# ---- 5. the blind-corridor drop is also a room entry ----
rep('''    [state.row, state.col] = pick(cells);
    state.roomId = null;                 // force a fresh room announce
    tick();
    describeCell(false);
    redraw();''',
'''    const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
    [state.row, state.col] = pick(cells);
    state.roomId = null;                 // force a fresh room announce
    tick();
    if (checkAmbush(prevLoc)) { e.preventDefault(); return; }
    describeCell(false);
    redraw();''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("PATCH_COMPLETE")
PATCH_EOF
cat /tmp/p.log; grep -q PATCH_COMPLETE /tmp/p.log && node --check web/engine.js && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && echo "BUILD OK"

PATCH_COMPLETE
BUILD OK
bash_toolVerify room-entry-only kills, safe corridors, stable watched guests
bash
cd /home/claude/web && node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 555, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const flat = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(flat().slice(-500)); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };
const g = D.house().grid;
const M = () => world.suspects.find(s => s.key === world.murderer.key);

// --- 1. guests in your room are rock-stable
const guest = world.suspects[0];
guest.room = 4; state.roomId = 4;
const seat = D.house().anchors["4"]; state.row = seat[0]; state.col = seat[1];
for (let i = 0; i < 400; i++) key(i % 2 ? "ArrowUp" : "ArrowDown");
assert(guest.room === 4, "a watched guest never vanishes (400 turns in shared room)");

// --- 2. corridors and halls are safe at stalking level, forever
D.execute("restart");
world.threat = 2; world.deadline = world.turn + 39;
// stand in the main hall (loc 15, not a kill zone), murderer adjacent
state.roomId = 15; state.row = 8; state.col = 10;
M().room = 4;
for (let i = 0; i < 300 && !world.over; i++) key(i % 2 ? "ArrowUp" : "ArrowDown");
assert(!world.over, "no mid-corridor kill: stalking murderer never strikes in halls");
assert(world.threat === 3, "deadline expiry arms the trap instead of killing");

// --- 3. the trap springs exactly at the next room entry
// walk from the hall into the dining room via its door at (2,8)
while (state.row > 2) key("ArrowUp");
while (state.col > 9) key("ArrowLeft");
key("ArrowLeft"); key("ArrowLeft");           // door, then dining
assert(world.over === "lost", "threat-3 murderer intercepts at next room entry");
assert(flat().includes("locks the door and turns toward you"), "corner variant on interception");
type("restart");

// --- 4. stalking ambush: only when entering the murderer's room
world.threat = 2; world.deadline = world.turn + 500;
M().room = 1;                                  // waits in the dining room
state.roomId = 15; state.row = 2; state.col = 9;   // hall outside
// walking the hall past the dining door: safe
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) { key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowUp"); }
assert(!world.over, "walking past the killer's door is safe");
key("ArrowLeft"); if (!world.over) key("ArrowLeft");   // step in
assert(world.over === "lost" && flat().includes("as you enter the"),
       "ambush fires on room entry, with the authentic 'as you enter' line");
type("restart");

// --- 5. murderer entering YOUR room does not kill; re-entry does
world.threat = 2; world.deadline = world.turn + 500;
state.roomId = 3;                              // living room
const s3 = D.house().anchors["3"]; state.row = s3[0]; state.col = s3[1];
M().room = 3;                                  // stalker walks in on you
for (let i = 0; i < 20 && !world.over; i++) key(i % 2 ? "ArrowUp" : "ArrowDown");
assert(!world.over, "stalker sharing your room does not strike (you were here first)");
console.log("\nALL BEHAVIOR-CORRECTION TESTS PASSED");
EOF
Output
ok: a watched guest never vanishes (400 turns in shared room)
ok: no mid-corridor kill: stalking murderer never strikes in halls
ok: deadline expiry arms the trap instead of killing
ok: threat-3 murderer intercepts at next room entry
ok: corner variant on interception
ok: walking past the killer's door is safe
▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀▀█ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █───▄ █ █ █ ▄───█ █ █ █───█ █ █ █ █───█ █ █ █───█ █ █ █ █ █───█ █ ████████████████████ ████████████████ ███████████████████████████████████████ DINING ROOM turn 22 You are now in the dining room. A large oak table dominates the room. A silver serving set sits on a smaller table in one corner of the room. A rather large candelabra sits in the middle of the table. A magnifying glass is lying on the floor. Earl Crompton is examining the silver serving set. > _

FAIL: ambush fires on room entry, with the authentic 'as you enter' line

Thinking · Diagnosing why the ambush trigger misfires.
I'm debugging why the ambush isn't triggering when the player enters the dining room where Earl (the murderer) is waiting. The test confirms Earl is present in the right location and the threat level is set to 2, but checkAmbush isn't firing — so something in its conditions must be failing. So in tryMove I'm computing prevLoc from the stale roomId before describeCell updates it, which correctly captures the room before the move. The ArrowLeft from (2,9) to (2,8) takes the normal walkable path and reaches the end where checkAmbush runs. During tick(), threatCheck() keeps threat at 2 since the deadline hasn't passed, then stalkStep() tries to move the murderer toward the player's location—but since roomId is still 15 (a hall, not in the NPC graph), the pathfinding returns early without advancing the murderer. But here's the issue: during those 20 ticks of wandering, the murderer isn't locked in place—the regular wander loop runs for all suspects including the murderer, and with a 5% chance per tick to move, there's roughly a 64% chance the murderer drifted away from room 1 entirely. So when the player finally enters, the murderer might not be there anymore, which would explain why the ambush didn't trigger even though the threat level and position checks should have fired it. The fix is to prevent the murderer from random wandering when threat is high enough—they should only move via the stalking logic, which can't path to the hall anyway and would keep them frozen in place. I'll also harden the test by setting the murderer's room right before the player steps in, and I should verify that when the murderer is actually in the player's room, the engagement logic keeps them there and blocks both the wander and stalk movements.
Thinking · Identified and resolved threat-level logic flaw affecting murderer behavior.
I found that the murderer wanders away before the ambush can trigger at threat level 2, which breaks the stalking behavior. I'll fix this by making threat ≥ 2 use only pursuit logic instead of the random-wander loop.
bash_toolExclude stalking murderer from wander; full behavior verification
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 -c "
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
old = '''  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room === locOf(state.roomId)) continue;   // engaged: stays put
    if (rng() < NPC_MOVE_CHANCE) {'''
new = '''  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room === locOf(state.roomId)) continue;   // engaged: stays put
    if (world.threat >= 2 && s.key === world.murderer.key) continue;
    if (rng() < NPC_MOVE_CHANCE) {'''
assert old in src
open('web/engine.js','w').write(src.replace(old, new))
print('patched: stalking murderer moves only via pursuit')
" && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && cd web && node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 555, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const flat = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(flat().slice(-400)); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };
const M = () => world.suspects.find(s => s.key === world.murderer.key);

// stable watched guest
const guest = world.suspects.find(s => s.key !== world.murderer.key);
guest.room = 4; state.roomId = 4;
const seat = D.house().anchors["4"]; state.row = seat[0]; state.col = seat[1];
for (let i = 0; i < 400; i++) key(i % 2 ? "ArrowUp" : "ArrowDown");
assert(guest.room === 4, "watched guest never vanishes (400 turns)");

// corridors safe at stalking; deadline arms instead of kills
D.execute("restart");
world.threat = 2; world.deadline = world.turn + 39;
state.roomId = 15; state.row = 8; state.col = 10;
M().room = 4;
for (let i = 0; i < 300 && !world.over; i++) key(i % 2 ? "ArrowUp" : "ArrowDown");
assert(!world.over && world.threat === 3, "halls safe forever; deadline arms the trap");

// trap springs at next room entry
while (state.row > 2) key("ArrowUp");
while (state.col > 9 && !world.over) key("ArrowLeft");
if (!world.over) { key("ArrowLeft"); key("ArrowLeft"); }
assert(world.over === "lost" && flat().includes("locks the door and turns toward you"),
       "threat-3 interception at next room entry (corner variant)");
type("restart");

// stalking ambush only on entering the murderer's room
world.threat = 2; world.deadline = world.turn + 500;
M().room = 1;
state.roomId = 15; state.row = 2; state.col = 9;
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) { key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowUp"); }
assert(!world.over && M().room === 1, "stalker holds position; walking past the door is safe");
key("ArrowLeft");
assert(world.over === "lost" && flat().includes("as you enter the"),
       "ambush on room entry with the authentic 'as you enter' line");
type("restart");

// stalker entering YOUR room does not strike
world.threat = 2; world.deadline = world.turn + 500;
state.roomId = 3;
const s3 = D.house().anchors["3"]; state.row = s3[0]; state.col = s3[1];
M().room = 3;
for (let i = 0; i < 30 && !world.over; i++) key(i % 2 ? "ArrowUp" : "ArrowDown");
assert(!world.over, "stalker sharing your room does not strike");

// full win regression
type("restart");
world.hasGlass = true;
const g = D.house().grid;
const gotoRoom = (rid) => { outer: for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===rid){state.row=r;state.col=c;state.roomId=rid;break outer;} };
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wk = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
type("examine " + wk); type("get " + wk);
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("gather"); type("accuse " + world.murderer.key);
assert(world.over === "won", "win path regression");
console.log("\nALL BEHAVIOR-CORRECTION TESTS PASSED");
EOF

ok: halls safe forever; deadline arms the trap

ok: stalker holds position; walking past the door is safe
ok: ambush on room entry with the authentic 'as you enter' line
ok: stalker sharing your room does not strike

You've got the right room, but I was the one who used the hourglass." Very slipshod of you inspector. Do you want to quit or to restart? > _
