Thinking · Tracing inventory and room state inconsistencies.
The behavior tests are passing, but there's a regression in the win condition where the accusation logic found the player wasn't carrying the hourglass. I'm tracing through the issue: when gotoRoom teleports to the weapon room and I examine it, the state correctly shows the room ID matches, but the GET action failed to actually add the glass to the player's inventory—it only appears in the dump output. I need to figure out why the examine action succeeded but the subsequent GET didn't register the item as carried. Ah, there's the bug — when I examine the hourglass, the parser is matching "GLASS" as a substring and firing the magnifying glass branch instead of recognizing it as the weapon. The original game probably checked weapon keywords first or required "MAGNIF" to trigger the glass logic. I need to reorder the matching so weapon objects are tested before the generic glass check, or tighten the glass pattern to avoid false matches with room objects. I'll prioritize checking for "MAGNIF" first in both functions to catch the magnifying glass unambiguously, then fall back to the broader "GLASS" keyword only if that doesn't match — this way "EXAMINE MAGNIFYING GLASS" gets the right object, while "EXAMINE GLASS" alone can still refer to room-specific glasses if they exist. I need to update the order in doExamine and doTake to implement this: FLOOR check, then MAGNIF-specific branch, then examinables and weapons, then the bare GLASS fallback.
Thinking · Identified and rectified parser keyword ordering conflict.
Found a parser bug where "GET HOURGLASS" was matching the magnifying glass check for "GLASS" — I'm reordering the keyword checks so object names are matched before the magnifier's substring.
bash_toolFix GLASS/HOURGLASS collision in examine, take, and drop; verify
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - > /tmp/p.log 2>&1 << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:70]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

# ---- doExamine: MAGNIF early; bare GLASS only after objects ----
rep('''  if (inputHas(input, "GLASS") || inputHas(input, "MAGNIF")) {
    if (world.hasGlass || here === world.glassRoom)
      say("It looks as though it might prove useful for your investigation.");
    else say("Perhaps I just don't understand what you're looking for, but " +
             "I don't see anything like that around here.");
    return;
  }

  // the room's scripted examinables (from DESCR) come before weapons \u2014
  // "EXAMINE SILVER" in the dining room is scenery, not evidence''',
'''  const glassReply = () => {
    if (world.hasGlass || here === world.glassRoom)
      say("It looks as though it might prove useful for your investigation.");
    else say("Perhaps I just don't understand what you're looking for, but " +
             "I don't see anything like that around here.");
  };
  if (inputHas(input, "MAGNIF")) { glassReply(); return; }

  // the room's scripted examinables (from DESCR) come before weapons \u2014
  // "EXAMINE SILVER" in the dining room is scenery, not evidence.
  // Bare "GLASS" is checked only AFTER objects: the hourglass taught
  // us that substring parsers bite their makers.''')

rep('''  const noun = matchGenericNoun(input);
  if (noun) {
    const resp = VOCAB.noun_responses[noun.id - 1];
    const a = resp ? resp.examine_response : noun.word.toLowerCase() + ".";
    // SUNDRIES stores fragments ("floor.") for composition and full
    // sentences (chairs/room/house) to speak verbatim
    say(/^[a-z]/.test(a)
        ? "I don't see much of anything unusual about the " + a
        : a);
    return;
  }''',
'''  if (inputHas(input, "GLASS")) { glassReply(); return; }

  const noun = matchGenericNoun(input);
  if (noun) {
    const resp = VOCAB.noun_responses[noun.id - 1];
    const a = resp ? resp.examine_response : noun.word.toLowerCase() + ".";
    // SUNDRIES stores fragments ("floor.") for composition and full
    // sentences (chairs/room/house) to speak verbatim
    say(/^[a-z]/.test(a)
        ? "I don't see much of anything unusual about the " + a
        : a);
    return;
  }''')

# ---- doTake: same reordering ----
rep('''  if (inputHas(input, "GLASS") || inputHas(input, "MAGNIF")) {
    if (world.hasGlass)
      say("You are already carrying the magnifying glass.");
    else if (here === world.glassRoom) {
      world.hasGlass = true; world.glassRoom = null;
      say("You are now carrying the magnifying glass.");
    } else
      say("Perhaps I just don't understand what you're trying to get, but " +
          "I don't see anything like that around here.");
    return;
  }''',
'''  const glassTake = () => {
    if (world.hasGlass)
      say("You are already carrying the magnifying glass.");
    else if (here === world.glassRoom) {
      world.hasGlass = true; world.glassRoom = null;
      say("You are now carrying the magnifying glass.");
    } else
      say("Perhaps I just don't understand what you're trying to get, but " +
          "I don't see anything like that around here.");
  };
  if (inputHas(input, "MAGNIF")) { glassTake(); return; }''')

rep('''  if (!obj) say("But there's nothing around here to take!");
  else say("Perhaps I just don't understand what you're trying to get, " +
           "but I don't see anything like that around here.");''',
'''  if (inputHas(input, "GLASS")) { glassTake(); return; }
  if (!obj) say("But there's nothing around here to take!");
  else say("Perhaps I just don't understand what you're trying to get, " +
           "but I don't see anything like that around here.");''')

# ---- doDrop glass branch: same guard ----
rep('''function doDrop(input) {
  if (inputHas(input, "GLASS") || inputHas(input, "MAGNIF")) {''',
'''function doDrop(input) {
  const carriedObj = world.carrying && world.objects[world.weaponRoom];
  if ((inputHas(input, "MAGNIF")) ||
      (inputHas(input, "GLASS") && !(carriedObj &&
        matchObjectKeyword(input, carriedObj)))) {''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("PATCH_COMPLETE")
PATCH_EOF
cat /tmp/p.log; grep -q PATCH_COMPLETE /tmp/p.log && node --check web/engine.js && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && cd web && node << 'EOF' 2>&1 | tail -8
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 555, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const flat = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(flat().slice(-400)); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };
const g = D.house().grid;
const gotoRoom = (rid) => { outer: for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===rid){state.row=r;state.col=c;state.roomId=rid;break outer;} };

// the hourglass game, end to end
world.hasGlass = true;
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
const wk = world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0];
console.log("weapon this seed:", world.objects[world.weaponRoom].name);
type("examine " + wk);
assert(flat().includes("traces of blood"), "EXAMINE HOURGLASS reaches the weapon, not the magnifier");
type("get " + wk);
assert(world.carrying, "GET HOURGLASS takes the weapon");
// magnifier still reachable by both names
type("examine magnifying glass");
assert(flat().includes("prove useful for your investigation"), "EXAMINE MAGNIFYING GLASS still works");
type("drop glass");
assert(flat().includes("only useful tool in the house") && world.carrying, "DROP GLASS refuses; hourglass still carried");
type("drop " + wk);
assert(!world.carrying && flat().includes("rogue footman"), "DROP HOURGLASS summons the footman");
type("get " + wk);
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("gather"); type("accuse " + world.murderer.key);
assert(world.over === "won", "full win path with the hourglass");
console.log("ALL PARSER-COLLISION TESTS PASSED");
EOF
Output
PATCH_COMPLETE
weapon this seed: brass plaque
ok: EXAMINE HOURGLASS reaches the weapon, not the magnifier
ok: GET HOURGLASS takes the weapon
ok: EXAMINE MAGNIFYING GLASS still works
ok: DROP GLASS refuses; hourglass still carried
ok: DROP HOURGLASS summons the footman
ok: full win path with the hourglass
ALL PARSER-COLLISION TESTS PASSED
Thinking · Scrutinizing test naming conventions and anomalies.
The tests are passing across the board—the weapon type shifted to brass plaque due to the RNG draw order changing with the code fix, but since the tests don't care about the specific weapon, they all went green including the collision paths. I'm noticing something odd though: the test is named after the hourglass weapon but it's actually running with the plaque, and the collision assertion still passed, which makes me wonder if the keyword matching is catching "GLASS" somewhere in "PLAQUE" or if there's something else going on with the spec. I should force the hourglass weapon specifically to make sure that collision path is actually being exercised—I'll find which room has the hourglass, rig the world objects to include it, and run a quick targeted test with examine, get, and drop to verify the behavior is correct.
Thinking · Examined game mechanics discrepancies and assessed technical feasibility constraints.
The seed's weapon shifted to the brass plaque, so I'll force the hourglass to directly test the collision fix.
bash_toolProve the hourglass fix directly, update README, repackage
bash
cd /home/claude/web && node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: 555, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const flat = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(flat().slice(-300)); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };

// force the hourglass as the murder weapon
const hour = D.house().weapons.find(w => w.keys.some(k => k.includes("GLASS")));
console.log("forcing weapon:", hour.name, hour.keys, "room", hour.room);
world.weaponRoom = hour.room;
world.objects[hour.room] = { ...hour, examined: false };
world.hasGlass = true;
const g = D.house().grid;
outer: for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===hour.room){state.row=r;state.col=c;state.roomId=hour.room;break outer;}

type("examine hourglass");
assert(flat().includes("traces of blood"), "EXAMINE HOURGLASS -> the weapon (blood), not the magnifier");
type("take hourglass");
assert(world.carrying === hour.name, "TAKE HOURGLASS -> carrying the hourglass");
type("examine glass");
assert(flat().includes("prove useful for your investigation"), "bare EXAMINE GLASS -> still the magnifier");
type("drop hourglass");
assert(!world.carrying && flat().includes("footman"), "DROP HOURGLASS -> footman (not the glass refusal)");
type("drop glass");
assert(flat().includes("only useful tool in the house"), "DROP GLASS -> the authentic refusal");
console.log("\nHOURGLASS COLLISION DEFINITIVELY FIXED");
EOF
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
r = open('web/README_PHASE2.md').read()
r = r.replace('''## The endgame (M5 design notes)''',
'''## Room-entry kills and calm corridors (M5.5 field notes)

Two playtest corrections against the original, one of which the data
had been asserting all along: the murderer only ever strikes when the
detective ENTERS a room — the death scene literally reads "locks the
door behind you *as you enter the*…". So corridors, halls, and the
landing are safe ground at any threat level; the stalker positions
but never strikes mid-stride; a murderer who walks in on YOU merely
waits; and when the deadline expires the trap is armed rather than
sprung — it fires at your next room entry ("locks the door and turns
toward you"). Guest movement also calmed down: guests in your room
never move (all wandering happens off-screen, so the only visible
transition is an arrival), and the base rate dropped to 0.05/turn.

Bonus catch from the regression suite: the hourglass. A substring
parser that checks "GLASS" for the magnifier before checking weapon
keywords will cheerfully take your magnifying glass when you say GET
HOURGLASS. MAGNIF is now checked early, object keywords next, bare
GLASS last. Substring parsers bite their makers; they always have.

## The endgame (M5 design notes)''')
open('web/README_PHASE2.md','w').write(r)
print("README updated")
EOF
cd web && cp sleuth_playable.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/ && zip -qo /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m5.zip index.html engine.js data_shared.js data_v1.js data_v2.js gen_web_data.py README_PHASE2.md sleuth_playable.html && echo packaged

ok: EXAMINE HOURGLASS -> the weapon (blood), not the magnifier
ok: TAKE HOURGLASS -> carrying the hourglass
ok: bare EXAMINE GLASS -> still the magnifier
ok: DROP HOURGLASS -> footman (not the glass refusal)
ok: DROP GLASS -> the authentic refusal

HOURGLASS COLLISION DEFINITIVELY FIXED
README updated
packaged
present_filesPresenting file(s)...
json
{
  "filepaths": [
    "/mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_playable.html",

Output
[local_resource block]
[local_resource block]

Both behaviors corrected — and your first observation turned out to be documented in the primary source all along:

1. Kills happen only when the detective enters a room. The ambush string literally reads "quietly locks the door behind you as you enter the «room»" — the trigger condition was embedded in the sentence and I'd built a mid-corridor assassin over it anyway. Now: corridors, halls, and the landing are safe ground at any threat level; the stalking murderer positions via pursuit but never strikes mid-stride; a murderer who walks in on you just waits (leave and re-enter at your peril); and deadline expiry arms the trap rather than springing it — the "locks the door and turns toward you" scene fires at your next room entry. Verified: 300 turns pacing a hall at maximum threat, untouched; one step through the wrong doorway, dead.

2. Guest movement calmed. Guests in your room never move — all wandering happens off-screen, so the only visible transition is ever an arrival (announced with an idle action, as before). The base rate also dropped to 0.05/turn. Verified: 400 turns sharing a room with a guest, zero vanishings. This also quietly retires the wander-mid-interrogation quirk.

The regression suite then paid for itself: the seed's weapon was the hourglass, and my substring parser matched "GLASS" for the magnifier before checking weapon keywords — GET HOURGLASS was taking the magnifying glass. Now MAGNIF is checked early, object keywords next, bare GLASS last, proven with a forced-hourglass test across examine/take/drop. Substring parsers bite their makers; forty-three years hasn't changed that.

On DOSBox in my sandbox: not currently doable. I checked — no dosbox/dosbox-x/dosemu/qemu present, and this environment has no network egress, so I can't install anything. Even if you uploaded a DOSBox binary, it needs SDL and a display; headless automation of it here would be a fight not worth having. The practical division of labor is what we've already been doing, and honestly it's worked remarkably well — your observational reports have been the dynamic analysis, and every one has either corrected the engine or confirmed the static reading. If you ever want to go deeper on your side, DOSBox-X (a fork) has a built-in debugger that can watch memory — that's the tool that could pin down the last true unknowns: the exact NPC movement rate, the real deadline constant, and whether the panel spot is fixed or per-game. But none of those block anything; they're calibration, not architecture.

The remaster now matches every behavior you've reported from the original. M6 — the custom-content documentation and your own houses — remains whenever you want it.

2026-07-03 00:06
You

A couple of other things I'd like to tweak:

1) The X-axis of the player seems to be one unit off? See the first two screenshots. The dining room doorway message is triggered when the player icon is one unit to the right of the doorway. The kitchen room doorway message is triggered when the player icon is one unit past the doorway.

2) The secret passageway should be only accessed from the room on top of it. Presently, it's accessible from the room below it.

3) Let's add some shortcut abbreviations for the commonly used commands. 'q' for question, 'g' for gather, 'a' for accuse. Check the original Sleuth documentation/manual as well. I think 'ex' for examine is already working.

4) The suspicious and stalking behavior also seems to be happening a little faster than the original. I suppose I can eventually do some actual test counts/comparisons on the original in DOSbox, but for now, what are the stated turn requirements for ranks like Holmesian Sleuth, and so on? Can we approximate the total turns allowed to have the murder conceivably solvable at the most relaxed turn requirement ranking?

📎 1783045061993_image.png📎 1783045122872_image.png
2026-07-03 02:30
Claude
Thinking · Thinking about fixing game mechanics, command shortcuts, and balancing turn requirements.
Looking at the alignment issue between my sprite rendering and the grid system—the BSAVE format positions characters at specific screen columns, with walls drawn at the left character of each cell, but my sprites are rendering at the right half position, causing them to appear offset from where they should be visually. The fix is to shift sprite rendering one column left (grid_col*2 - 1 instead of *2), with a boundary check to clamp grid column 0 to screen column 0. For the guest anchors, I need to verify this doesn't break their positioning—the stored screen coordinates should map back consistently through the grid conversion, so if the original code used one mapping for both player and guests, shifting everything uniformly should maintain that alignment. The visual wall line actually sits at the left character of each cell (2c-1), so the doorway gap aligns there too—meaning sprites should draw at 2c-1 to appear "in" the doorway rather than right of it. But the original's stored coordinates for dining guests point to 2c, which works fine inside rooms where the offset is imperceptible; the user's screenshots reveal the problem only at doors and walls where the pixel-level positioning becomes visible. Shifting my sprite column back by 1 should fix the alignment. I should also update the anchor-verification test to match this new offset, and verify against the original DOSBox screenshot if possible—though the cyan guest's exact position there is hard to read precisely. The door-entry trigger fires when the player steps on a grid column, but the user perceives the trigger location by where the sprite lands, so shifting the sprite fixes that perception too. The kitchen door case (grid 3,12) is in a horizontal wall, which might need separate handling. For the secret passageway, I need to restrict panel access to only the top wall of the passage—specifically, cells where the room above is a regular room (1-20) and the cell below is the passage (2). Based on the user's original observation, entry comes from pressing Down in the dining room above, but the exit is via the blind corridor, suggesting the panel might be one-way downward only. I'll implement it as entry-only for now and can flip it if needed. I also need to check whether V2's passage has a room above it to apply the same logic. For the NPC graph, the panel edges currently connect through any adjacent wall, but I could restrict them to only the above-room connection for consistency—though since guests use the panel abstractly, I'll keep the current approach. For shortcuts, I see the original supports Q and EX prefixes with spaces. I need to check the manual for documented abbreviations, then add support for g (gather), a (accuse), and potentially bare single-letter forms. The tricky part is avoiding substring matches—I'll need exact-token matching for single letters before scanning the verb table. Now I'm looking at ranks and move thresholds in the documentation. I need to grep the manual and engine strings for the full rank list (there might be more than just Apprentice and Professional), find the move limits for each rank, and set the deadline so the most relaxed tier is actually achievable. Then I'll execute: restore the workspace, search the docs for abbreviations and rank info, implement the fixes, and calibrate the deadline. Then I need to test the sprite collision detection with the visual row check, verify the panel navigation rules (down from dining works, up from living is blocked), confirm V2 panel existence, validate the shortcuts, and check the threat pacing values before packaging.

Four solid items. Before touching code, let me consult the documentation we've barely used — the manual may answer both the abbreviations and the rank thresholds directly:

Thinking · Deliberating article usage for rank advancement messaging.
I'm looking at the rank progression system from the executable with four tiers: Novice, Apprentice, Professional Detective (abbreviated as "ProfessionalD"), and Holmesian. The rank display message follows the pattern "You now rank as a[n] «rank»", and I'm trying to figure out whether the third rank should be displayed as "Professional D" or the full "Professional Detective". I should also check the raw bytes around that section to see the complete strings, and there might be a digit table nearby along with compiled rank threshold constants. The MANUAL.DOC file is 14KB and should contain command abbreviations and rank documentation, but my earlier grep search came up empty — which is suspicious. It's likely the file uses WordStar encoding with high-bit characters set on word-final letters, which would cause grep to fail. I need to read the raw bytes to see what's actually in there.
Thinking · Investigated encoding issues and cross-referenced game mechanics documentation.
I found four ranks in the EXE and suspect the manual uses WordStar encoding, which is why grep failed. Let me check both possibilities.
bash_toolRead raw rank table region and diagnose manual encoding
bash
cd /home/claude/sleuth && python3 << 'EOF'
raw = open('SLEUTH.EXE','rb').read()
i = raw.find(b'Novice')
print("=== raw bytes around the rank table ===")
print(raw[i-80:i+120])
# manual encoding check
m = open('MANUAL.DOC','rb').read()
hi = sum(1 for b in m[:2000] if b >= 0x80)
print(f"\nMANUAL.DOC high-bit bytes in first 2000: {hi}")
print("first 200 raw:", m[:200])
EOF

b'\x00\x10\x85\x00\x00H\x86\x00\x00\x00\x86\x00\x00H\x85\x00\x00N\x88!\x00\xe6\x18 PRESS ANY KEY TO BEGIN THE GAME \x00\x00\x00`\x83\t\x00\x10\x19 Bumbling\x10\x07\x00\x1e\x19 Novice!\x0c\x00*\x19n Apprentice\r\x00:\x19 ProfessionalD\x07\x00L\x19 MasterY\n\x00X\x19 Holmesian\n\x00f\x191234567890\x06\x00t\x19alibi1\x00\x00@\x82\x00\x00@\x84\x01\x00\x86\x19\\\xc6\x06\x00\x8c\x19alibi2\x08\x00\x96\x19s'

MANUAL.DOC high-bit bytes in first 2000: 0
first 200 raw: b'\r\n\r\n                    SLEUTH - A MURDER MYSTERY\r\n\r\n\r\nSystem Requirements - Requires one disk drive, an 80-column\r\n     display, and 128K of memory.\r\n\r\n \r\nGetting Started - You should be sure to make'
Thinking · Calibrating rank thresholds and measuring turn requirements.
I'm examining the binary structure of a rank descriptor table, parsing through the length and offset values embedded in the string data to understand how each rank title is encoded and positioned. Now I'm thinking through how to calibrate the rank thresholds by measuring realistic turn counts — questioning all six guests across the twelve locations would take roughly 120–180 steps just for traversal, plus additional turns for examining rooms and gathering clues before the final accusation. Rather than building a full pathfinding bot, I can estimate this analytically and then tune the thresholds empirically based on what makes the puzzle solvable at each difficulty level. I'll build a bot that actually walks the grid using pathfinding to visit all rooms, question guests, and solve the case — that gives me an empirical turn count and serves as the ultimate integration test. I should also check both MANUAL.DOC and SLEUTH.DOC for command documentation since the earlier grep didn't find the rank/moves keywords. Then I'll implement the remaining features: sprite positioning at the correct column, the panel entrance as a one-way passage from above, and shorthand commands like Q/G/A/EX.

Thinking · Thinking about interpreting an unclear or incomplete request.
The SLEUTH.DOC file is showing up as binary due to a stray byte, so I need to use a different approach like strings or Python to read it properly. I should also check line 73 of MANUAL.DOC which mentions that searching or accusing only requires typing someone's first name. Then I can move forward with implementing the four fixes. Looking at the specifics now: the sprite needs to be drawn at a particular x-offset, the panel should only allow entry from above as a one-way passage, the shortcuts need to be single-token commands, and the ranking system uses a 6-tier ladder with a deadline calibrated through a walking bot measurement. I should also verify the workspace state first since the 5-hour limit suggests the container may have reset and started a new session.

Picking up where we left off. Workspace check first (session boundary), then finish reading the docs — SLEUTH.DOC needs a binary-safe read:

bash_toolRestore workspace and read SLEUTH.DOC binary-safe for command docs
bash
cd /home/claude 2>/dev/null && ls web/engine.js >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo INTACT || (mkdir -p /home/claude/sleuth /home/claude/web && cd /home/claude && unzip -qo /mnt/user-data/uploads/sleuth.zip -d sleuth && cp /mnt/user-data/outputs/extract_sleuth.py . && cd web && unzip -qo /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m5.zip && cp gen_web_data.py .. && cd .. && python3 extract_sleuth.py sleuth extracted > /dev/null && echo RESTORED); cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
doc = open('sleuth/SLEUTH.DOC','rb').read().decode('cp437', errors='replace')
doc = doc.split('\x1a')[0]
import re
# find the command/instruction section
lines = [l.rstrip() for l in doc.replace('\r\n','\n').split('\n')]
for i, l in enumerate(lines):
    if re.search(r'(?i)command|question|examine|accuse|gather|assemble|abbrev|move|arrow|type', l):
        print(f"{i:3d}| {l}")
EOF

Thinking · Scrutinizing documentation gaps in game mechanics implementation.
I'm noticing the documentation mentions abbreviations like Q for "QUESTION" and EX for "EXAMINE" around line 75, and I'm also seeing that SEARCH can be used as a verb to search people—which means we haven't yet implemented the ability to examine or search people as objects in the game.

Line 75 is the payoff — the original documents its abbreviations. Full context:

house  with  people  that  you  already know.  Just  select the
"Personalized"  option at  the beginning  of the  game and  the
computer  will prompt you  for the names of  seven people  (six
suspects and one victim!).

SLEUTH  uses a fairly limited  vocabulary, so only  one or  two
words should be  necessary to communicate  your intentions.  If
you like, you can abbreviate "QUESTION" as "Q" and "EXAMINE" as
"EX".  If you are having difficulties, you can get a summary of
these instructions by typing HELP. You may also QUIT or RESTART
at any  time during  the game.  To  get  a hard  copy of  these
instructions, just hit the `PrtSc' key as each page is shown to
you.  When you exit from  these instructions you  should be all
set to enter the world of SLEUTH.  Good Luck!

P.S.  You cannot use the four cursor keys (udrl)  while you are
Thinking · Proceeding with task execution.
So the original documents only have Q and EX, and the user wants to add G and A as convenient remasters—that makes sense given they were already hacking around the system to avoid retyping. The "Personalized Sleuth" feature letting users enter their own seven names solves exactly what they were trying to work around. Now I'm implementing all four fixes, starting with the sprite positioning. For the panel mechanics, I need to ensure it only enters from above: candidates are wall cells with a room (1-20, not 2) directly above and a passage (2) below. Entry happens by moving down from the room onto the panel, and from there down into the passage. Moving up from the passage onto the panel should be blocked—I'll restrict the trigger to downward movement only when coming from the room above. Once standing on the panel, normal movement resumes, so you can move down into the passage or back up to the room. For V2, I'll check if the passage has a room directly above; if not, I'll relax the constraint and log a warning. For shortcuts, I'm adding token-based pre-checks in matchVerb: Q maps to 2, EX to 1, G to 4, and A to 7. I'll check the first token before scanning for partial matches. Now I'm designing a test bot that uses BFS pathfinding to play through the game honestly: it tours rooms in nearest-neighbor order, examines the floor in each, questions guests, collects the glass when found, examines objects once it has the glass, then navigates to the weapon room, murder room, gathers evidence, and accuses the murderer. I'm tracking discovered state like the murder room and weapon location, and counting turns to calibrate the rank thresholds and deadline. I'll run this bot across several seeds with the threat disabled to get a baseline "relaxed but purposeful" solve time, then set the deadline to about 1.4× that baseline with suspicious behavior kicking in 120 turns before deadline and stalking 50 turns before. The rank boundaries will be calibrated from those measurements—Holmesian at roughly 35% of the baseline, then Master, Professional, Apprentice, and Novice tiers above that, with anything slower falling into Bumbling. The implementation should be around 100 lines in the test harness, so I'll patch the engine first, write the bot, set the constants from measurements, retest, and package it up.
Thinking · Orchestrated document verification and engineered performance measurement systems.
I've confirmed the original documents Q and EX, with G and A as new remasters labeled accordingly. Next I'll fix the engine issues and build a walking bot to measure solve times and calibrate the deadline and ranking system.
bash_toolApply sprite offset, top-only panel, shortcuts, rank ladder; check V2 panel
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - > /tmp/p.log 2>&1 << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:70]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

# ---- 1. sprite x: the visual wall-line sits at col*2-1, so do we ----
rep('''    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;                // assembled crowd
    if (ar <= 15) put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, ac * 2, "\\u263b", bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }''',
'''    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;                // assembled crowd
    if (ar <= 15)
      put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, Math.max(0, ac * 2 - 1), "\\u263b",
          bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }''')
rep('''  if (!world.blind && state.row <= 15)
    put(state.row + SPRITE_TOP, state.col * 2, "\\u263b",
        bgAt(state.row, state.col) | 0x0e);''',
'''  // the BSAVE draws each cell's visuals at columns 2c-1..2c with the
  // wall line on the LEFT char, so sprites sit at 2c-1 to read as
  // standing in doorways rather than one unit right of them
  if (!world.blind && state.row <= 15)
    put(state.row + SPRITE_TOP, Math.max(0, state.col * 2 - 1), "\\u263b",
        bgAt(state.row, state.col) | 0x0e);''')

# ---- 2. the panel: entered from the room ABOVE, downward only ----
rep('''  const g = house().grid, cand = [];
  let west = null;
  for (let r = 0; r < g.length; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < g[0].length; c++) {
      if (g[r][c] === 2 && (!west || c < west[1] ||
          (c === west[1] && r < west[0]))) west = [r, c];
      if (g[r][c] !== 0) continue;
      const n = (rr, cc) => (g[rr] && g[rr][cc]) || 0;
      if ((n(r-1,c) === 2) !== (n(r+1,c) === 2) &&
          (n(r-1,c) === 2 ? n(r+1,c) : n(r-1,c)) > 0 &&
          (n(r-1,c) === 2 ? n(r+1,c) : n(r-1,c)) <= 20) cand.push([r,c]);
      if ((n(r,c-1) === 2) !== (n(r,c+1) === 2) &&
          (n(r,c-1) === 2 ? n(r,c+1) : n(r,c-1)) > 0 &&
          (n(r,c-1) === 2 ? n(r,c+1) : n(r,c-1)) <= 20) cand.push([r,c]);
    }
  }
  world.panelCell = cand.length ? pick(cand) : null;''',
'''  // playtest-corrected: the panel is only entered from the room ABOVE
  // the passageway, pressing Down (the original never admits you from
  // below). Candidates: wall cells with a room overhead and the
  // passage underneath.
  const g = house().grid, cand = [];
  let west = null;
  for (let r = 0; r < g.length; r++) {
    for (let c = 0; c < g[0].length; c++) {
      if (g[r][c] === 2 && (!west || c < west[1] ||
          (c === west[1] && r < west[0]))) west = [r, c];
      if (g[r][c] !== 0) continue;
      const n = (rr, cc) => (g[rr] && g[rr][cc]) || 0;
      if (n(r + 1, c) === 2 && n(r - 1, c) > 0 && n(r - 1, c) <= 20 &&
          n(r - 1, c) !== 2) cand.push([r, c]);
    }
  }
  world.panelCell = cand.length ? pick(cand) : null;''')

rep('''    // THE SECRET PANEL \u2014 two-step, as observed in the original:
    // bump the (randomised, per game) panel cell and you step INTO
    // the wall itself, message fires; your next move carries you on
    // through. Works from either side.
    if (world.panelCell && r === world.panelCell[0] &&
        c === world.panelCell[1]) {''',
'''    // THE SECRET PANEL \u2014 two-step, as observed in the original:
    // bump the (randomised, per game) panel cell from the room above,
    // moving DOWN, and you step INTO the wall itself, message fires;
    // your next move carries you on through into the passageway.
    if (world.panelCell && r === world.panelCell[0] &&
        c === world.panelCell[1] && dr === 1 &&
        cellAt(state.row, state.col) !== 2) {''')

# ---- 3. shortcuts: Q/EX documented in SLEUTH.DOC; G/A remaster adds ----
rep('''function matchVerb(input) {
  // the original scanned the verb table in file order with INSTR \u2014
  // a substring hit anywhere in the input wins. "GUESTS" matches
  // GUEST; so, faithfully, would "EXAMINE THE TARGET" match GET if
  // GET came first in the table. It's 1983; embrace it.
  for (const v of VOCAB.verbs)
    if (input.includes(v.word)) return v.id;
  return null;
}''',
'''const SHORTCUTS = {
  // Q and EX are documented in SLEUTH.DOC ("you can abbreviate
  // QUESTION as Q and EXAMINE as EX"); G and A are remaster
  // conveniences in the same spirit
  Q: 2, EX: 1, G: 4, A: 7,
};

function matchVerb(input) {
  const first = input.split(/\\s+/)[0];
  if (first in SHORTCUTS) return SHORTCUTS[first];
  // the original scanned the verb table in file order with INSTR \u2014
  // a substring hit anywhere in the input wins. "GUESTS" matches
  // GUEST; so, faithfully, would "EXAMINE THE TARGET" match GET if
  // GET came first in the table. It's 1983; embrace it.
  for (const v of VOCAB.verbs)
    if (input.includes(v.word)) return v.id;
  return null;
}''')

# ---- 4a. the six-rank ladder (thresholds calibrated below) ----
rep('''function rankFor(turns) {
  return turns <= 100 ? "Holmesian"
       : turns <= 180 ? "Professional" : "Apprentice";
}''',
'''// the full ladder recovered from the EXE's string table:
// Bumbling / Novice! / Apprentice / Professional / Master / Holmesian.
// Thresholds are compiled-in originals we cannot read; ours are
// calibrated against measured bot playthroughs (see README).
const RANKS = [
  [150, "Holmesian"], [220, "Master"], [300, "Professional"],
  [400, "an Apprentice"], [520, "Novice"],
];
function rankFor(turns) {
  for (const [max, name] of RANKS) if (turns <= max) return name;
  return "Bumbling";
}''')
rep('''  say(`The case is closed after ${world.turn} moves.  You now rank ` +
      `as a ${rankFor(world.turn)}!`);''',
'''  const rank = rankFor(world.turn);
  say(`The case is closed after ${world.turn} moves.  You now rank ` +
      `as ${rank.startsWith("an ") ? rank : "a " + rank}!`);''')

# ---- 4b. relax the clock (final values set after measurement) ----
rep('''  world.deadline = 220;        // tunable; the original's is compiled-in''',
'''  world.deadline = 600;        // tunable; the original's is compiled-in''')
rep('''  if (world.threat < 1 && remaining <= 90) {''',
'''  if (world.threat < 1 && remaining <= 150) {''')
rep('''  if (world.threat < 2 && remaining <= 40) {''',
'''  if (world.threat < 2 && remaining <= 60) {''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("PATCH_COMPLETE")
PATCH_EOF
cat /tmp/p.log; grep -q PATCH_COMPLETE /tmp/p.log && node --check web/engine.js && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && echo "BUILD OK" && python3 -c "
# does V2 have a from-above panel candidate?
import json
for v in ('v1','v2'):
    g = json.load(open('extracted/content_%s.json' % v))['map']['cells']
    cand = [(r,c) for r in range(1,16) for c in range(40)
            if g[r][c]==0 and g[r+1][c]==2 and 0<g[r-1][c]<=20 and g[r-1][c]!=2]
    print(v, 'from-above panel candidates:', len(cand), cand[:5])"
Output
PATCH_COMPLETE
BUILD OK
v1 from-above panel candidates: 7 [(6, 1), (6, 2), (6, 3), (6, 4), (6, 5)]
v2 from-above panel candidates: 7 [(6, 1), (6, 2), (6, 3), (6, 4), (6, 5)]
Thinking · Architecting pathfinding algorithm for walking bot navigation.
Both houses have 7 candidates visible from above, with V2's passage positioned below a room. I'm building a walking bot to measure solve time using BFS pathfinding across grid cells in the node harness—it'll navigate 4-directionally through walkable cells, and I need to account for the upstairs teleport mechanic at the stairs landing (32→33) since rooms 1-11 don't require special panel or stair handling. The bot must handle disconnected components by checking reachability: if the target is unreachable in the current area, it paths to landing 32 first (which triggers the teleport), then continues from position 33. For the secret passage in room 2, the bot approaches from above, positions at the panel column, and presses Down twice to enter—I'll use the known panel cell location and add a small constant for the hunt allowance since that's a minor underestimate. The blind corridor at the passage's west end is the only exit (the panel is one-way down), so after exploring the passage, the bot walks there, presses Down, then any key to randomly teleport out. The tour itself starts from spawn and greedily visits unvisited rooms 1-11 using component-aware pathfinding, examining each room's object with the glass once I've collected it, and questioning anyone present with up to 3 tries if they refuse (each attempt costs a turn). After touring all rooms, I'll revisit any rooms whose objects weren't examined with glass if the weapon wasn't identified yet, then hunt down remaining suspects by checking their known rooms, grab the weapon, head to the murder room, gather evidence, and accuse—all while tracking turn count. I'll disable the deadline by setting it to a high value, handle movement by converting path cells to arrow keys with re-sync after each press in case teleports occur, and implement the passage entry as standing one row above the panel and pressing Down twice. This should be around 120 lines; I'll test it on 4 seeds to collect turn counts, then adjust constants and run the behavior regression.
Thinking · Orchestrated measurement calibration across validated properties.
I've confirmed both houses have suitable panel candidates. Now I'll run the measurement bot through its pathfinding routine—touring rooms, examining floors, gathering intel, and locating key items—to generate calibration data from the turn count.
bash_toolRun the calibration bot across six seeds to measure relaxed solve length
bash
cd /home/claude/web && cat > /tmp/playerbot.js << 'BOT_EOF'
// A relaxed-but-purposeful player: measures how many turns a full
// honest solve takes, for deadline/rank calibration.
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_SEED: Number(process.argv[2] || 42), SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
world.deadline = 99999;                       // measuring, not dying

const g = D.house().grid;
const H = g.length, W = g[0].length;
const walkable = (r,c) => r>=0 && r<H && c>=0 && c<W && g[r][c] > 0;

function bfs(from, goalFn) {
  const prev = {}, seen = new Set([from.join()]), q = [from];
  while (q.length) {
    const [r,c] = q.shift();
    if (goalFn(r,c)) {                        // rebuild path
      const path = []; let k = [r,c].join();
      while (k !== from.join()) { path.unshift(k.split(',').map(Number)); k = prev[k]; }
      return path;
    }
    for (const [dr,dc] of [[1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[0,-1]]) {
      const nr=r+dr, nc=c+dc, kk=[nr,nc].join();
      if (walkable(nr,nc) && !seen.has(kk)) { seen.add(kk); prev[kk]=[r,c].join(); q.push([nr,nc]); }
    }
  }
  return null;
}

function walkTo(goalFn) {                      // returns true if arrived
  for (let guard = 0; guard < 40; guard++) {
    if (goalFn(state.row, state.col)) return true;
    let path = bfs([state.row, state.col], goalFn);
    if (!path) {                               // other floor: via landing
      path = bfs([state.row, state.col], (r,c) => g[r][c] === 32 || g[r][c] === 33);
      if (!path) return false;
    }
    for (const [r,c] of path) {
      const dr = r - state.row, dc = c - state.col;
      key(dr === 1 ? "ArrowDown" : dr === -1 ? "ArrowUp" : dc === 1 ? "ArrowRight" : "ArrowLeft");
      if (state.row !== r || state.col !== c) break;   // teleported: replan
      if (world.over) return false;
    }
  }
  return goalFn(state.row, state.col);
}

const questioned = new Set();
function interview() {
  const here = world.suspects.filter(s => s.room === (state.roomId === 13 ? 12 : state.roomId));
  for (const s of here) {
    if (questioned.has(s.key)) continue;
    for (let i = 0; i < 4 && !world.alibis[s.key].told; i++) type("q");
    if (world.alibis[s.key].told) questioned.add(s.key);
  }
}

function visitRoom(rid) {
  if (rid === 2) {                             // via the panel, from above
    const [pr, pc] = world.panelCell;
    if (!walkTo((r,c) => r === pr - 1 && c === pc)) return;
    key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowDown");        // into the wall, then through
  } else if (!walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === rid)) return;
  type("ex floor");
  interview();
  if (world.glassRoom === state.roomId) type("get glass");
  const obj = world.objects[state.roomId];
  if (world.hasGlass && obj && !obj.examined && !world.weaponKnown)
    type("ex " + obj.keys[0]);
  if (rid === 2) {                             // leave via blind corridor
    const [wr, wc] = world.westEnd;
    walkTo((r,c) => r === wr && c === wc);
    key("ArrowDown"); key("x");                // vanish; any key drops us
  }
}

// --- the tour: nearest-first over rooms 1-11 (2 last: it needs the panel)
const todo = [1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9,10,11,2];
for (const rid of todo) visitRoom(rid);
// revisit for weapon confirmation if the glass came late
if (!world.weaponKnown) for (const rid of todo) {
  if (world.weaponKnown) break;
  const obj = world.objects[rid];
  if (obj && !obj.examined) visitRoom(rid);
}
// chase uninterviewed guests
for (let hunt = 0; hunt < 8 && questioned.size < 6; hunt++) {
  const missing = world.suspects.find(s => !questioned.has(s.key));
  if (!missing) break;
  visitRoom(missing.room <= 11 ? missing.room : 1);
}
// endgame
type("get " + world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0]);
if (state.roomId !== world.weaponRoom) { walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === world.weaponRoom); type("get " + world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0]); }
walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === world.murderRoom);
type("g"); type("a " + world.murderer.key);
console.log(JSON.stringify({ seed: process.argv[2], turns: world.turn, result: world.over,
  questioned: questioned.size, sceneFound: world.sceneFound }));
BOT_EOF
for seed in 11 42 137 2026 555 8080; do node /tmp/playerbot.js $seed; done

Thinking · Diagnosing test failures and tracing failure modes.
Looking at the test data, full solves are taking 260–431 turns, with the most thorough run hitting 431 turns. Seed 2026 failed despite having a high deadline of 99999, and the world state shows 'lost' — so I'm tracing back through the possible failure modes: either the bot accused the wrong person, walked to the wrong room before accusing, or failed to acquire a weapon. The bot did question 4 people and found the scene, so the issue likely stems from incomplete information gathering. I'm realizing the core problem: the bot might be trying to pathfind to the murder room (room 2, the passage) but failing because it's disconnected from the main graph, forcing it to accuse from wherever it ended up instead. The fix is to route through the panel entry when the target is room 2, similar to how a player would navigate there. The same issue applies to retrieving the weapon if it's also in room 2. For the interview loop, only the thorough run achieved 6 questioned guests; others got 2–4 because the hunt loop was using stale room data as guests moved around. The 431-turn ceiling represents the informative "relaxed thorough" baseline. Now I'm recalibrating the deadline and tension mechanics: my current engine has deadline 600 with suspicious at turn 450 and stalking at turn 540. Against the measured 260–431 range, a thorough player at 431 would just barely avoid triggering suspicion. I could tighten this to deadline 650 with suspicious at 480 and stalking at 580 to give more headroom, but the original game clearly pressured players, so I need to balance solvability against that tension. The user wants the most relaxed completion tier (Novice/Bumbling) to be conceivable. My measured relaxed-thorough is around 430 turns; accounting for human inefficiency (the bot's pathing is optimal), I'd estimate a leisurely human needs roughly 650 turns. So I'm setting deadline 700, suspicious at turn 520, and stalking at turn 630. Then I'll rank the difficulty tiers: Holmesian ≤ 200, Master ≤ 280, Professional ≤ 380, Apprentice ≤ 500, Novice ≤ 650, and Bumbling beyond 650 (still winnable before the deadline). I need to update the rank constants, adjust the deadline, fix the room-2 routing in the bot, and test with a seed where the murder happens in the passage.
Thinking · Diagnosed performance gaps and recalibrated optimization parameters.
I found that optimal solves run 260–431 turns and identified a specific failure in seed 2026 where the bot missed a secret passageway route to the murder room. Now I'll calibrate the bot's constants to handle these cases better.
bash_toolCalibrate rank/deadline constants; fix bot passage routing; re-measure
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
src = src.replace('''const RANKS = [
  [150, "Holmesian"], [220, "Master"], [300, "Professional"],
  [400, "an Apprentice"], [520, "Novice"],
];''',
'''// Calibrated against measured bot playthroughs (optimal pathing,
// full interviews: 260-431 turns; humans path ~1.5x worse):
const RANKS = [
  [200, "Holmesian"], [280, "Master"], [380, "Professional"],
  [500, "an Apprentice"], [650, "Novice"],
];''')
src = src.replace('''  world.deadline = 600;        // tunable; the original's is compiled-in''',
'''  world.deadline = 700;        // tunable; the original's is compiled-in.
                               // Calibrated so a leisurely full solve
                               // (~650 turns) stays conceivable.''')
src = src.replace('''  if (world.threat < 1 && remaining <= 150) {''',
'''  if (world.threat < 1 && remaining <= 180) {''')
src = src.replace('''  if (world.threat < 2 && remaining <= 60) {''',
'''  if (world.threat < 2 && remaining <= 70) {''')
open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("constants calibrated")
EOF
python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && cd web && python3 - << 'EOF'
# bot fix: route to room 2 (or a weapon there) via the panel
src = open('/tmp/playerbot.js').read()
src = src.replace('''// endgame
type("get " + world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0]);
if (state.roomId !== world.weaponRoom) { walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === world.weaponRoom); type("get " + world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0]); }
walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === world.murderRoom);
type("g"); type("a " + world.murderer.key);''',
'''// endgame (rooms behind the panel included)
function goRoom(rid) {
  if (rid === 2) {
    const [pr, pc] = world.panelCell;
    walkTo((r,c) => r === pr - 1 && c === pc);
    key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowDown");
  } else walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === rid);
}
goRoom(world.weaponRoom);
type("get " + world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0]);
goRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("g"); type("a " + world.murderer.key);''')
open('/tmp/playerbot.js','w').write(src)
print("bot fixed")
EOF
for seed in 2026 11 42 99 777; do node /tmp/playerbot.js $seed; done

bash_toolDiagnose seed 2026 mystery configuration

cd /home/claude/web && node << 'EOF'
process.argv[2] = "2026";

Thinking · Navigating weapon acquisition constraints strategically.
The bot needs to navigate to the weapon room by going to the panel at (6,2), moving down twice to reach it, then continuing to the passage. But there's a critical constraint: the weapon must be examined with the glass first before it can be picked up, so I need to work through the tour's examination sequence in room 2 before attempting to get the item. Once the weapon is confirmed and the bot tries to return to the murder room, it hits a pathfinding problem—the passage is disconnected from the rest of the grid, so the BFS fails and the bot gets stuck gathering in the wrong location, leading to an accusation failure. The fix is to make the bot exit the passage via the blind corridor before attempting to path anywhere else. I'll refactor goRoom to detect when the bot is currently in the passage and needs to leave: it'll walk to the corridor exit, trigger the blind drop, then proceed with the normal pathfinding to the target room. I can reuse the same exit pattern that visitRoom(2) already uses to keep the logic consistent.
Thinking · Diagnosed navigation flaw and devised corrective training method.
The bot successfully retrieves the weapon but can't navigate out because it avoids the blind corridor, causing it to get stuck and trigger the "You fool!!" taunt—I'll need to teach the bot to use that corridor as an exit route.
bash_toolTeach the bot the blind-corridor exit; full re-measurement
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
src = open('/tmp/playerbot.js').read()
src = src.replace('''function goRoom(rid) {
  if (rid === 2) {
    const [pr, pc] = world.panelCell;
    walkTo((r,c) => r === pr - 1 && c === pc);
    key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowDown");
  } else walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === rid);
}''',
'''function exitPassage() {
  const [wr, wc] = world.westEnd;
  walkTo((r,c) => r === wr && c === wc);
  key("ArrowDown"); key("x");                  // the blind corridor drop
}
function goRoom(rid) {
  if (g[state.row] && g[state.row][state.col] === 2 && rid !== 2)
    exitPassage();                              // only way out
  if (rid === 2) {
    const [pr, pc] = world.panelCell;
    walkTo((r,c) => r === pr - 1 && c === pc);
    key("ArrowDown"); key("ArrowDown");
  } else walkTo((r,c) => g[r][c] === rid);
}''')
open('/tmp/playerbot.js','w').write(src)
print("bot exits the passage properly")
EOF
cd web && for seed in 2026 11 42 99 777 31337; do node /tmp/playerbot.js $seed; done
