Thinking · Diagnosing regex pattern mismatches and spacing issues.
The actual output is rendering correctly — "2. Iguana" shows up fine in the dump. The test regex is the problem: it's looking for `/\d\. Iguana │/` but there's actually a lot of padding space between "Iguana" and the box border character, and the second pattern fails because the line doesn't end after "Iguana". I need to update the regex to `/\d\. Iguana\s+│/` to account for those spaces — this is just a test fix, not a screen rendering issue. I'll rerun the whole test with the corrected regex.
Thinking · Identified rendering issue and prepared corrected test rerun.
The rendering is correct — the issue was my regex not accounting for box padding. I'll rerun the full test with the fix.
bash_toolComplete verification with corrected regex
bash
cd /home/claude/web && timeout 150 node << 'EOF'
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_FAST: 1, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined,
                  addEventListener: (ev, fn) => { handlers[ev] = fn; } };
global.location = { search: "?seed=4242" };
let lastHTML = "";
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML = v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js','data_house3.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
const key = k => handlers.keydown({ key: k, preventDefault(){} });
const type = s => { for (const ch of s) key(ch); key("Enter"); };
const plain = () => lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').replace(/&amp;/g,'&');
const flat = () => plain().replace(/\s+/g,' ');
const D = global.window.SLEUTH_DEBUG;
const { state, world } = D;
const assert = (c,m) => { if(!c){ console.error("FAIL:",m); console.log(plain().slice(-700)); process.exit(1);} console.log("ok:",m); };

key(" "); key("h"); key("s");
type("Astral"); type("Winterstar"); type("Az");
type("Iguana"); key(" "); key("Enter"); key("Enter");
type("Vitril"); type("Chrome"); key("Enter");
for (let i = 4; i <= 7; i++) { type("Guest" + i); type("Tavern"); key("Enter"); }
assert(/\d\. Iguana\s+│/.test(plain()), "one-word name clean in the guest list");
key("a");
assert(D.ui.mode === "game", "game starts");
if (world.victim.nick !== "Az") {
  const s = world.suspects.find(x => x.nick === "Az");
  s.room = 1; state.roomId = 1;
  const a1 = D.house().anchors["1"]; state.row = a1[0]; state.col = a1[1];
  let told = false;
  for (let i = 0; i < 12 && !told; i++) { s.room = 1; type("q az"); told = world.alibis[s.key].told; }
  assert(told, "QUESTION by nickname");
  assert(/Az (replies|turns|whispers|sneers|says|leans|glances|draws|stares|looks|smiles|shrugs|pauses|hisses|ignores|faces|eyes)/.test(flat()) || flat().includes("Az "),
         "dialogue addresses by nickname");
}
let guard = 0;
while (world.victim.first !== "Iguana" && guard++ < 40) D.execute("restart");
if (world.victim.first === "Iguana") {
  assert(!flat().includes("the  estate") && flat().includes("Iguana estate"),
         "blank-last victim: estate falls back to nickname");
  assert(flat().includes("Iguana was brutally murdered"), "one-word intro clean");
} else console.log("(note: Iguana not drawn as victim in 40 tries — fallback untested this run)");
world.hasGlass = true;
const g = D.house().grid;
const gotoRoom = (rid) => { outer: for(let r=0;r<17;r++)for(let c=0;c<40;c++) if(g[r][c]===rid){state.row=r;state.col=c;state.roomId=rid;break outer;} };
gotoRoom(world.weaponRoom);
type("ex " + world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0]);
type("get " + world.objects[world.weaponRoom].keys[0]);
gotoRoom(world.murderRoom);
type("g"); type("a " + world.murderer.nickKey.toLowerCase());
assert(world.over === "won" && flat().includes("Sleuth!"), "ACCUSE by nickname wins");
console.log("\nALL SEVEN TWEAKS VERIFIED");
EOF

ok: QUESTION by nickname
ok: dialogue addresses by nickname
ok: blank-last victim: estate falls back to nickname
ok: one-word intro clean
ok: ACCUSE by nickname wins

ALL SEVEN TWEAKS VERIFIED
Thinking · Finalized testing, synthesized visual summary, prepared documentation.
Everything's passing — including the edge case with the Iguana victim scenario and its fallback logic. Now I'm putting together a quick visual summary for the final section, updating the README, and getting ready to package and present this.

All verified — including the estate-name fallback with Iguana as the one-word victim. Title visual check, then package:

bash_toolVisual dump of the reshaped title screen
bash
cd /home/claude/web && node -e "
const handlers = {};
global.window = { SLEUTH_FAST: 1, SLEUTH_HOUSES: undefined, addEventListener: (ev,fn)=>{handlers[ev]=fn} };
global.location = { search: '?seed=42' };
let lastHTML = '';
global.document = { getElementById: () => ({ set innerHTML(v){ lastHTML=v; } }) };
const fs = require('fs');
for (const f of ['data_shared.js','data_v1.js','data_v2.js','engine.js','data_house3.js'])
  eval(fs.readFileSync(f,'utf8').replace(/window\./g,'global.window.').replace(/location\.search/g,'global.location.search'));
console.log(lastHTML.replace(/<[^>]+>/g,'').replace(/&lt;/g,'<').split('\n').slice(0,25).join('\n'));"

Thinking · Refining card layout alignment and visual positioning.
The card layout is working well with the right half positioned correctly next to the left wing. There's a minor alignment issue where the T's stem is slightly left of center rather than perfectly centered, but it matches what was requested and is visually acceptable. bash_toolREADME field notes; final packaging
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - << 'EOF'
r = open('web/README_PHASE2.md').read()
r = r.replace('''## The 1983 presentation layer (M6.2 field notes)''',
'''## Nicknames, the guest list, and the hairline hunt (M6.3 field notes)

Personalized Sleuth now takes three fields per suspect: first name,
last name (a lone space means the character has none \u2014 one-word names
render cleanly everywhere, including "the Iguana estate"), and a
NICKNAME that defaults to the first name. The nickname is used
wherever a single name appears \u2014 dialogue framings, refusals,
arrivals, accusation theatre, confessions \u2014 and the parser accepts it
alongside the first name for QUESTION and ACCUSE. Names and nicknames
must be mutually distinct, because they are the parser's match keys.
The startup menu gained a Guest List panel showing whoever will
attend: the selected house's original cast, or the personalized seven
with nicknames in parentheses. The title card now covers the right
half of the house exactly, boxed, with a blank row before the block
letters (whose T went on a diet).

And the mystery of the two thin black lines across coloured houses:
fractional line-height. The screen font is `clamp()`-sized with
line-height 1.18, so at non-integer display scaling (Windows 125%)
some line boxes round a pixel short and the black `<pre>` background
peeks through as hairlines. Fix: 2px of vertical padding on the
inline spans \u2014 inline padding paints background without moving
layout, overpainting every seam. A rendering bug older than any of
our engine bugs: it lived in CSS the whole time.

## The 1983 presentation layer (M6.2 field notes)''')
open('web/README_PHASE2.md','w').write(r)
print("README updated")
EOF
cp gen_web_data.py web/ && cd web && cp sleuth_playable.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/ && zip -qo /mnt/user-data/outputs/sleuth_engine_m6.zip index.html engine.js data_shared.js data_v1.js data_v2.js data_house3.js gen_web_data.py README_PHASE2.md HOUSES.md sleuth_playable.html && echo packaged

Output
[local_resource block]
[local_resource block]

All seven landed:

1–3, the title screen: the credits card now covers the right half of the house exactly — a proper box, rows 1–17 × columns 40–79, with the left wing fully visible beside it. The block letters dropped a row (blank space between house and letters), the T's stem went on a diet to half width, and the calcaprt.com link moved to its own footer line so the plate breathes again.

4, the startup menu: options pushed to the left margin with all values column-aligned at 30, plus the Guest List panel on the right showing whoever will attend — the selected house's original cast by default, or your personalized seven with nicknames in parentheses, exactly per your mockup. (Your mockup's cast choices were noted with a smile.) One collision surfaced and was fixed: house labels now strip their "House N ·" prefix and clamp so they can't run into the panel border.

5–6, nicknames and one-word names: Personalized Sleuth now takes three fields. The nickname defaults to the first name and is used everywhere a single name appears — dialogue framings, refusals, arrivals, the can't-hear line, accusation theatre, confessions — and the parser accepts it alongside the first name for QUESTION and ACCUSE (verified: a full game won with a <nickname>). A lone space in the last-name field means the character has none, and everything downstream tolerates it — including the intro, where a one-word victim gets "the Iguana estate" via nickname fallback rather than the grammatically tragic "the estate." Names and nicknames must be mutually distinct since they're parser match keys; the entry screen explains this if you collide.

7, the hairlines — solved, and it's a fun one: not the engine at all. The screen font is clamp()-sized with line-height 1.18, so at non-integer display scaling (your Windows setup is likely at 125%) some line boxes round a pixel short, and the black <pre> background peeks through as hairlines at irregular rows — which is why they only showed against colored backgrounds. The fix exploits a CSS quirk: vertical padding on inline spans paints background without affecting layout, so 2px of padding overpaints every seam. A rendering bug older than any bug we wrote — it was in the CSS from milestone 1, invisible until you pressed C.

The remaster's front-of-house is now fully dressed: title, menu, guest list, personalization with nicknames, instructions from the 1985 doc, the stormy-night card, and a house with no mysterious lines through it — only the intended mystery inside.

Let's do a little more debugging:

I was trying the personalized version and I ran into several issues. None of the suspects stared at the floor very frequently, so I couldn't find the correct room and the turns overran into the 500s as I moved around. The murder suspicion level also didn't seem to fire off the "gotten suspicious" line, and just jumped from nothing to stalking.

Other fixes:
1) The gathered suspects are arranged in a U shape, as per the first screenshot, and a little | door is actually drawn to simulate the closed door. The detective isn't able to wander out of that room, but can move around the room. Also check if the end game messages for our remaster version match the original move summary and sleuth rank message?

2) On moving in a room, the room description should refresh. This makes it easier to identify items to examine, instead of trying to remember the initial description on entry. In fact, each message/description seems to refresh in the original game, which allows the messages to fit into that space without overrun.

3) On identifying murder weapon and room, the text is in red for blood, see the second and third screenshot.

4) I noticed that the guest/suspect is actually drawn on the right side of the same character space, as per the fourth screenshot, so that the player and suspect can occupy the same location without overlapping.

5) The staircase teleport should be one unit up, for both instances. Right now the detective is appearing in the wall. (5th and 6th screenshots)

6) I've managed to record the exact sounds that play when exiting the secret passage, when making an accusation and getting it wrong and failing, and when making an accusation and succeeding. See the attached three MP3s. Can you replicate those system sounds in our version somehow?

Claude
Thinking · Thinking about debugging a text-based detective game with multiple mechanical and audio issues.
I'm working through a batch of bugs, starting with personalized game issues where the staring mechanic is misfiring—suspicion levels are jumping straight from normal to stalking without the intermediate "grown suspicious" state, and turns are exceeding 500. I suspect there's an interaction between the random number generator and the RESEED logic when the game starts through the personalization menu. The staring initialization sets a value around 40-70 frames, and then each tick checks a probability threshold to trigger the staring state, but something in that flow is breaking down. Now I'm reconsidering whether the murder room occupancy is actually the culprit—with a custom cast, guests should still be distributed across locations at roughly the same rate as the original cast, so the staring probability shouldn't differ much. The real issue might be in the suspicion escalation logic itself. I'm noticing that the threat level checks happen sequentially each tick: threat 1 fires when remaining time hits 180, then threat 2 fires when remaining hits 70. Both conditions should never trigger simultaneously unless the deadline gets shortened dramatically in a single jump—like when a weapon is picked up, which sets the deadline to turn+70, potentially causing both threat checks to fire on the same tick. If that happens, both the "suspicious" and "stalking" messages would print, but they might be getting lost in the message queue or scrolling off-screen before the user notices them. The fix would be to soften the deadline reduction on weapon pickup—maybe setting it to turn+150 instead of turn+70—so the suspicion escalation happens in proper stages rather than all at once. But I'm also realizing the staring mechanic might genuinely be the bottleneck: for a guest to stare, they need to be in the murder room AND the stare timer needs to trigger, which happens at roughly 0.084 probability per tick. The player has to actually be in that same room to witness it, and if the guest wanders out before the player arrives, the stare never happens. The drift test passed when forced, so the stare-drift loop itself works. My real suspicion now is whether the murder room is even being set correctly with the custom cast, or if there's something about how guests move that's preventing them from reaching it. Let me run an empirical simulation over 600 turns to measure how often the murder-room occupant is actually staring and how frequently a touring player witnesses staring anywhere. If the numbers look reasonable, the issue is just feel—I'd bump the murder-room stare probability from 0.7 to 0.85, increase the re-roll rate from 0.12 to 0.2, lower red-herring staring to 0.05, and make staring sticky: once a guest starts staring in the murder room, they stay staring while they're there, but elsewhere stares are brief and fleeting. Implementing the stare logic... For the threat escalation, I'm using an else-if chain so only one message fires per tick—first checking if threat is below 1 and remaining drops to 180 or less, then checking if threat is below 2 and remaining hits 70 or less. This keeps the progression sequential and prevents duplicate messages on the same turn. Now for the endgame gathering: I need to arrange suspects in a U-shape around the player, with guests positioned at offsets from a center anchor point in the room—something like placing two at the top corners and four spread across the bottom to form that opening-upward U formation. The endgame message needs a period instead of an exclamation mark, and the confession should use the suspect's full name with the original framing "turns toward you, smiling triumphantly." rather than the current nickname + grinning template. I need to switch to using fullName(m) for the confession line and ensure the rank message ends with a period. Also noticing that room descriptions seem to refresh on each move within a room based on the screenshots. The original clears the message area on each action rather than scrolling, which prevents text overflow — so I should clear the log at the start of each player action (tryMove and execute), then always redescribe the current room after a successful move. This means calling describeCell with a force flag to reprint the full description including occupants and scene details, then the arrival announcements integrate naturally. For execute(), I'd clear the log first, echo the command, then show the response. I also need to handle blood text in red by adding an optional attribute parameter to the say() function, storing both text and color in log entries. Guest positioning needs fixing too — guests should draw at the right character of a cell (col*2) while the player occupies the left (col*2-1), so they don't overlap when sharing a space. And there's an issue with stair teleportation landing the detective in the wall instead of one unit up from the landing cell. For the three MP3 files, I'm thinking about analyzing them to extract the beep sequences — if ffmpeg is available in the sandbox, I can convert them to WAV and use zero-crossing or autocorrelation to estimate pitch per frame, then segment into individual notes with their frequencies and durations. That would let me recreate the original PC speaker sounds as WebAudio square wave sequences, embedding them as tuples of (frequency, milliseconds) and playing them through an extended beep function. The work order is clear: start with audio analysis to get the sound data, then tackle the engine mega-patch covering stare rework, threat staging, gathering mechanics, logging, red attribute lines, guest drawing, stair teleport fix, and sound playback. Tests need to verify stare reliability, threat sequencing, gather mechanics, description refresh, red text on discovery, guest positioning, stair landing placement, and that the sound data exists and plays (with guards for headless environments). Let me check if ffmpeg and numpy are available first.

A rich debugging haul — and you've handed me actual recordings of the 1983 PC speaker, which means I can reverse-engineer the beep sequences into data. Let me start there (checking what audio tooling the sandbox has), then diagnose the staring/suspicion bugs:

Thinking · Synthesizing audio from MP3 files using pitch detection.
I've got ffmpeg and numpy available, so I can convert each MP3 to mono WAV at 22050 Hz, then run pitch detection on 512-sample frames with autocorrelation to identify note segments with their frequencies and durations. Since PC speaker output is monophonic square waves, autocorrelation should work well for this. I'm setting up the analyzer to read the audio, apply RMS gating per frame, and extract pitch information. Finishing the pitch detection...
Thinking · Mobilized debugging toolkit to address gameplay mechanics comprehensively.
I have the full toolkit available and I'm now extracting the beep sequences using autocorrelation pitch-tracking.
bash_toolPitch-track the three PC-speaker recordings into note sequences
bash
cd /tmp && for f in Sleuth_Success_Accuse Sleuth_Failed_Accuse Sleuth_Passage_Sound; do ffmpeg -y -loglevel error -i /mnt/user-data/uploads/$f.mp3 -ac 1 -ar 22050 $f.wav; done && python3 << 'EOF'
import wave, numpy as np

def notes(path):
    w = wave.open(path)
    sr = w.getframerate()
    data = np.frombuffer(w.readframes(w.getnframes()), dtype=np.int16).astype(float)
    data /= (np.abs(data).max() or 1)
    N, hop = 1024, 256
    gate = 0.08
    frames = []
    for i in range(0, len(data) - N, hop):
        seg = data[i:i+N]
        rms = np.sqrt((seg**2).mean())
        if rms < gate:
            frames.append((i/sr, 0)); continue
        seg = seg - seg.mean()
        ac = np.correlate(seg, seg, 'full')[N-1:]
        ac /= (ac[0] or 1)
        lo, hi = int(sr/3000), int(sr/100)
        lag = lo + np.argmax(ac[lo:hi])
        # refine: reject subharmonic confusion by checking half-lag
        if lag//2 >= lo and ac[lag//2] > 0.9 * ac[lag]:
            lag = lag//2
        frames.append((i/sr, sr/lag))
    # merge into notes
    out, cur = [], None
    for t, f in frames:
        if f == 0:
            if cur: out.append(cur); cur = None
            continue
        if cur and abs(f - cur[1]) / cur[1] < 0.06:
            cur = (cur[0], (cur[1]+f)/2, t)
        else:
            if cur: out.append(cur)
            cur = (t, f, t)
    if cur: out.append(cur)
    hopdur = hop/sr
    return [(round(f), round((e - s + hopdur)*1000)) for s, f, e in out if (e-s) > 0.015]

for name in ("Sleuth_Passage_Sound", "Sleuth_Success_Accuse", "Sleuth_Failed_Accuse"):
    ns = notes(f"/tmp/{name}.wav")
    print(f"{name}: {len(ns)} notes")
    print("  ", ns[:24])
EOF
Output

Sleuth_Success_Accuse: 3 notes
   [(525, 244), (525, 430), (788, 592)]
Sleuth_Failed_Accuse: 6 notes
   [(3150, 116), (3150, 348), (3150, 337), (131, 35), (3150, 290), (3150, 1033)]
Thinking · Analyzing frequency patterns to replicate success sound tones.
Looking at the frequency analysis, I'm seeing what appears to be two distinct notes in the success sound: a 525 Hz tone and a 788 Hz tone that form a perfect fifth interval—that classic triumphant "da-DAA!" pattern. The data's a bit messy with some frequency splitting, but it seems like there's roughly a 500ms segment for the first note and a 600ms segment for the second, though the exact boundaries are blurry. For the failed sound, that 3150 Hz keeps popping up repeatedly, which feels like an artifact—probably the autocorrelation algorithm locking onto a harmonic of something lower. The single 131 Hz detection is the real clue: the actual buzzer sound is likely a low-frequency buzz around 130-140 Hz, which would have strong harmonics that confuse the pitch detector. I need to adjust my algorithm to prefer lower fundamental frequencies and check for octave errors when the same frequency appears too many times. The passage sound itself might be a glissando—a smooth pitch slide rather than discrete notes—which my current frame-merging approach would flatten into a meaningless average. To see what's actually happening, I should output the raw pitch track over time without merging, and apply the octave-bias fix to get cleaner detections.
Thinking · Diagnosed audio quality issues and planned diagnostic investigation.
I found that one tone is clean but the other two have octave errors and possible pitch slides. Let me dump the raw pitch tracks to see what's actually happening.
bash_toolDump raw pitch tracks with subharmonic bias to reveal true shapes
bash
cd /tmp && python3 << 'EOF'
import wave, numpy as np

def track(path):
    w = wave.open(path)
    sr = w.getframerate()
    data = np.frombuffer(w.readframes(w.getnframes()), dtype=np.int16).astype(float)
    data /= (np.abs(data).max() or 1)
    N, hop = 2048, 256
    out = []
    for i in range(0, len(data) - N, hop):
        seg = data[i:i+N]
        rms = np.sqrt((seg**2).mean())
        if rms < 0.06: out.append((i/sr, 0, rms)); continue
        seg = seg - seg.mean()
        ac = np.correlate(seg, seg, 'full')[N-1:]
        ac /= (ac[0] or 1)
        lo, hi = int(sr/2500), int(sr/80)
        lag = lo + int(np.argmax(ac[lo:hi]))
        # prefer the LOWEST lag whose ac is near the max (true fundamental)
        best = ac[lag]
        for mult in (4, 3, 2):
            cand = lag * mult
            if cand < hi and ac[cand] > 0.85 * best:
                lag = cand; break
        out.append((i/sr, sr/lag, rms))
    return out

for name in ("Sleuth_Passage_Sound", "Sleuth_Success_Accuse", "Sleuth_Failed_Accuse"):
    tr = track(f"/tmp/{name}.wav")
    print(f"===== {name} =====")
    line = []
    for t, f, r in tr[::4]:
        line.append(f"{t:5.2f}s:{int(f):4d}")
        if len(line) == 8: print("  ".join(line)); line = []
    if line: print("  ".join(line))
EOF

Thinking · Cataloging debugging fixes for game mechanics and audio replication.
I'm analyzing the audio frequency contours—there's a rising glissando sweeping from around 100 Hz up to 158 Hz over about 1.1 seconds, then some octave-bias artifacts distort the measurement before it continues climbing toward 200 Hz, completing roughly a full octave rise over 2.2 seconds total. To synthesize this, I'd use a square wave oscillator with an exponential or linear frequency ramp stepping the pitch upward in small increments (around 2-3 Hz per frame, like a simple loop) from 100 to 200 Hz over that 2.2-second window. The WebAudio approach would be a linearRampToValueAtTime call to sweep the frequency smoothly. But now I'm seeing conflicting frequency detections in the success sound—the low-bias correction is picking up alternating 131 Hz and 175 Hz segments, with a longer 196 Hz stretch, which maps to 525 and 788 Hz when scaled up by 4. The subharmonic bias correction might be over-correcting on the square wave's harmonic peaks, since square waves naturally have high energy at odd multiples. The original unbiased run had it right with 525, 525, 788—those are the actual fundamentals. The issue is that autocorrelation on a perfect square wave peaks equally at every harmonic multiple, so my bias toward lower lags with comparable autocorrelation values is backwards. I need to flip the logic: prefer smaller lags when their autocorrelation is close to the best match, since harmonics fold upward. For a more reliable approach, I should switch to FFT-based pitch detection or harmonic product spectrum (HPS), which handles square waves much better than autocorrelation alone. The failed sound is a different pattern entirely—three short bursts around 131 Hz with gaps of 2756 or 1378 frames (likely transients), followed by a much longer, lower buzz at 104 Hz lasting about a second. That's the "razz" pattern: three quick buzzes then one sustained lower tone. The success passage is clearer now: a rising sweep from 100 to 200 Hz over about 2.2 seconds, then three distinct notes. The question is whether those notes are 525, 525, 788 (C5, C5, G5) or the lower estimates 131, 175, 196 (C3, F3, G3). The C-F-G progression is more musical than C-C-G, but the timing data from the low-biased run shows alternating 131/175 values that might indicate a trill or wobble rather than clean separate notes. HPS should resolve which interpretation is correct. I'll run Harmonic Product Spectrum with finer time resolution to pin down the exact note sequence and durations for both the success and failed passages.
Thinking · Analyzed audio waveforms and applied spectral techniques.
I found that the passage uses a rising glissando, but square waves create ambiguous autocorrelation results, so I'll apply harmonic-product-spectrum analysis to identify the true fundamentals.
bash_toolHarmonic product spectrum pass on success and failed sounds
bash
cd /tmp && python3 << 'EOF'
import wave, numpy as np

def hps_track(path, hop=128):
    w = wave.open(path)
    sr = w.getframerate()
    data = np.frombuffer(w.readframes(w.getnframes()), dtype=np.int16).astype(float)
    data /= (np.abs(data).max() or 1)
    N = 2048
    out = []
    for i in range(0, len(data) - N, hop):
        seg = data[i:i+N] * np.hanning(N)
        rms = np.sqrt((seg**2).mean())
        if rms < 0.03: out.append((i/sr, 0)); continue
        spec = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(seg, 4*N))
        hps = spec.copy()
        for h in (2, 3, 4):
            hps[:len(spec)//h] *= spec[::h][:len(spec)//h]
        fmin, fmax = 60, 2500
        binf = sr / (4*N)
        lo, hi = int(fmin/binf), int(fmax/binf)
        f = (lo + np.argmax(hps[lo:hi])) * binf
        out.append((i/sr, f))
    return out

for name, upto in (("Sleuth_Success_Accuse", 99), ("Sleuth_Failed_Accuse", 2.4)):
    tr = hps_track(f"/tmp/{name}.wav")
    print(f"===== {name} (HPS) =====")
    line = []
    for t, f in tr[::3]:
        if t > upto: break
        line.append(f"{t:4.2f}:{int(f):4d}")
        if len(line) == 10: print(" ".join(line)); line = []
    if line: print(" ".join(line))
EOF
Output

0.17: 131 0.19: 131 0.21: 131 0.23: 131 0.24: 131 0.26: 131 0.28: 524 0.30: 527 0.31: 527 0.33: 527

0.52: 131 0.54: 131 0.56: 519 0.57: 524 0.59: 524 0.61: 131 0.63: 129 0.64: 131 0.66:  61 0.68:  59
0.70: 785 0.71: 785 0.73: 785 0.75: 780 0.77: 780 0.78: 783 0.80: 261 0.82: 783 0.84: 783 0.85: 783
0.87: 785 0.89: 783 0.91: 785 0.92: 785 0.94: 788 0.96: 780 0.98: 780 0.99: 783 1.01: 785 1.03: 785
1.04: 785 1.06: 780 1.08: 783 1.10: 783 1.11: 785 1.13: 785 1.15: 785 1.17: 783 1.18: 780 1.20: 785

===== Sleuth_Failed_Accuse (HPS) =====

Thinking · Orchestrating dramatic fanfare with alternating tones and sustained notes.
I'm mapping out the success sound effect with alternating high and low tones creating a dramatic fanfare—starting with rapid alternations between C5 and C3, then finishing with a long sustained G5 note that ties it all together. The gaps are quieter than the tones, so I'm treating them as silence rather than played notes—that gives me the success pattern: three quick C5 bursts (~180ms each with ~90ms gaps) followed by a longer G5 (~550ms), the classic "dun dun dun-DAAA" fanfare. For the failed sound, the 131 Hz segments (~310ms each) drop to a lower 104 Hz for the long note (~1s), creating that sad trombone effect. For the passage, I'm looking at a rising sweep from roughly 100 Hz up to 200 Hz over about 2 seconds—the rate looks fairly consistent across the range, so I'll use an exponential ramp to keep it musically linear. Now I'm setting up the WebAudio synthesizer with a square oscillator at low gain, scheduling the tone sequences with precise timing, and using a frequency ramp for the sweep. I need to handle the case where AudioContext might not be available. Starting the engine mega-patch now, beginning with reworking the stare mechanic in the murder room... Refactoring stare logic to be sticky: suspects in the murder room lock into staring with higher probability and stay that way, while red herrings fade out gradually, with rare stares happening elsewhere. When someone leaves the murder room, their stare persists until the decay kicks in. Adjusting threat staging by increasing the deadline buffer from 70 to 150 turns on pickup, and restructuring the threat progression to use else-if chains so only one message fires per tick. This ensures threat escalates through 1 then 2 before reaching 3, preventing edge cases where threat jumps stages... Now working on the gathering phase: computing U-shaped seating around an anchor point with fallback to any free cells, drawing gathered guests at their assigned seats with door glyphs marking the room boundary, and updating the confession to use full names. Also refactoring the action loop to clear the log before each command and force full cell descriptions after successful moves. For special cases like panel interactions, blind corridor entry, and locked doors, I'm adding freshLog calls before displaying messages to keep the screen clean. Stairs and blocked moves follow their own paths—stairs teleport and redescribe, while wall bumps leave the previous screen intact. I'm also removing the command echo from the message area since the original doesn't show it, which frees up a line for longer outputs like elaborated alibis. Blood-colored text lines use a red attribute when displaying discovery information like bloodstains and weapon traces. Writing audio system... Now I need to verify all the anchor points across the game logic—checking the known text blocks like pickup lines, threat checks, gathering mechanics, confession dialogue, rank display, command execution for movement and echo, the describe-cell flow, pagination in the say function, the paint loop attributes, blood mechanics, locked doors, and the blind-drop sound replacement. I'll grep for the uncertain ones in a single pass to identify what needs patching.
Thinking · Decoded audio cues and prepared implementation for batch corrections.
I've decoded the three sounds — a rising whooop for passage, a fanfare for success, and a sad trombone for failure. Now I'll apply these to the engine for the batch fix.

function doGather() {
  if (world.gathered) {
    say("Everyone is waiting with baited breath to hear your accusation!");
    return;
  }
  const loc = locOf(state.roomId);
  if (!NPC_LOCATIONS.includes(loc)) {
    say("There's no need to observe common courtesies in this house.");
    return;
  }
  for (const s of world.suspects) s.room = loc;
  world.gathered = true;
  world.gatherRoom = loc;
  say("One by one, the houseguests file in. Everyone is waiting with " +
      "baited breath to hear your accusation!  The door is now locked.");

Output
  const code = cellAt(r, c);
  if ((code === 32 || code === 33) &&
      state.lastStairs !== `${r},${c}`) {
    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      [state.row, state.col] = partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;
      const stairRoom = house().rooms[String((code === 32 ? 33 : 32) - 20)];
      if (stairRoom && stairRoom.entry_description)
        say(stairRoom.entry_description);
      tick();
      redraw();
      return;
    }
  }
  if (code <= 20) state.lastStairs = null;
Thinking · Orchestrating stair positioning, sticky behavior, and threat staging mechanics.
I need to fix the stair teleport landing position so it places the partner one row above if that cell is walkable. Then I'm implementing a major patch that covers the sticky model behavior, threat staging with pickup logic and conditional messages, gathering UI elements like seats and door glyphs along with confession data and rank periods, and setting up per-action refresh calls that update the log and describe cells while managing drop echoes and text output.

All anchors in hand. The mega-patch:

bash_toolApply mega-patch: staring, threat, gather-U, refresh, red text, sounds
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - > /tmp/p.log 2>&1 << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:70]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

# ===== A. STICKY STARING: reliable tell, fading herrings =====
rep('''  if (world.turn >= world.stareStart) {
    for (const s of world.suspects) {
      if (rng() < 0.12)
        s.staring = rng() < (s.room === world.murderRoom ? 0.7 : 0.08);
    }
  }''',
'''  // Sticky staring (playtest recalibration): in the murder room a
  // guest soon starts staring and KEEPS staring while they remain;
  // elsewhere, stares are rare red herrings that quickly fade.
  if (world.turn >= world.stareStart) {
    for (const s of world.suspects) {
      if (s.room === world.murderRoom) {
        if (!s.staring && rng() < 0.25) s.staring = true;
      } else if (s.staring) {
        if (rng() < 0.4) s.staring = false;
      } else if (rng() < 0.02) {
        s.staring = true;
      }
    }
  }''')

# ===== B. THREAT STAGING: escalation always sequential =====
rep('''      world.deadline = Math.min(world.deadline, world.turn + 70);''',
'''      world.deadline = Math.min(world.deadline, world.turn + 150);''')
rep('''  if (world.threat < 2 && remaining <= 70) {
    world.threat = 2;
    say("The murderer is now stalking you!");
  }''',
'''  else if (world.threat === 1 && remaining <= 70) {
    world.threat = 2;                      // else-if: never both warnings
    say("The murderer is now stalking you!");   // in the same breath
  }''')

# ===== E. COLOURED say(): red for blood =====
rep('''function say(text) {
  if (text) for (const line of wrap(text, COLS - 2)) {
    if (PAGINATE) pending.push(line);
    else { log.push(line); while (log.length > MSG_ROWS) log.shift(); }
  }
  pump();
}''',
'''function say(text, attr) {
  if (text) for (const line of wrap(text, COLS - 2)) {
    const entry = { t: line, a: attr || 0x07 };
    if (PAGINATE) pending.push(entry);
    else { log.push(entry); while (log.length > MSG_ROWS) log.shift(); }
  }
  pump();
}

// the original clears the message area for every action, which is
// how it never overruns: each move or command starts a fresh page
function freshLog() { log.length = 0; freshLines = 0; }''')

rep('''  for (let i = 0; i < MSG_ROWS; i++) {
    clearRow(MSG_TOP + i, 0x07);
    if (log[i]) print(MSG_TOP + i, 1, log[i], 0x07);
  }''',
'''  for (let i = 0; i < MSG_ROWS; i++) {
    clearRow(MSG_TOP + i, 0x07);
    if (log[i]) print(MSG_TOP + i, 1, log[i].t, log[i].a);
  }''')

rep('''      say("There seem to be bloodstains on the floor!  This must be the " +
          "scene of the murder!");''',
'''      say("There seem to be bloodstains on the floor!  This must be the " +
          "scene of the murder!", 0x0c);          // in blood red''')
rep('''      say("There are traces of blood on it!  This must be the murder weapon!");''',
'''      say("There are traces of blood on it!  This must be the murder weapon!",
          0x0c);                                   // in blood red''')

# ===== D. PER-ACTION REFRESH + always redescribe on movement =====
rep('''  say("> " + raw.trim());
''', '''  freshLog();                            // each command starts clean
''')
rep('''  const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  tick();
  if (checkAmbush(prevLoc)) return;
  describeCell(false);''',
'''  const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  freshLog();                            // every step repaints its story
  tick();
  if (checkAmbush(prevLoc)) return;
  describeCell(true);''')
rep('''    const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
    [state.row, state.col] = pick(cells);
    state.roomId = null;                 // force a fresh room announce
    tick();''',
'''    const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
    [state.row, state.col] = pick(cells);
    state.roomId = null;                 // force a fresh room announce
    freshLog();
    tick();''')
rep('''    if (v !== 0 && dest !== world.gatherRoom) {
      say("The door is now locked.");''',
'''    if (v !== 0 && dest !== world.gatherRoom) {
      freshLog();
      say("The door is now locked.");''')

# ===== G. STAIRS: arrive one step above the landing, not in the wall =====
rep('''    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      [state.row, state.col] = partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;''',
'''    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      // arrive one step UP the far flight: the landing cell sits in
      // the wall row, and the original never draws you inside a wall
      const above = [partner[0] - 1, partner[1]];
      [state.row, state.col] =
        cellAt(above[0], above[1]) > 0 ? above : partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;
      freshLog();''')

# ===== C+F. GATHER U-FORMATION + drawn door; guests on the right char =====
rep('''  for (const s of world.suspects) s.room = loc;
  world.gathered = true;
  world.gatherRoom = loc;''',
'''  for (const s of world.suspects) s.room = loc;
  world.gathered = true;
  world.gatherRoom = loc;
  // seat the assembly in a U around the room's anchor, opening toward
  // the detective (as the original arranges them)
  const base = house().anchors[String(loc)] || middleCell(loc);
  const U = [[1,-3],[1,-1],[1,1],[1,3],[0,-3],[0,3],[1,-2],[1,2],[0,-2],[0,2]];
  const g2 = house().grid, taken = new Set();
  world.gatherSeats = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    let seat = null;
    for (const [dr, dc] of U) {
      const rr = base[0] + dr, cc = base[1] + dc, k = rr + "," + cc;
      if (g2[rr] && g2[rr][cc] === loc && !taken.has(k)) { seat = [rr, cc]; break; }
    }
    if (!seat) {                            // odd-shaped room: any cell
      outer: for (let rr = 0; rr < g2.length; rr++)
        for (let cc = 0; cc < g2[0].length; cc++)
          if (g2[rr][cc] === loc && !taken.has(rr + "," + cc)) { seat = [rr, cc]; break outer; }
    }
    if (seat) { taken.add(seat.join()); world.gatherSeats[s.key] = seat; }
  }
  freshLog();''')

rep('''  const seatTaken = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room !== locOf(state.roomId)) continue;
    const pos = house().anchors[String(s.room)] || middleCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    let [ar, ac] = pos;
    while (seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] && cellAt(ar, ac + 1) > 0) ac += 1;
    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;                // assembled crowd
    if (ar <= 16)
      put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, Math.max(0, ac * 2 - 1), "\\u263b",
          bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }''',
'''  // guests draw on the RIGHT character of the cell (the detective
  // takes the left), so both can share a square without overlap —
  // exactly as the original composes them
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room !== locOf(state.roomId)) continue;
    const pos = (world.gathered && world.gatherSeats[s.key]) ||
                house().anchors[String(s.room)] || middleCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    const [ar, ac] = pos;
    if (ar <= 16)
      put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, ac * 2, "\\u263b", bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }
  // the locked door, drawn shut while the assembly waits
  if (world.gathered) {
    const g3 = house().grid, dv = world.gatherRoom + 20;
    for (let rr = 0; rr < g3.length; rr++)
      for (let cc = 0; cc < g3[0].length; cc++)
        if (g3[rr][cc] === dv && rr <= 16)
          put(rr + SPRITE_TOP, Math.max(0, cc * 2 - 1), "\\u2502", 0x0f);
  }''')

# endgame text to match the original exactly
rep('''  say(`${m.nick} ${fillTokens(pick(DIALOGUE.confessions), m)}`);''',
'''  playTune(SOUNDS.win);
  say(`${fullName(m)} ${fillTokens(pick(DIALOGUE.confessions), m)}`);''')
rep('''  say(`The case is closed after ${world.turn} moves.  You now rank ` +
      `as ${rank.startsWith("an ") ? rank : "a " + rank} Sleuth!`);''',
'''  say(`The case is closed after ${world.turn} moves.  You now rank ` +
      `as ${rank.startsWith("an ") ? rank : "a " + rank} Sleuth.`);''')

# ===== H. THE RECOVERED PC-SPEAKER SOUNDS =====
rep('''function beep() {''',
'''/* The three sounds, reverse-engineered from recordings of the
   original via pitch-tracking: the passage exit is a rising octave
   glissando (100->200 Hz over ~2.1s); a correct accusation is a C-C-C
   -G fanfare; a wrong one is three low buzzes sagging to a long
   deeper one. Square waves, as the PC speaker intended. */
const SOUNDS = {
  passage: { sweep: [100, 200], ms: 2100 },
  win:  [[523, 150], [0, 110], [523, 150], [0, 110], [523, 80], [0, 90],
         [784, 600]],
  fail: [[131, 310], [0, 90], [131, 310], [0, 90], [131, 310], [0, 90],
         [104, 1000]],
};

function playTune(seq) {
  try {
    const ctx = new (window.AudioContext || window.webkitAudioContext)();
    const gain = ctx.createGain();
    gain.gain.value = 0.07;
    gain.connect(ctx.destination);
    let t = ctx.currentTime;
    if (seq.sweep) {
      const o = ctx.createOscillator();
      o.type = "square";
      o.frequency.setValueAtTime(seq.sweep[0], t);
      o.frequency.linearRampToValueAtTime(seq.sweep[1], t + seq.ms / 1000);
      o.connect(gain); o.start(t); o.stop(t + seq.ms / 1000);
      return;
    }
    for (const [f, ms] of seq) {
      if (f > 0) {
        const o = ctx.createOscillator();
        o.type = "square";
        o.frequency.value = f;
        o.connect(gain); o.start(t); o.stop(t + ms / 1000);
      }
      t += ms / 1000;
    }
  } catch (_) { /* no audio device: 1983 in silence */ }
}

function beep() {''')

rep('''    beep();''', '''    playTune(SOUNDS.passage);''')
rep('''  if (accused.key !== m.key) {               // an innocent, wrongly named
    say(`${accused.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!"`);''',
'''  if (accused.key !== m.key) {               // an innocent, wrongly named
    playTune(SOUNDS.fail);
    say(`${accused.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!"`);''')
rep('''  if (here !== world.murderRoom) {           // right villain, wrong room
    say(`${m.nick} stands up and exclaims, "You fool!!  Yes, I used ` +''',
'''  if (here !== world.murderRoom) {           // right villain, wrong room
    playTune(SOUNDS.fail);
    say(`${m.nick} stands up and exclaims, "You fool!!  Yes, I used ` +''')
rep('''  if (!world.carrying) {                     // right room, empty hands
    say(`${m.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!  ` +''',
'''  if (!world.carrying) {                     // right room, empty hands
    playTune(SOUNDS.fail);
    say(`${m.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!  ` +''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("PATCH_COMPLETE")
PATCH_EOF
cat /tmp/p.log; grep -q PATCH_COMPLETE /tmp/p.log && node --check web/engine.js && python3 gen_web_data.py extracted web sleuth > /dev/null && echo "BUILD OK"
Output
PATCH_COMPLETE
BUILD OK
2026-07-05 02:00
Claude
Thinking · Thought process
I'm working through a batch of bugs, starting with personalized game issues where the staring mechanic is misfiring—suspicion levels are jumping straight from normal to stalking without the intermediate "grown suspicious" state, and turns are exceeding 500. I suspect there's an interaction between the random number generator and the RESEED logic when the game starts through the personalization menu. The staring initialization sets a value around 40-70 frames, and then each tick checks a probability threshold to trigger the staring state, but something in that flow is breaking down. Now I'm reconsidering whether the murder room occupancy is actually the culprit—with a custom cast, guests should still be distributed across locations at roughly the same rate as the original cast, so the staring probability shouldn't differ much. The real issue might be in the suspicion escalation logic itself. I'm noticing that the threat level checks happen sequentially each tick: threat 1 fires when remaining time hits 180, then threat 2 fires when remaining hits 70. Both conditions should never trigger simultaneously unless the deadline gets shortened dramatically in a single jump—like when a weapon is picked up, which sets the deadline to turn+70, potentially causing both threat checks to fire on the same tick. If that happens, both the "suspicious" and "stalking" messages would print, but they might be getting lost in the message queue or scrolling off-screen before the user notices them. The fix would be to soften the deadline reduction on weapon pickup—maybe setting it to turn+150 instead of turn+70—so the suspicion escalation happens in proper stages rather than all at once. But I'm also realizing the staring mechanic might genuinely be the bottleneck: for a guest to stare, they need to be in the murder room AND the stare timer needs to trigger, which happens at roughly 0.084 probability per tick. The player has to actually be in that same room to witness it, and if the guest wanders out before the player arrives, the stare never happens. The drift test passed when forced, so the stare-drift loop itself works. My real suspicion now is whether the murder room is even being set correctly with the custom cast, or if there's something about how guests move that's preventing them from reaching it. Let me run an empirical simulation over 600 turns to measure how often the murder-room occupant is actually staring and how frequently a touring player witnesses staring anywhere. If the numbers look reasonable, the issue is just feel—I'd bump the murder-room stare probability from 0.7 to 0.85, increase the re-roll rate from 0.12 to 0.2, lower red-herring staring to 0.05, and make staring sticky: once a guest starts staring in the murder room, they stay staring while they're there, but elsewhere stares are brief and fleeting. Implementing the stare logic... For the threat escalation, I'm using an else-if chain so only one message fires per tick—first checking if threat is below 1 and remaining drops to 180 or less, then checking if threat is below 2 and remaining hits 70 or less. This keeps the progression sequential and prevents duplicate messages on the same turn. Now for the endgame gathering: I need to arrange suspects in a U-shape around the player, with guests positioned at offsets from a center anchor point in the room—something like placing two at the top corners and four spread across the bottom to form that opening-upward U formation. The endgame message needs a period instead of an exclamation mark, and the confession should use the suspect's full name with the original framing "turns toward you, smiling triumphantly." rather than the current nickname + grinning template. I need to switch to using fullName(m) for the confession line and ensure the rank message ends with a period. Also noticing that room descriptions seem to refresh on each move within a room based on the screenshots. The original clears the message area on each action rather than scrolling, which prevents text overflow — so I should clear the log at the start of each player action (tryMove and execute), then always redescribe the current room after a successful move. This means calling describeCell with a force flag to reprint the full description including occupants and scene details, then the arrival announcements integrate naturally. For execute(), I'd clear the log first, echo the command, then show the response. I also need to handle blood text in red by adding an optional attribute parameter to the say() function, storing both text and color in log entries. Guest positioning needs fixing too — guests should draw at the right character of a cell (col*2) while the player occupies the left (col*2-1), so they don't overlap when sharing a space. And there's an issue with stair teleportation landing the detective in the wall instead of one unit up from the landing cell. For the three MP3 files, I'm thinking about analyzing them to extract the beep sequences — if ffmpeg is available in the sandbox, I can convert them to WAV and use zero-crossing or autocorrelation to estimate pitch per frame, then segment into individual notes with their frequencies and durations. That would let me recreate the original PC speaker sounds as WebAudio square wave sequences, embedding them as tuples of (frequency, milliseconds) and playing them through an extended beep function. The work order is clear: start with audio analysis to get the sound data, then tackle the engine mega-patch covering stare rework, threat staging, gathering mechanics, logging, red attribute lines, guest drawing, stair teleport fix, and sound playback. Tests need to verify stare reliability, threat sequencing, gather mechanics, description refresh, red text on discovery, guest positioning, stair landing placement, and that the sound data exists and plays (with guards for headless environments). Let me check if ffmpeg and numpy are available first.

A rich debugging haul — and you've handed me actual recordings of the 1983 PC speaker, which means I can reverse-engineer the beep sequences into data. Let me start there (checking what audio tooling the sandbox has), then diagnose the staring/suspicion bugs:

bash_tool
bash
which ffmpeg ffprobe; python3 -c "import numpy; print('numpy', numpy.__version__)" 2>&1; ls -la /mnt/user-data/uploads/*.mp3
Thinking · Thought process
I've got ffmpeg and numpy available, so I can convert each MP3 to mono WAV at 22050 Hz, then run pitch detection on 512-sample frames with autocorrelation to identify note segments with their frequencies and durations. Since PC speaker output is monophonic square waves, autocorrelation should work well for this. I'm setting up the analyzer to read the audio, apply RMS gating per frame, and extract pitch information. Finishing the pitch detection...
Thinking · Thought process
I have the full toolkit available and I'm now extracting the beep sequences using autocorrelation pitch-tracking.
bash_tool
bash
cd /tmp && for f in Sleuth_Success_Accuse Sleuth_Failed_Accuse Sleuth_Passage_Sound; do ffmpeg -y -loglevel error -i /mnt/user-data/uploads/$f.mp3 -ac 1 -ar 22050 $f.wav; done && python3 << 'EOF'
import wave, numpy as np

def notes(path):
    w = wave.open(path)
    sr = w.getframerate()
    data = np.frombuffer(w.readframes(w.getnframes()), dtype=np.int16).astype(float)
    data /= (np.abs(data).max() or 1)
    N, hop = 1024, 256
    gate = 0.08
    frames = []
    for i in range(0, len(data) - N, hop):
        seg = data[i:i+N]
        rms = np.sqrt((seg**2).mean())
        if rms < gate:
            frames.append((i/sr, 0)); continue
        seg = seg - seg.mean()
        ac = np.correlate(seg, seg, 'full')[N-1:]
        ac /= (ac[0] or 1)
        lo, hi = int(sr/3000), int(sr/100)
        lag = lo + np.argmax(ac[lo:hi])
        # refine: reject subharmonic confusion by checking half-lag
        if lag//2 >= lo and ac[lag//2] > 0.9 * ac[lag]:
            lag = lag//2
        frames.append((i/sr, sr/lag))
    # merge into notes
    out, cur = [], None
    for t, f in frames:
        if f == 0:
            if cur: out.append(cur); cur = None
            continue
        if cur and abs(f - cur[1]) / cur[1] < 0.06:
            cur = (cur[0], (cur[1]+f)/2, t)
        else:
            if cur: out.append(cur)
            cur = (t, f, t)
    if cur: out.append(cur)
    hopdur = hop/sr
    return [(round(f), round((e - s + hopdur)*1000)) for s, f, e in out if (e-s) > 0.015]

for name in ("Sleuth_Passage_Sound", "Sleuth_Success_Accuse", "Sleuth_Failed_Accuse"):
    ns = notes(f"/tmp/{name}.wav")
    print(f"{name}: {len(ns)} notes")
    print("  ", ns[:24])
EOF
Thinking · Thought process
Looking at the frequency analysis, I'm seeing what appears to be two distinct notes in the success sound: a 525 Hz tone and a 788 Hz tone that form a perfect fifth interval—that classic triumphant "da-DAA!" pattern. The data's a bit messy with some frequency splitting, but it seems like there's roughly a 500ms segment for the first note and a 600ms segment for the second, though the exact boundaries are blurry. For the failed sound, that 3150 Hz keeps popping up repeatedly, which feels like an artifact—probably the autocorrelation algorithm locking onto a harmonic of something lower. The single 131 Hz detection is the real clue: the actual buzzer sound is likely a low-frequency buzz around 130-140 Hz, which would have strong harmonics that confuse the pitch detector. I need to adjust my algorithm to prefer lower fundamental frequencies and check for octave errors when the same frequency appears too many times. The passage sound itself might be a glissando—a smooth pitch slide rather than discrete notes—which my current frame-merging approach would flatten into a meaningless average. To see what's actually happening, I should output the raw pitch track over time without merging, and apply the octave-bias fix to get cleaner detections.
Thinking · Thought process
I found that one tone is clean but the other two have octave errors and possible pitch slides. Let me dump the raw pitch tracks to see what's actually happening.
bash_tool
bash
cd /tmp && python3 << 'EOF'
import wave, numpy as np

def track(path):
    w = wave.open(path)
    sr = w.getframerate()
    data = np.frombuffer(w.readframes(w.getnframes()), dtype=np.int16).astype(float)
    data /= (np.abs(data).max() or 1)
    N, hop = 2048, 256
    out = []
    for i in range(0, len(data) - N, hop):
        seg = data[i:i+N]
        rms = np.sqrt((seg**2).mean())
        if rms < 0.06: out.append((i/sr, 0, rms)); continue
        seg = seg - seg.mean()
        ac = np.correlate(seg, seg, 'full')[N-1:]
        ac /= (ac[0] or 1)
        lo, hi = int(sr/2500), int(sr/80)
        lag = lo + int(np.argmax(ac[lo:hi]))
        # prefer the LOWEST lag whose ac is near the max (true fundamental)
        best = ac[lag]
        for mult in (4, 3, 2):
            cand = lag * mult
            if cand < hi and ac[cand] > 0.85 * best:
                lag = cand; break
        out.append((i/sr, sr/lag, rms))
    return out

for name in ("Sleuth_Passage_Sound", "Sleuth_Success_Accuse", "Sleuth_Failed_Accuse"):
    tr = track(f"/tmp/{name}.wav")
    print(f"===== {name} =====")
    line = []
    for t, f, r in tr[::4]:
        line.append(f"{t:5.2f}s:{int(f):4d}")
        if len(line) == 8: print("  ".join(line)); line = []
    if line: print("  ".join(line))
EOF
Thinking · Thought process
I'm analyzing the audio frequency contours—there's a rising glissando sweeping from around 100 Hz up to 158 Hz over about 1.1 seconds, then some octave-bias artifacts distort the measurement before it continues climbing toward 200 Hz, completing roughly a full octave rise over 2.2 seconds total. To synthesize this, I'd use a square wave oscillator with an exponential or linear frequency ramp stepping the pitch upward in small increments (around 2-3 Hz per frame, like a simple loop) from 100 to 200 Hz over that 2.2-second window. The WebAudio approach would be a linearRampToValueAtTime call to sweep the frequency smoothly. But now I'm seeing conflicting frequency detections in the success sound—the low-bias correction is picking up alternating 131 Hz and 175 Hz segments, with a longer 196 Hz stretch, which maps to 525 and 788 Hz when scaled up by 4. The subharmonic bias correction might be over-correcting on the square wave's harmonic peaks, since square waves naturally have high energy at odd multiples. The original unbiased run had it right with 525, 525, 788—those are the actual fundamentals. The issue is that autocorrelation on a perfect square wave peaks equally at every harmonic multiple, so my bias toward lower lags with comparable autocorrelation values is backwards. I need to flip the logic: prefer smaller lags when their autocorrelation is close to the best match, since harmonics fold upward. For a more reliable approach, I should switch to FFT-based pitch detection or harmonic product spectrum (HPS), which handles square waves much better than autocorrelation alone. The failed sound is a different pattern entirely—three short bursts around 131 Hz with gaps of 2756 or 1378 frames (likely transients), followed by a much longer, lower buzz at 104 Hz lasting about a second. That's the "razz" pattern: three quick buzzes then one sustained lower tone. The success passage is clearer now: a rising sweep from 100 to 200 Hz over about 2.2 seconds, then three distinct notes. The question is whether those notes are 525, 525, 788 (C5, C5, G5) or the lower estimates 131, 175, 196 (C3, F3, G3). The C-F-G progression is more musical than C-C-G, but the timing data from the low-biased run shows alternating 131/175 values that might indicate a trill or wobble rather than clean separate notes. HPS should resolve which interpretation is correct. I'll run Harmonic Product Spectrum with finer time resolution to pin down the exact note sequence and durations for both the success and failed passages.
Thinking · Thought process
I found that the passage uses a rising glissando, but square waves create ambiguous autocorrelation results, so I'll apply harmonic-product-spectrum analysis to identify the true fundamentals.
bash_tool
bash
cd /tmp && python3 << 'EOF'
import wave, numpy as np

def hps_track(path, hop=128):
    w = wave.open(path)
    sr = w.getframerate()
    data = np.frombuffer(w.readframes(w.getnframes()), dtype=np.int16).astype(float)
    data /= (np.abs(data).max() or 1)
    N = 2048
    out = []
    for i in range(0, len(data) - N, hop):
        seg = data[i:i+N] * np.hanning(N)
        rms = np.sqrt((seg**2).mean())
        if rms < 0.03: out.append((i/sr, 0)); continue
        spec = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(seg, 4*N))
        hps = spec.copy()
        for h in (2, 3, 4):
            hps[:len(spec)//h] *= spec[::h][:len(spec)//h]
        fmin, fmax = 60, 2500
        binf = sr / (4*N)
        lo, hi = int(fmin/binf), int(fmax/binf)
        f = (lo + np.argmax(hps[lo:hi])) * binf
        out.append((i/sr, f))
    return out

for name, upto in (("Sleuth_Success_Accuse", 99), ("Sleuth_Failed_Accuse", 2.4)):
    tr = hps_track(f"/tmp/{name}.wav")
    print(f"===== {name} (HPS) =====")
    line = []
    for t, f in tr[::3]:
        if t > upto: break
        line.append(f"{t:4.2f}:{int(f):4d}")
        if len(line) == 10: print(" ".join(line)); line = []
    if line: print(" ".join(line))
EOF
Thinking · Thought process
I'm mapping out the success sound effect with alternating high and low tones creating a dramatic fanfare—starting with rapid alternations between C5 and C3, then finishing with a long sustained G5 note that ties it all together. The gaps are quieter than the tones, so I'm treating them as silence rather than played notes—that gives me the success pattern: three quick C5 bursts (~180ms each with ~90ms gaps) followed by a longer G5 (~550ms), the classic "dun dun dun-DAAA" fanfare. For the failed sound, the 131 Hz segments (~310ms each) drop to a lower 104 Hz for the long note (~1s), creating that sad trombone effect. For the passage, I'm looking at a rising sweep from roughly 100 Hz up to 200 Hz over about 2 seconds—the rate looks fairly consistent across the range, so I'll use an exponential ramp to keep it musically linear. Now I'm setting up the WebAudio synthesizer with a square oscillator at low gain, scheduling the tone sequences with precise timing, and using a frequency ramp for the sweep. I need to handle the case where AudioContext might not be available. Starting the engine mega-patch now, beginning with reworking the stare mechanic in the murder room... Refactoring stare logic to be sticky: suspects in the murder room lock into staring with higher probability and stay that way, while red herrings fade out gradually, with rare stares happening elsewhere. When someone leaves the murder room, their stare persists until the decay kicks in. Adjusting threat staging by increasing the deadline buffer from 70 to 150 turns on pickup, and restructuring the threat progression to use else-if chains so only one message fires per tick. This ensures threat escalates through 1 then 2 before reaching 3, preventing edge cases where threat jumps stages... Now working on the gathering phase: computing U-shaped seating around an anchor point with fallback to any free cells, drawing gathered guests at their assigned seats with door glyphs marking the room boundary, and updating the confession to use full names. Also refactoring the action loop to clear the log before each command and force full cell descriptions after successful moves. For special cases like panel interactions, blind corridor entry, and locked doors, I'm adding freshLog calls before displaying messages to keep the screen clean. Stairs and blocked moves follow their own paths—stairs teleport and redescribe, while wall bumps leave the previous screen intact. I'm also removing the command echo from the message area since the original doesn't show it, which frees up a line for longer outputs like elaborated alibis. Blood-colored text lines use a red attribute when displaying discovery information like bloodstains and weapon traces. Writing audio system... Now I need to verify all the anchor points across the game logic—checking the known text blocks like pickup lines, threat checks, gathering mechanics, confession dialogue, rank display, command execution for movement and echo, the describe-cell flow, pagination in the say function, the paint loop attributes, blood mechanics, locked doors, and the blind-drop sound replacement. I'll grep for the uncertain ones in a single pass to identify what needs patching.
Thinking · Thought process
I've decoded the three sounds — a rising whooop for passage, a fanfare for success, and a sad trombone for failure. Now I'll apply these to the engine for the batch fix.

Thinking · Thought process
I need to fix the stair teleport landing position so it places the partner one row above if that cell is walkable. Then I'm implementing a major patch that covers the sticky model behavior, threat staging with pickup logic and conditional messages, gathering UI elements like seats and door glyphs along with confession data and rank periods, and setting up per-action refresh calls that update the log and describe cells while managing drop echoes and text output.

All anchors in hand. The mega-patch:

bash_tool
bash
cd /home/claude && python3 - > /tmp/p.log 2>&1 << 'PATCH_EOF'
src = open('web/engine.js').read()
def rep(old, new):
    global src
    assert old in src, "NOT FOUND: " + old[:70]
    src = src.replace(old, new)

# ===== A. STICKY STARING: reliable tell, fading herrings =====
rep('''  if (world.turn >= world.stareStart) {
    for (const s of world.suspects) {
      if (rng() < 0.12)
        s.staring = rng() < (s.room === world.murderRoom ? 0.7 : 0.08);
    }
  }''',
'''  // Sticky staring (playtest recalibration): in the murder room a
  // guest soon starts staring and KEEPS staring while they remain;
  // elsewhere, stares are rare red herrings that quickly fade.
  if (world.turn >= world.stareStart) {
    for (const s of world.suspects) {
      if (s.room === world.murderRoom) {
        if (!s.staring && rng() < 0.25) s.staring = true;
      } else if (s.staring) {
        if (rng() < 0.4) s.staring = false;
      } else if (rng() < 0.02) {
        s.staring = true;
      }
    }
  }''')

# ===== B. THREAT STAGING: escalation always sequential =====
rep('''      world.deadline = Math.min(world.deadline, world.turn + 70);''',
'''      world.deadline = Math.min(world.deadline, world.turn + 150);''')
rep('''  if (world.threat < 2 && remaining <= 70) {
    world.threat = 2;
    say("The murderer is now stalking you!");
  }''',
'''  else if (world.threat === 1 && remaining <= 70) {
    world.threat = 2;                      // else-if: never both warnings
    say("The murderer is now stalking you!");   // in the same breath
  }''')

# ===== E. COLOURED say(): red for blood =====
rep('''function say(text) {
  if (text) for (const line of wrap(text, COLS - 2)) {
    if (PAGINATE) pending.push(line);
    else { log.push(line); while (log.length > MSG_ROWS) log.shift(); }
  }
  pump();
}''',
'''function say(text, attr) {
  if (text) for (const line of wrap(text, COLS - 2)) {
    const entry = { t: line, a: attr || 0x07 };
    if (PAGINATE) pending.push(entry);
    else { log.push(entry); while (log.length > MSG_ROWS) log.shift(); }
  }
  pump();
}

// the original clears the message area for every action, which is
// how it never overruns: each move or command starts a fresh page
function freshLog() { log.length = 0; freshLines = 0; }''')

rep('''  for (let i = 0; i < MSG_ROWS; i++) {
    clearRow(MSG_TOP + i, 0x07);
    if (log[i]) print(MSG_TOP + i, 1, log[i], 0x07);
  }''',
'''  for (let i = 0; i < MSG_ROWS; i++) {
    clearRow(MSG_TOP + i, 0x07);
    if (log[i]) print(MSG_TOP + i, 1, log[i].t, log[i].a);
  }''')

rep('''      say("There seem to be bloodstains on the floor!  This must be the " +
          "scene of the murder!");''',
'''      say("There seem to be bloodstains on the floor!  This must be the " +
          "scene of the murder!", 0x0c);          // in blood red''')
rep('''      say("There are traces of blood on it!  This must be the murder weapon!");''',
'''      say("There are traces of blood on it!  This must be the murder weapon!",
          0x0c);                                   // in blood red''')

# ===== D. PER-ACTION REFRESH + always redescribe on movement =====
rep('''  say("> " + raw.trim());
''', '''  freshLog();                            // each command starts clean
''')
rep('''  const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  tick();
  if (checkAmbush(prevLoc)) return;
  describeCell(false);''',
'''  const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
  freshLog();                            // every step repaints its story
  tick();
  if (checkAmbush(prevLoc)) return;
  describeCell(true);''')
rep('''    const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
    [state.row, state.col] = pick(cells);
    state.roomId = null;                 // force a fresh room announce
    tick();''',
'''    const prevLoc = locOf(state.roomId);
    [state.row, state.col] = pick(cells);
    state.roomId = null;                 // force a fresh room announce
    freshLog();
    tick();''')
rep('''    if (v !== 0 && dest !== world.gatherRoom) {
      say("The door is now locked.");''',
'''    if (v !== 0 && dest !== world.gatherRoom) {
      freshLog();
      say("The door is now locked.");''')

# ===== G. STAIRS: arrive one step above the landing, not in the wall =====
rep('''    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      [state.row, state.col] = partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;''',
'''    const partner = findCell(code === 32 ? 33 : 32);
    if (partner) {
      // arrive one step UP the far flight: the landing cell sits in
      // the wall row, and the original never draws you inside a wall
      const above = [partner[0] - 1, partner[1]];
      [state.row, state.col] =
        cellAt(above[0], above[1]) > 0 ? above : partner;
      state.lastStairs = `${partner[0]},${partner[1]}`;
      freshLog();''')

# ===== C+F. GATHER U-FORMATION + drawn door; guests on the right char =====
rep('''  for (const s of world.suspects) s.room = loc;
  world.gathered = true;
  world.gatherRoom = loc;''',
'''  for (const s of world.suspects) s.room = loc;
  world.gathered = true;
  world.gatherRoom = loc;
  // seat the assembly in a U around the room's anchor, opening toward
  // the detective (as the original arranges them)
  const base = house().anchors[String(loc)] || middleCell(loc);
  const U = [[1,-3],[1,-1],[1,1],[1,3],[0,-3],[0,3],[1,-2],[1,2],[0,-2],[0,2]];
  const g2 = house().grid, taken = new Set();
  world.gatherSeats = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    let seat = null;
    for (const [dr, dc] of U) {
      const rr = base[0] + dr, cc = base[1] + dc, k = rr + "," + cc;
      if (g2[rr] && g2[rr][cc] === loc && !taken.has(k)) { seat = [rr, cc]; break; }
    }
    if (!seat) {                            // odd-shaped room: any cell
      outer: for (let rr = 0; rr < g2.length; rr++)
        for (let cc = 0; cc < g2[0].length; cc++)
          if (g2[rr][cc] === loc && !taken.has(rr + "," + cc)) { seat = [rr, cc]; break outer; }
    }
    if (seat) { taken.add(seat.join()); world.gatherSeats[s.key] = seat; }
  }
  freshLog();''')

rep('''  const seatTaken = {};
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room !== locOf(state.roomId)) continue;
    const pos = house().anchors[String(s.room)] || middleCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    let [ar, ac] = pos;
    while (seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] && cellAt(ar, ac + 1) > 0) ac += 1;
    seatTaken[`${ar},${ac}`] = true;                // assembled crowd
    if (ar <= 16)
      put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, Math.max(0, ac * 2 - 1), "\\u263b",
          bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }''',
'''  // guests draw on the RIGHT character of the cell (the detective
  // takes the left), so both can share a square without overlap —
  // exactly as the original composes them
  for (const s of world.suspects) {
    if (s.room !== locOf(state.roomId)) continue;
    const pos = (world.gathered && world.gatherSeats[s.key]) ||
                house().anchors[String(s.room)] || middleCell(s.room);
    if (!pos) continue;
    const [ar, ac] = pos;
    if (ar <= 16)
      put(ar + SPRITE_TOP, ac * 2, "\\u263b", bgAt(ar, ac) | 0x0b);
  }
  // the locked door, drawn shut while the assembly waits
  if (world.gathered) {
    const g3 = house().grid, dv = world.gatherRoom + 20;
    for (let rr = 0; rr < g3.length; rr++)
      for (let cc = 0; cc < g3[0].length; cc++)
        if (g3[rr][cc] === dv && rr <= 16)
          put(rr + SPRITE_TOP, Math.max(0, cc * 2 - 1), "\\u2502", 0x0f);
  }''')

# endgame text to match the original exactly
rep('''  say(`${m.nick} ${fillTokens(pick(DIALOGUE.confessions), m)}`);''',
'''  playTune(SOUNDS.win);
  say(`${fullName(m)} ${fillTokens(pick(DIALOGUE.confessions), m)}`);''')
rep('''  say(`The case is closed after ${world.turn} moves.  You now rank ` +
      `as ${rank.startsWith("an ") ? rank : "a " + rank} Sleuth!`);''',
'''  say(`The case is closed after ${world.turn} moves.  You now rank ` +
      `as ${rank.startsWith("an ") ? rank : "a " + rank} Sleuth.`);''')

# ===== H. THE RECOVERED PC-SPEAKER SOUNDS =====
rep('''function beep() {''',
'''/* The three sounds, reverse-engineered from recordings of the
   original via pitch-tracking: the passage exit is a rising octave
   glissando (100->200 Hz over ~2.1s); a correct accusation is a C-C-C
   -G fanfare; a wrong one is three low buzzes sagging to a long
   deeper one. Square waves, as the PC speaker intended. */
const SOUNDS = {
  passage: { sweep: [100, 200], ms: 2100 },
  win:  [[523, 150], [0, 110], [523, 150], [0, 110], [523, 80], [0, 90],
         [784, 600]],
  fail: [[131, 310], [0, 90], [131, 310], [0, 90], [131, 310], [0, 90],
         [104, 1000]],
};

function playTune(seq) {
  try {
    const ctx = new (window.AudioContext || window.webkitAudioContext)();
    const gain = ctx.createGain();
    gain.gain.value = 0.07;
    gain.connect(ctx.destination);
    let t = ctx.currentTime;
    if (seq.sweep) {
      const o = ctx.createOscillator();
      o.type = "square";
      o.frequency.setValueAtTime(seq.sweep[0], t);
      o.frequency.linearRampToValueAtTime(seq.sweep[1], t + seq.ms / 1000);
      o.connect(gain); o.start(t); o.stop(t + seq.ms / 1000);
      return;
    }
    for (const [f, ms] of seq) {
      if (f > 0) {
        const o = ctx.createOscillator();
        o.type = "square";
        o.frequency.value = f;
        o.connect(gain); o.start(t); o.stop(t + ms / 1000);
      }
      t += ms / 1000;
    }
  } catch (_) { /* no audio device: 1983 in silence */ }
}

function beep() {''')

rep('''    beep();''', '''    playTune(SOUNDS.passage);''')
rep('''  if (accused.key !== m.key) {               // an innocent, wrongly named
    say(`${accused.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!"`);''',
'''  if (accused.key !== m.key) {               // an innocent, wrongly named
    playTune(SOUNDS.fail);
    say(`${accused.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!"`);''')
rep('''  if (here !== world.murderRoom) {           // right villain, wrong room
    say(`${m.nick} stands up and exclaims, "You fool!!  Yes, I used ` +''',
'''  if (here !== world.murderRoom) {           // right villain, wrong room
    playTune(SOUNDS.fail);
    say(`${m.nick} stands up and exclaims, "You fool!!  Yes, I used ` +''')
rep('''  if (!world.carrying) {                     // right room, empty hands
    say(`${m.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!  ` +''',
'''  if (!world.carrying) {                     // right room, empty hands
    playTune(SOUNDS.fail);
    say(`${m.nick} rises and declares angrily, "You're wrong!!  ` +''')

open('web/engine.js','w').write(src)
print("PATCH_COMPLETE")
